recipes.name: Couscous Royal
recipes.description: Algeria's grandest couscous dish featuring fluffy steamed semolina topped with lamb, merguez sausage, and a fragrant vegetable broth loaded with chickpeas, turnips, and courgettes.
recipes.story: Couscous is inscribed on UNESCO's Intangible Cultural Heritage list, shared among the Maghreb nations. In Algeria, Friday couscous is a sacred family tradition. The Royal version brings together multiple meats and a rich vegetable broth, reserved for celebrations, weddings, and honoured guests. Steaming the couscous in a couscoussier above the simmering broth infuses every grain with flavour.
recipes.occasion: Friday lunch, weddings, celebrations
recipes.best_served_with: Buttermilk (lben) and harissa on the side
recipes.image_alt: Couscous Royal recipe from Algeria on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Start the broth
steps.instruction: In the bottom of a couscoussier or large pot, brown the lamb in oil. Add the onion, tomato paste, 1 tsp each of ras el hanout, black pepper, and salt. Cover with 2 litres of water and bring to a boil. Reduce heat and simmer for 45 minutes.
steps.tip: Skim any foam that rises to the surface for a clear broth.

steps.title: Add vegetables
steps.instruction: Add the chickpeas, turnips, and carrots to the broth. Continue simmering for 20 minutes. Then add the courgettes and cook for another 15 minutes until all vegetables are tender.
steps.tip: Add vegetables in stages so nothing overcooks.

steps.title: Steam the couscous
steps.instruction: While the broth simmers, place the couscous in a wide bowl. Sprinkle with salted water and rake with your fingers. Transfer to the steamer basket and steam over the broth for 20 minutes. Remove, fluff, and repeat the process once more.
steps.tip: Steaming twice gives the lightest, fluffiest couscous. Never let the couscous touch the broth directly.

steps.title: Cook the merguez
steps.instruction: Grill or pan-fry the merguez sausages until cooked through and charred on the outside, about 8 minutes.

steps.title: Assemble and serve
steps.instruction: Mound the couscous on a large serving platter. Arrange the lamb and merguez on top. Surround with the vegetables and ladle broth over everything. Serve with harissa on the side.
steps.tip: Serve the extra broth in a separate bowl so guests can add more to their liking.

recipes.name: Amiwo
recipes.description: A savoury red corn porridge cooked in a rich tomato and crab sauce. The cornmeal absorbs the tomato colour and flavour, turning a beautiful red-orange.
recipes.story: Amiwo is a celebratory dish from southern Benin, always present at ceremonies and important gatherings. The red colour from the tomato sauce symbolises joy and festivity in Fon culture.
recipes.occasion: Celebrations and ceremonies
recipes.best_served_with: Fried chicken or grilled fish
recipes.image_alt: Amiwo recipe from Benin on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make the tomato sauce
steps.instruction: Heat oil, fry onion until soft. Add tomato paste and cook 3 minutes. Add blended tomatoes and cook until reduced and thick, about 15 minutes.

steps.title: Add crab
steps.instruction: Add crab meat to the sauce and stir. Cook for 5 minutes.

steps.title: Add cornmeal
steps.instruction: Add 3 cups water to the sauce. Bring to a boil. Gradually sprinkle in cornmeal while stirring constantly to prevent lumps.

steps.title: Cook the amiwo
steps.instruction: Reduce heat to low. Stir continuously for 15-20 minutes until the cornmeal is cooked through and pulls away from the pot sides.
steps.tip: The colour should be a vibrant red-orange.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Shape into a mound on a plate. Serve with fried chicken or grilled fish.

recipes.name: Kuli-Kuli
recipes.description: Crunchy groundnut crackers made by extracting the oil from peanut paste, then shaping and deep-frying the remaining cake. A beloved protein-rich snack.
recipes.story: Kuli-Kuli are the crunch of Beninese markets. Vendors shape the groundnut paste into sticks, rings, or balls before frying. They are eaten as snacks, crumbled into salads, or ground back into powder for soups.
recipes.occasion: Snack
recipes.best_served_with: On its own or crumbled over salads
recipes.image_alt: Kuli-Kuli recipe from Benin on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Grind the peanuts
steps.instruction: Blend roasted peanuts in a food processor until they form a thick paste, similar to peanut butter.

steps.title: Extract the oil
steps.instruction: Knead the paste vigorously, squeezing out as much oil as possible. The paste should become crumbly and dry. Reserve the extracted oil.
steps.tip: This step is crucial — the drier the paste, the crunchier the kuli-kuli.

steps.title: Season and shape
steps.instruction: Mix salt and cayenne into the paste. Shape into small sticks, rings, or flat rounds about 1cm thick.

steps.title: Fry
steps.instruction: Heat oil to 170C. Fry in batches until deep golden brown and very crunchy, about 4-5 minutes per batch.
steps.tip: Keep the heat moderate to fry through without burning.

steps.title: Drain and cool
steps.instruction: Drain on paper towels. Let cool completely — they crisp up more as they cool. Store in an airtight container.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

collections.name: Street Food
collections.description: Popular street snacks and quick bites found at markets and roadside stalls across Africa.

recipes.name: Seswaa
recipes.description: Botswana's national dish — beef slow-boiled until falling apart, then shredded and pounded to a tender, savory pulled-meat texture, seasoned simply with salt.
recipes.story: Seswaa is the pride of Botswana, served at weddings, funerals, national celebrations, and important family gatherings. The dish is traditionally prepared by men in a large three-legged cast-iron pot over an open fire. The simplicity of the recipe — just meat, water, and salt — allows the quality of Botswana's grass-fed beef to shine through.
recipes.occasion: Celebrations and weddings
recipes.best_served_with: Bogobe (sorghum porridge) or pap
recipes.image_alt: Seswaa recipe from Botswana on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Boil the meat
steps.instruction: Place the beef pieces and quartered onion in a large heavy pot. Add enough water to cover the meat by about 5cm. Bring to a boil over high heat.
steps.tip: Bone-in cuts give the broth more flavor and body.

steps.title: Skim and simmer
steps.instruction: Skim off any foam that rises to the surface. Reduce heat to low, cover, and simmer gently for about 3 hours until the meat is completely tender and falling off the bone.
steps.tip: Check occasionally and add more water if the level drops below the meat.

steps.title: Remove bones
steps.instruction: Remove the meat from the pot and discard all bones. Reserve the cooking broth. Place the meat on a large wooden board or in a mortar.

steps.title: Pound and shred
steps.instruction: Using two forks or a wooden pestle, pound and shred the meat until it has a fine, pulled texture. Season with salt and pepper.
steps.tip: Traditionally this is done with a wooden pole in the pot itself — the pounding action is what gives seswaa its name.

steps.title: Moisten and serve
steps.instruction: Return the shredded meat to the pot and add a few ladles of the reserved broth to keep it moist. Heat through and serve with bogobe, pap, or dumplings.
steps.tip: The cooking broth is precious — serve some on the side as a gravy.

recipes.name: Riz Gras
recipes.description: Burkina Faso's rich one-pot rice cooked in a meat and tomato broth with vegetables. The rice is "fat" from absorbing all the flavours of the stock.
recipes.story: Riz Gras, meaning "fat rice," is the celebratory dish of Burkina Faso. The Mossi people prepare it for weddings, baptisms, and any occasion worthy of gathering. The rice cooks directly in the meat broth, absorbing every drop of flavour.
recipes.occasion: Celebrations and family gatherings
recipes.best_served_with: Fried plantain and a simple salad
recipes.image_alt: Riz Gras recipe from Burkina Faso on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Brown the meat
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a large pot. Season meat and brown on all sides. Remove and reserve.

steps.title: Build the base
steps.instruction: Fry onions in the same pot until golden. Add tomato paste and cook 3 minutes. Add 5 cups water and the browned meat. Bring to a boil and simmer until meat is tender, about 25 minutes.

steps.title: Add vegetables
steps.instruction: Add carrots and cabbage to the pot. Cook for 5 minutes.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.instruction: Add the washed rice to the pot, ensuring liquid covers it by about 2cm. Cover tightly and cook on low heat for 25 minutes without opening.
steps.tip: The rice should absorb all the flavoured broth.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Fluff rice with a fork. Serve with the meat and vegetables arranged on top.

collections.name: One-Pot Wonders
collections.description: Hearty single-pot dishes that are easy to make and full of flavor. Less cleanup, more taste.

recipes.name: Koki Beans
recipes.description: Black-eyed peas blended into a smooth paste with palm oil and spices, then wrapped in banana leaves and steamed into a savoury, custard-like cake. A Cameroonian classic.
recipes.story: Koki is a festive dish from the grasslands of western Cameroon, traditionally prepared by the Bamileke people for celebrations and rites of passage. The black-eyed peas are soaked, peeled, and blended into a smooth batter enriched with palm oil, then steamed in banana leaf parcels. The result is a dense, flavourful cake with a beautiful orange hue from the palm oil.
recipes.occasion: Festivals, celebrations, rites of passage
recipes.best_served_with: Ripe plantains, puff-puff, or on its own as a snack
recipes.image_alt: Koki Beans recipe from Cameroon on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Prepare the beans
steps.instruction: Soak black-eyed peas overnight. Rub between your hands to loosen the skins, then rinse — the skins will float to the surface. Repeat until most skins are removed.
steps.tip: Peeling the beans is essential for a smooth texture. Patience here pays off.

steps.title: Blend the batter
steps.instruction: Blend the peeled beans with onion, scotch bonnet, and a splash of water until very smooth. Transfer to a large bowl and stir in palm oil and salt, mixing vigorously until the oil is fully incorporated.
steps.tip: The batter should be thick but pourable — add water sparingly.

steps.title: Wrap in banana leaves
steps.instruction: Soften banana leaves over a flame or in hot water. Place a ladleful of batter in the centre of each leaf and fold into a neat rectangular parcel, securing with string or toothpicks.
steps.tip: Make sure the parcels are sealed well to prevent water from getting in during steaming.

steps.title: Steam the koki
steps.instruction: Arrange parcels in a large steamer or pot with a raised platform and water at the bottom. Cover tightly and steam for 1 hour until firm and set.
steps.tip: Check water level every 20 minutes and top up if needed.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Unwrap the koki parcels and slice or serve whole. Enjoy warm with ripe plantains or on its own.
steps.tip: Koki can be reheated by re-steaming the next day.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Ndolé
recipes.description: Cameroon's national dish — bitter leaves cooked down with ground peanuts, crayfish, and prawns into a rich, complex stew. Earthy, nutty, and deeply satisfying.
recipes.story: Ndolé is the pride of the Douala people of coastal Cameroon and has been elevated to the status of national dish. The key ingredient — ndolé or bitter leaves — must be washed multiple times to reduce bitterness before being combined with ground peanuts and protein. The dish is labour-intensive but deeply rewarding, and no Cameroonian celebration is complete without it.
recipes.occasion: Weddings, national holidays, family celebrations
recipes.best_served_with: Boiled plantains, rice, or miondo (cassava sticks)
recipes.image_alt: Ndolé recipe from Cameroon on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Wash the bitter leaves
steps.instruction: Place bitter leaves in a large bowl of water, squeeze and wash vigorously to remove bitterness. Repeat at least 3 times until the water runs relatively clear. Boil the washed leaves for 15 minutes, then drain.
steps.tip: If using spinach as a substitute, skip the washing — just wilt briefly.

steps.title: Prepare the peanut paste
steps.instruction: Roast raw peanuts in a dry pan until golden. Grind in a blender with a little water to form a smooth paste.
steps.tip: Alternatively, use 200g of natural smooth peanut butter.

steps.title: Cook the aromatics
steps.instruction: Heat palm oil in a large pot. Sauté the onion until translucent. Add garlic and ground crayfish, cooking for 2 minutes until fragrant.

steps.title: Build the stew
steps.instruction: Add the peanut paste to the pot and stir continuously for 5 minutes. Add the drained bitter leaves and stock cube. Stir to combine and cook for 20 minutes on medium-low heat.
steps.tip: Stir frequently to prevent the peanut paste from sticking to the bottom.

steps.title: Add the prawns
steps.instruction: Add the prawns to the stew and cook for 8-10 minutes until pink and cooked through. Taste and adjust seasoning.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve hot with boiled plantains, rice, or miondo. The stew should be thick and glossy.
steps.tip: Ndolé tastes even better the next day as the flavours deepen overnight.

collections.name: Sunday Specials
collections.description: Elaborate weekend dishes worth the extra effort. These are the recipes that make Sunday lunch special.

recipes.name: Cachupa Rica
recipes.description: Cape Verde's national dish — a rich, slow-cooked stew of hominy corn, beans, and multiple meats and sausages. The "rica" (rich) version is the celebratory feast edition.
recipes.story: Cachupa is to Cape Verde what the soul itself is to its people. Every island has its own version, and the dish is said to embody the melting pot of African, Portuguese, and Brazilian influences that define Cape Verdean culture. Cachupa Rica is the celebratory version with multiple meats.
recipes.occasion: National holidays and celebrations
recipes.best_served_with: Fried egg on top (for leftover cachupa)
recipes.image_alt: Cachupa Rica recipe from Cape Verde on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Cook the corn and beans
steps.instruction: Drain soaked corn and beans. Place in a large pot with fresh water and boil for 45 minutes until beginning to soften.
steps.tip: The corn takes the longest — start with it.

steps.title: Add the meats
steps.instruction: Add pork ribs, beef, and sausage to the pot. Season with salt, garlic, and bay leaves. Continue simmering for 30 minutes.

steps.title: Add vegetables
steps.instruction: Add sweet potatoes, cabbage, onions, and any other vegetables. Cook for another 25-30 minutes until everything is tender.

steps.title: Adjust consistency
steps.instruction: The cachupa should be thick and hearty — more stew than soup. If too watery, cook uncovered to reduce. If too thick, add a little water.
steps.tip: Cachupa tastes even better the next day, reheated and fried.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Ladle into deep bowls. Each serving should have a generous mix of corn, beans, meats, and vegetables.

collections.name: Sunday Specials
collections.description: Elaborate weekend dishes worth the extra effort. These are the recipes that make Sunday lunch special.

recipes.name: Gozo
recipes.description: A thick, stiff cassava flour porridge that serves as the staple base for most Central African meals. Smooth, stretchy, and perfect for scooping up stews and sauces.
recipes.story: Gozo is the daily bread of the Central African Republic. Made from cassava flour and water, this stiff porridge is shaped into balls and used to scoop up every sauce and stew in the country. Preparing gozo well requires strong arms and practice — the flour must be stirred vigorously and continuously to achieve the right consistency without lumps.
recipes.occasion: Every meal, daily staple
recipes.best_served_with: Any stew or sauce — especially peanut or okra-based sauces
recipes.image_alt: Gozo recipe from Central African Republic on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Boil the water
steps.instruction: Bring 4 cups of water to a rolling boil in a heavy-bottomed pot. Add salt if using.
steps.tip: Use a heavy pot — you will be stirring vigorously.

steps.title: Add cassava flour gradually
steps.instruction: Reduce heat to medium. Begin adding cassava flour a little at a time, stirring constantly with a strong wooden spoon. Continue adding flour and stirring.
steps.tip: Add flour slowly to prevent lumps from forming.

steps.title: Stir vigorously
steps.instruction: Once all the flour is incorporated, stir vigorously for 10-12 minutes. The mixture should pull away from the sides of the pot and form a smooth, stretchy dough.
steps.tip: This requires significant arm strength — do not stop stirring or the bottom will scorch.

steps.title: Shape and serve
steps.instruction: Wet your hands and shape the gozo into smooth balls. Place on a plate and serve immediately alongside your choice of stew or sauce.
steps.tip: Dip your hands in water before shaping to prevent sticking.

collections.name: Quick & Easy
collections.description: Delicious African dishes ready in 30 minutes or less. Perfect for busy weeknight dinners.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Daraba
recipes.description: A Chadian peanut-okra stew with greens, tomato, sweet potato, and a creamy sauce made for boule or rice.
recipes.story: Daraba is the kind of stew that feeds a table without fuss. Okra gives it body, peanut butter gives it richness, and greens turn the pot earthy and generous.
recipes.occasion: Everyday meals, communal dining
recipes.best_served_with: Boule (millet porridge) or rice
recipes.regional_variations: Sweet potato, pumpkin, or eggplant can move in and out of the pot depending on the market.
recipes.image_alt: Daraba recipe from Chad on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

ingredients.name: mixed greens
ingredients.prep_note: spinach, sorrel, cassava leaves, or sweet potato leaves, chopped

steps.title: Start the base
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a large pot. Cook onion until soft, then add tomatoes and scotch bonnet if using. Cook until the tomatoes collapse.

steps.title: Simmer the firm vegetables
steps.instruction: Add sweet potato, eggplant if using, okra, and water or stock. Simmer until the sweet potato is almost tender.

steps.title: Stir in the peanut
steps.instruction: Whisk peanut butter with a ladle of hot broth, then stir it back into the pot until the sauce turns creamy.
steps.tip: Loosening the peanut butter first keeps the sauce smooth.

steps.title: Add the greens
steps.instruction: Fold in the chopped greens, cover, and cook until tender. The stew should be thick enough to sit on boule.

steps.title: Finish
steps.instruction: Season with salt and black pepper. Serve hot with boule, rice, or sorghum.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Moambe Chicken
recipes.description: Tender chicken simmered in palm nut sauce with tomato, onion, garlic, chile, and optional okra.
recipes.story: Moambe Chicken belongs to the Congo Basin table. The sauce should be deep orange, rich from palm nut cream, and thick enough to cling to chicken before it meets fufu, rice, or plantain.
recipes.occasion: Sunday meals, celebrations, family gatherings
recipes.best_served_with: Fufu, boiled plantains, or white rice
recipes.image_alt: Moambe Chicken recipe from Democratic Republic of Congo on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Brown the chicken
steps.instruction: Season chicken with salt and black pepper. Heat oil and brown the pieces on all sides, then remove them from the pot.

steps.title: Cook the aromatics
steps.instruction: Cook onions in the same pot until soft. Add garlic and tomato paste and stir until fragrant.

steps.title: Build the sauce
steps.instruction: Add tomatoes, palm nut cream, water, and okra if using. Stir until smooth.

steps.title: Simmer
steps.instruction: Return chicken to the pot, add whole scotch bonnets, cover, and simmer until the chicken is tender and the sauce is thick.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Remove the whole peppers, adjust seasoning, and serve with fufu, rice, or boiled plantains.

recipes.name: Koshari
recipes.description: Egypt's iconic street food — layers of rice, lentils, macaroni, and chickpeas topped with a spiced tomato sauce, garlicky vinegar dressing, and shatteringly crispy fried onions.
recipes.story: Koshari is Cairo in layers: rice, lentils, pasta, chickpeas, tomato sauce, garlicky vinegar, and a crown of onions fried until crisp.
recipes.occasion: Everyday lunch, street food
recipes.best_served_with: Extra crispy onions and hot sauce
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks fold vermicelli into the rice, some keep the rice plain. The tomato sauce, dakka, and fried onions carry the soul either way.
recipes.image_alt: Koshari recipe from Egypt on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Cook the lentils
steps.instruction: Boil lentils in salted water until tender but still holding shape. Drain and set aside.

steps.title: Cook rice and pasta
steps.instruction: Toast vermicelli in a little onion oil if using, then cook with rice. Boil macaroni separately until al dente.

steps.title: Fry the onions
steps.instruction: Fry sliced onions in batches until deep golden and crisp. Drain well and keep the onion oil.

steps.title: Make tomato sauce
steps.instruction: Cook half the garlic in a little onion oil. Add tomatoes, some vinegar, cumin, salt, and chile, then simmer until thick.

steps.title: Make dakka
steps.instruction: Sizzle the remaining garlic in oil, then add vinegar and cumin. It should smell sharp and garlicky.

steps.title: Assemble
steps.instruction: Layer rice, lentils, macaroni, and chickpeas. Spoon over tomato sauce and dakka, then finish with crispy onions.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

collections.name: One-Pot Wonders
collections.description: Hearty single-pot dishes that are easy to make and full of flavor. Less cleanup, more taste.

collections.name: Street Food
collections.description: Popular street snacks and quick bites found at markets and roadside stalls across Africa.

recipes.name: Succotash de Guinée
recipes.description: A colorful bean, corn, okra, and tomato stew cooked with palm oil and chile in an Equatoguinean home-style frame.
recipes.story: Succotash has a complicated route through the Atlantic world, so cook this page as a practical bean-and-corn stew rather than a fixed ancient formula. The strength is in the contrast: creamy peas, sweet corn, tender okra, and tomato.
recipes.occasion: Everyday meals, communal dining
recipes.best_served_with: Fried plantains, rice, grilled fish, or roast chicken
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions stay simple with beans and corn, while others add okra, bell pepper, or green onion.
recipes.image_alt: Succotash de Guinée recipe from Equatorial Guinea on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Cook the peas
steps.instruction: Cover soaked black-eyed peas with fresh water and simmer until tender but not falling apart. Drain.

steps.title: Build the base
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a pot. Cook onion until soft, then add garlic, tomato, bell pepper if using, and chile. Cook until the tomato turns saucy.

steps.title: Simmer vegetables
steps.instruction: Add cooked peas, corn, okra, and water or stock. Simmer until the okra is tender and the stew thickens.

steps.title: Finish
steps.instruction: Season with salt and black pepper. Scatter green onions over the top if using and serve hot.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Sishwala with Emasi
recipes.description: Thick Swazi maize porridge served with cool tangy emasi, the simple pairing that makes the plate complete.
recipes.story: Sishwala should be firm enough to scoop, while emasi brings the cool sour balance. The dish is humble, filling, and deeply Swazi.
recipes.occasion: Daily meals and ceremonies
recipes.best_served_with: Umbhidvo, bean stew, grilled meat, or tomato relish
recipes.regional_variations: Some tables pour emasi over the porridge, while others serve it on the side for dipping.
recipes.image_alt: Sishwala with Emasi recipe from Eswatini on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Boil water
steps.instruction: Bring the water and salt to a boil in a heavy-bottomed pot.

steps.title: Make thin porridge
steps.instruction: Mix about a quarter of the maize meal with a little cold water to form a paste. Stir this into the boiling water. Cook for 5 minutes, stirring constantly.

steps.title: Thicken
steps.instruction: Gradually add the remaining maize meal in batches, stirring vigorously after each addition. Use a strong wooden spoon and put your weight into it — the porridge will become very thick and stiff.
steps.tip: The sishwala should be firmer than regular porridge — thick enough to hold its shape when scooped.

steps.title: Steam
steps.instruction: Reduce heat to very low. Cover the pot tightly and let the sishwala steam for 10-15 minutes until fully cooked.

steps.title: Serve with emasi
steps.instruction: Scoop the sishwala onto plates, forming a mound. Pour generous amounts of emasi over the top or serve it in a bowl on the side. The cool, tangy emasi complements the warm, bland sishwala perfectly.
steps.tip: Traditionally, emasi is served at room temperature, not chilled, to complement the warm sishwala.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Umbhidvo Wetintsanga
recipes.description: Tender Swazi pumpkin leaves simmered with tomato, onion, and crushed groundnuts until earthy, nutty, and soft.
recipes.story: Umbhidvo wetintsanga is garden cooking at its best. Young pumpkin leaves soften quickly, and crushed groundnuts turn the pot creamy without needing dairy.
recipes.occasion: Everyday side dish
recipes.best_served_with: Sishwala, emasi, beans, rice, or stewed meat
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions are only leaves and groundnuts; others add onion, tomato, or a little oil.
recipes.image_alt: Umbhidvo Wetintsanga recipe from Eswatini on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Prepare the leaves
steps.instruction: Wash pumpkin leaves well, remove tough stems, and chop. Blanch briefly if the leaves are mature or hairy.
steps.tip: Young tender leaves can go straight into the pot.

steps.title: Crush the groundnuts
steps.instruction: Pound groundnuts until coarse. Do not turn them into a smooth paste.

steps.title: Cook the base
steps.instruction: Heat oil, soften the onion, then add tomatoes and cook until saucy.

steps.title: Simmer greens
steps.instruction: Add pumpkin leaves, crushed groundnuts, water or stock, and salt. Cover and simmer until the leaves are tender and the groundnuts turn creamy.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve warm with sishwala or another starch.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Doro Wat
recipes.description: Ethiopian chicken and egg stew built on slow-cooked onions, berbere, tomato paste, and niter kibbeh.
recipes.story: Doro wat rewards patience. Let the onions reduce properly, bloom the berbere in spiced butter, then cook the chicken until the sauce clings dark red and deep.
recipes.best_served_with: Injera, ayib, gomen, lentils, or a simple tomato salad
recipes.regional_variations: For a dairy-free fasting-style adaptation, use spiced oil instead of niter kibbeh.
recipes.image_alt: Doro Wat recipe from Ethiopia on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Marinate Chicken
steps.instruction: Rub chicken pieces with lemon juice and salt. Set aside for 30 minutes.
steps.tip: The lemon bath removes gamey flavors and tenderizes the meat.

steps.title: Dry-Cook Onions
steps.instruction: Place finely diced onions in a dry heavy pot over medium heat. Stir frequently until onions are deeply browned and reduced by half. Do not add oil or water.
steps.tip: This is the most important step — patience here builds the stew's depth of flavor.

steps.title: Build Sauce
steps.instruction: Add niter kibbeh, garlic, ginger, and berbere spice. Stir continuously for 5 minutes. Add tomato paste and water. Simmer until oil separates from sauce.

steps.title: Cook Chicken
steps.instruction: Add chicken pieces to the sauce, turning to coat. Cover and simmer on low heat until chicken is very tender and sauce is thick.

steps.title: Add Eggs
steps.instruction: Score each hard-boiled egg with a knife in a crosshatch pattern. Nestle eggs into the stew and simmer 10 more minutes so they absorb the sauce.
steps.tip: Scoring the eggs lets the berbere flavor penetrate — the deeper the cuts, the more flavorful.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Ladle doro wat onto a large platter of injera. Place eggs on top. Serve with extra injera on the side for scooping.
steps.tip: Eat communally from a shared platter — it is called "gursha" when you hand-feed someone a morsel.

collections.name: Sunday Specials
collections.description: Elaborate weekend dishes worth the extra effort. These are the recipes that make Sunday lunch special.

recipes.name: Mustard Chicken
recipes.description: Gabonese-style mustard chicken marinated with lemon, garlic, onion, chile, salt, and pepper, then roasted until golden.
recipes.story: Mustard does the heavy lifting here. Give the chicken time in the marinade, roast it hot, and spoon the pan juices back over the meat.
recipes.occasion: Gatherings, weeknight dinner
recipes.best_served_with: Rice, fried plantains, cassava, green salad, or tomato relish
recipes.image_alt: Mustard Chicken recipe from Gabon on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

ingredients.name: chile flakes or minced fresh chile

steps.title: Make the marinade
steps.instruction: Mix mustard, lemon juice, garlic, oil, chile if using, salt, and black pepper.

steps.title: Marinate chicken
steps.instruction: Coat chicken well, cover, and refrigerate for at least 30 minutes.
steps.tip: Two hours gives a deeper mustard flavor.

steps.title: Roast
steps.instruction: Heat oven to 200C. Arrange chicken skin-side up with onion rings and roast until golden and cooked through.

steps.title: Rest and serve
steps.instruction: Rest 5 minutes, then spoon pan juices over the chicken and serve.

recipes.name: Nyembwe Chicken
recipes.description: Gabon's palm-nut chicken stew, simmered with onion, tomato, garlic, chile, and nyembwe sauce until rich and tender.
recipes.story: Nyembwe is about the sauce. Brown the chicken, soften the aromatics, then let palm nut cream simmer gently until it coats every piece.
recipes.occasion: National celebrations, family feasts, Sunday lunch
recipes.best_served_with: Boiled plantains, cassava, rice, fufu, or baton de manioc
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks season the chicken with mustard or bouillon; others keep the palm nut sauce simpler.
recipes.image_alt: Nyembwe Chicken recipe from Gabon on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

ingredients.name: palm nut cream or sauce graine
neutral oil or red palm oil

steps.title: Season chicken
steps.instruction: Season chicken with salt, pepper, garlic, and mustard if using. Rest briefly.

steps.title: Brown chicken
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a heavy pot and brown the chicken on all sides. Remove to a plate.

steps.title: Cook aromatics
steps.instruction: Cook onions in the same pot until soft, then add tomatoes and cook until they collapse.

steps.title: Add nyembwe
steps.instruction: Stir in palm nut cream and water or stock until smooth. Add the whole chile if using.

steps.title: Braise
steps.instruction: Return chicken to the pot, cover, and simmer gently until tender and the sauce thickens.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Remove the whole chile. Taste for salt and serve hot with plantain, cassava, rice, or fufu.

recipes.name: Domoda
recipes.description: Gambian groundnut stew with beef, tomato, pumpkin, chile, lemon, and a creamy peanut sauce served over rice.
recipes.story: Domoda should be rich but not rushed. Let the peanut sauce simmer until glossy and the pumpkin softens into the stew.
recipes.occasion: Everyday meals and celebrations
recipes.best_served_with: Plain white rice, lime wedges, cucumber, or steamed greens
recipes.regional_variations: Chicken, fish, beef, or vegetarian versions are all common. Pumpkin, sweet potato, okra, or bitter tomato can appear depending on the cook.
recipes.image_alt: Domoda recipe from Gambia on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Brown the meat
steps.instruction: Season beef with salt and pepper, then brown in a little oil. Remove and reserve.

steps.title: Start the stew
steps.instruction: Cook onions and tomatoes until soft. Stir in tomato paste, water or stock, and the browned meat.

steps.title: Add groundnut
steps.instruction: Whisk peanut butter with warm broth from the pot, then stir it back in until smooth. Add pumpkin and whole chile.
steps.tip: Loosening the peanut butter first keeps the sauce from clumping.

steps.title: Simmer slowly
steps.instruction: Cook on low heat until the meat and pumpkin are tender and the sauce is thick and glossy.

steps.title: Finish
steps.instruction: Taste for salt, pepper, and lemon or lime. Serve hot over white rice.

recipes.name: Ghanaian Jollof Rice
recipes.description: Ghanaian jollof rice with basmati rice steamed in a reduced tomato, onion, ginger, garlic, chile, and bay-leaf stew.
recipes.story: Ghanaian jollof is all about the stew before the rice. Fry the tomato base until it tastes rounded, then let basmati steam gently under a tight lid.
recipes.occasion: Parties, funerals, and Sunday meals
recipes.best_served_with: Shito, fried plantain, grilled chicken, salad, or kelewele
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks use curry powder, thyme, nutmeg, or stock cubes. The rice is often perfumed basmati or another long-grain rice.
recipes.image_alt: Ghanaian Jollof Rice recipe from Ghana on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

ingredients.name: canned tomatoes or fresh tomato puree

steps.title: Blend the sauce
steps.instruction: Blend tomatoes with one onion, garlic, and ginger until smooth.

steps.title: Fry the tomato stew
steps.instruction: Heat oil, cook sliced onion, then fry tomato paste. Add blended sauce, bay leaves, chile, and optional curry or thyme. Cook until thick and glossy.

steps.title: Add rice
steps.instruction: Stir in washed rice until coated. Add hot stock or water and season well.

steps.title: Steam
steps.instruction: Cover tightly with foil and a lid. Cook on low until the rice is tender and the liquid is absorbed.

steps.title: Rest and fluff
steps.instruction: Rest off heat for 10 minutes, remove bay leaves and chile, then fluff gently.

collections.name: One-Pot Wonders
collections.description: Hearty single-pot dishes that are easy to make and full of flavor. Less cleanup, more taste.

recipes.name: Guinean Chicken Yassa
recipes.description: Guinean-style chicken yassa with lime, ginger, mustard, garlic, hot pepper, and plenty of slow-cooked onions.
recipes.story: A good yassa is onion first. Marinate the chicken sharply, brown it well, then let the onions collapse into a sweet-tangy sauce.
recipes.occasion: Family meals and celebrations
recipes.best_served_with: Steamed white rice, fried plantains, or a simple cucumber salad
recipes.regional_variations: Senegalese, Gambian, and Guinean versions overlap, with ginger and chile levels shifting by cook.
recipes.image_alt: Guinean Chicken Yassa recipe from Guinea on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Marinate
steps.instruction: Combine chicken with onions, citrus juice, mustard, garlic, ginger, peppers, bay leaf if using, salt, and pepper. Chill for at least 2 hours.

steps.title: Brown chicken
steps.instruction: Lift chicken from the marinade. Brown it in hot oil on all sides, then remove.

steps.title: Cook onions
steps.instruction: Add the onions and marinade to the pot. Cook until the onions are very soft and golden.

steps.title: Simmer
steps.instruction: Return chicken to the pot, cover, and simmer until tender and the onion sauce is thick.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Taste for salt and acidity. Serve over white rice.

recipes.name: Caldo de Mancarra
recipes.description: Guinea-Bissau peanut broth with chicken, tomato, sweet potato, ginger, chile, and a creamy mancarra sauce for rice.
recipes.story: Caldo de mancarra should feel like a broth that became generous. Keep the peanut smooth, the chicken tender, and the rice ready to drink up the sauce.
recipes.occasion: Everyday meals
recipes.best_served_with: White rice, lime, cilantro, or boiled cassava
recipes.regional_variations: Chicken, fish, shrimp, okra, carrot, or sweet potato may appear depending on the cook and coast.
recipes.image_alt: Caldo de Mancarra recipe from Guinea-Bissau on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Season and brown chicken
steps.instruction: Season chicken with salt and pepper. Brown in oil, then remove.

steps.title: Cook the base
steps.instruction: Cook onions, garlic, ginger, and tomatoes until soft and saucy.

steps.title: Add peanut broth
steps.instruction: Whisk peanut paste with warm stock, then add to the pot with remaining stock, chicken, sweet potatoes, and hot pepper.

steps.title: Simmer
steps.instruction: Simmer gently until the chicken is tender and the sweet potato is soft.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Taste for salt and pepper. Serve hot over white rice.

recipes.name: Attiéké with Grilled Fish
recipes.description: Fermented cassava attiéké served with grilled fish, onion-tomato salad, cucumber, lime, and hot Ivorian piment.
recipes.story: The plate works because every part has a job: smoky fish, fluffy attiéké, fresh onion and tomato, and a hot piment that wakes everything up.
recipes.occasion: Street-food lunches
recipes.best_served_with: Alloco, extra piment, sliced cucumber, and cold ginger juice
recipes.regional_variations: The fish may be grilled, fried, or oven-roasted; the attiéké is commonly finished with a little oil and served with raw garnish.
recipes.image_alt: Attiéké with Grilled Fish recipe from Ivory Coast on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Marinate fish
steps.instruction: Rub fish with oil, garlic, ginger, mustard if using, lime juice, salt, and pepper. Rest while the grill heats.

steps.title: Grill fish
steps.instruction: Grill or roast the fish until the skin is crisp and the flesh flakes easily, turning once.

steps.title: Steam attiéké
steps.instruction: Steam the attiéké until hot and fluffy. Break up clumps with a fork and drizzle with a little oil.

steps.title: Prepare garnish
steps.instruction: Toss onion, tomato, cucumber if using, salt, lime, and a little piment. Keep some piment separate for the table.

steps.title: Plate
steps.instruction: Serve the attiéké with grilled fish, fresh garnish, and piment on the side.

collections.name: Street Food
collections.description: Popular street snacks and quick bites found at markets and roadside stalls across Africa.

recipes.name: Kedjenou
recipes.description: A slow-cooked chicken stew made without any added water — the chicken and vegetables steam in their own juices with aromatic herbs and spices.
recipes.story: Kedjenou is the signature dish of the Baoulé people. Traditionally cooked in a sealed canari (clay pot) over low heat, the chicken releases its own juices creating an intensely flavoured broth without adding any water.
recipes.occasion: Family meals and gatherings
recipes.best_served_with: Attiéké or white rice
recipes.image_alt: Kedjenou recipe from Ivory Coast on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Layer the pot
steps.instruction: Place chicken in a heavy pot or Dutch oven. Add tomatoes, onions, eggplant, garlic, ginger, bay leaves, and whole scotch bonnet. Season with salt and pepper.
steps.tip: Do not add any water — this is the key to kedjenou.

steps.title: Seal and cook
steps.instruction: Cover the pot tightly with foil and then the lid to create a perfect seal. Cook on the lowest heat possible for 45 minutes.
steps.tip: Do not open the lid during cooking — shake the pot occasionally instead.

steps.title: Check doneness
steps.instruction: After 45 minutes, carefully open the lid. The chicken should be falling off the bone in a rich, concentrated broth of its own juices.

steps.title: Simmer if needed
steps.instruction: If the chicken needs more time, reseal and cook another 15 minutes. The sauce should be thick and deeply flavoured.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve hot with attiéké or fluffy white rice. The concentrated sauce is incredibly flavourful.

recipes.name: Ugali na Sukuma Wiki
recipes.description: Kenyan firm maize ugali served with sukuma wiki, collard greens quickly cooked with onion, tomato, garlic, and oil.
recipes.story: Ugali gives the plate its strength; sukuma wiki gives it life. Stir the ugali hard, then cook the greens quickly so they stay fresh.
recipes.occasion: Everyday meals
recipes.best_served_with: Nyama choma, beef stew, beans, or kachumbari
recipes.regional_variations: Some homes keep sukuma wiki very plain with onion and salt; others add tomato, garlic, chile, or a little stock.
recipes.image_alt: Ugali na Sukuma Wiki recipe from Kenya on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Boil water
steps.instruction: Bring water and salt to a strong boil in a heavy pot.

steps.title: Cook ugali
steps.instruction: Add maize flour gradually while stirring hard. Keep folding and pressing until the ugali is firm and pulls from the sides.

steps.title: Shape ugali
steps.instruction: Turn onto a wet plate and shape into a dome with a wet spoon. Cover to keep warm.

steps.title: Start greens
steps.instruction: Heat oil and cook onion until soft. Add garlic if using, then tomatoes, and cook until they break down.

steps.title: Cook sukuma
steps.instruction: Add sliced greens and chile if using. Toss until coated, then cover briefly until wilted but still bright.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Season the greens and serve hot beside the ugali.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Papa le Moroho
recipes.description: Lesotho's everyday plate of firm maize papa served with moroho, leafy greens cooked with onion, tomato, oil, and salt.
recipes.story: Papa le moroho is simple by design. Make the papa firm enough to scoop and keep the greens tender, seasoned, and generous.
recipes.occasion: Everyday meals
recipes.best_served_with: Beans, stewed meat, eggs, or tomato relish
recipes.regional_variations: Moroho may be wild greens, spinach, chard, or cabbage depending on season and household.
recipes.image_alt: Papa le Moroho recipe from Lesotho on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Start papa
steps.instruction: Bring most of the water to a boil. Mix a little maize meal with cool water to make a slurry, then stir it into the pot.

steps.title: Thicken papa
steps.instruction: Add the remaining maize meal gradually, stirring hard until very thick and pulling from the pot.

steps.title: Steam papa
steps.instruction: Cover and steam the papa on low heat so the meal cooks through.

steps.title: Cook tomato base
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a pan. Cook onion until soft, then add tomatoes and salt and cook until saucy.

steps.title: Cook moroho
steps.instruction: Add chopped greens and cook until tender. Add a splash of water only if the pan dries out.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve a mound of papa with moroho alongside or spooned over the top.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Palm Butter Soup
recipes.description: Liberian palm butter soup with palm fruit concentrate, beef or goat, smoked fish, dried shrimp, chile, okra, and rice.
recipes.story: Palm butter soup should be deep orange, glossy, and rich. Give the palm base time to simmer until the oil rises and the smoke from the fish seasons the whole pot.
recipes.occasion: Sunday rice meals
recipes.best_served_with: White rice, fufu, dumboy, or boiled cassava
recipes.regional_variations: Fresh palm nuts make the deepest flavor, but palm fruit concentrate is the practical home shortcut.
recipes.image_alt: Palm Butter Soup recipe from Liberia on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Cook meat
steps.instruction: Season meat with salt, pepper, onion, and garlic. Simmer with water until nearly tender, then reserve the stock.

steps.title: Start palm base
steps.instruction: Whisk palm fruit concentrate with stock in a large pot and bring to a simmer.

steps.title: Add proteins
steps.instruction: Add cooked meat, smoked fish, dried shrimp if using, onions, garlic, and peppers.

steps.title: Simmer glossy
steps.instruction: Simmer until the soup thickens and red oil rises to the surface. Stir occasionally so it does not catch.

steps.title: Finish vegetables
steps.instruction: Add okra or bitterball if using and cook until tender. Adjust salt and serve over rice.

recipes.name: Torborgee
recipes.description: Liberian torborgee, a fiery palm-oil stew of red cowpeas or lentils, bitter eggplant, dried seafood, onion, garlic, and chile.
recipes.story: Torborgee is not shy food. It should be thick, red with palm oil, bitter around the edges, and hot enough that plain rice becomes necessary.
recipes.occasion: Bold rice meals
recipes.best_served_with: White rice, fufu, or boiled cassava
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions use red cowpeas, lentils, or African pea eggplants; proteins may include dried fish, shrimp, chicken, smoked meat, or no meat.
recipes.image_alt: Torborgee recipe from Liberia on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

ingredients.name: African pea eggplant or bitter eggplant

steps.title: Cook beans
steps.instruction: Simmer cowpeas or lentils in water until tender but not falling apart. Reserve some cooking liquid.

steps.title: Build palm base
steps.instruction: Heat palm oil and cook onions until soft. Add garlic and peppers and stir until fragrant.

steps.title: Add bitter vegetable
steps.instruction: Add bitter eggplant if using and cook until it begins to soften.

steps.title: Add proteins and beans
steps.instruction: Stir in dried fish, dried shrimp if using, cooked beans, and enough cooking liquid for a thick stew.

steps.title: Simmer thick
steps.instruction: Simmer until the palm oil colors the stew deeply and the texture is thick enough to spoon over rice.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Adjust salt and pepper. Serve in a small, intense portion over plenty of white rice.

recipes.name: Bazin
recipes.description: Libyan bazin, a dense barley-flour dome surrounded by lamb tomato sauce, potatoes, eggs, and warm spices.
recipes.story: Bazin is communal food. The dough should be firm enough to tear, and the sauce should be rich enough to season every bite.
recipes.occasion: Friday lunch, Eid, weddings
recipes.best_served_with: Hard-boiled eggs, potatoes, lamb sauce, and simple salad
recipes.image_alt: Bazin recipe from Libya on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Start the stew
steps.instruction: Heat olive oil in a large pot. Brown the lamb on all sides. Add onions and cook until golden. Stir in tomato paste, turmeric, salt, pepper, and 1 tsp cayenne. Add enough water to cover the meat generously.

steps.title: Simmer until tender
steps.instruction: Bring to a boil, then reduce to a low simmer. Cover and cook for about 60 minutes. Add potatoes in the last 20 minutes. The lamb should be falling-off-the-bone tender and the sauce thick.
steps.tip: The sauce needs to be rich and reduced — it is the dipping sauce for the bazin.

steps.title: Make the bazin dough
steps.instruction: Bring 3 cups of salted water to a boil. Gradually add the barley flour while stirring vigorously with a wooden spoon. Keep stirring and cooking over low heat for 15 minutes until you have a very thick, smooth dough that pulls away from the pot.
steps.tip: Stir constantly and use muscle — the dough should be very thick and smooth.

steps.title: Shape the dome
steps.instruction: Wet your hands and shape the barley dough into a smooth dome. Place it in the centre of a large round serving tray.
steps.tip: Dip your hands in cold water to prevent sticking.

steps.title: Assemble and serve
steps.instruction: Arrange the lamb and potatoes around the bazin dome. Pour the stew sauce over and around it. Place the hard-boiled egg halves on top. Serve communally and eat by hand.
steps.tip: Tear small pieces of bazin and dip into the sauce — the combination is extraordinary.

collections.name: Sunday Specials
collections.description: Elaborate weekend dishes worth the extra effort. These are the recipes that make Sunday lunch special.

recipes.name: Tigadegena
recipes.description: Malian peanut stew with lamb, beef, or chicken simmered in tomato, onion, garlic, groundnut paste, and vegetables.
recipes.story: Tigadegena is all about a smooth peanut sauce. Loosen the paste first, simmer low, and stir so the groundnuts do not catch.
recipes.occasion: Daily meals and family gatherings
recipes.best_served_with: White rice, toh, fonio, couscous, or steamed millet
recipes.regional_variations: Beef, chicken, goat, or vegetables can be used. Some cooks add cabbage, sweet potato, or chilli for a fuller family-style stew.
recipes.image_alt: Tigadegena recipe from Mali on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

ingredients.name: natural peanut paste or peanut butter

steps.title: Brown the lamb
steps.instruction: Season lamb with salt. Heat oil and brown on all sides. Remove and reserve.

steps.title: Fry the aromatics
steps.instruction: Add onions and fry until softened. Stir in tomato paste and cook 3 minutes.

steps.title: Create peanut sauce
steps.instruction: Dissolve peanut paste in 3 cups warm water. Pour into pot with browned lamb. Simmer.

steps.title: Cook the stew
steps.instruction: Add potatoes and eggplant. Cover and simmer 40 minutes until meat is fork-tender and sauce thickened.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve over white rice or alongside toh.

recipes.name: Mauritanian Fish Rice
recipes.description: Mauritanian fish rice with tomato-rich broth, fried fish, cabbage, carrots, eggplant, okra, and rice cooked in the sauce.
recipes.story: Good thieboudienne is built in layers: season the fish, cook the vegetables in the broth, then let the rice absorb everything left in the pot.
recipes.occasion: Lunch gatherings
recipes.best_served_with: Chile paste, lime wedges, cucumber salad, or extra fried fish
recipes.image_alt: Mauritanian Fish Rice recipe from Mauritania on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

ingredients.name: firm ocean fish
ingredients.prep_note: cleaned, scored, and cut in large pieces

steps.title: Season fish
steps.instruction: Pound parsley, garlic, chile, lemon juice, salt, and pepper. Rub it into the scored fish and rest briefly.

steps.title: Brown fish
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a wide pot and brown the fish on both sides. Remove it before it flakes apart.

steps.title: Build broth
steps.instruction: Cook onions, tomato paste, and chopped tomatoes until the paste darkens. Add water or fish stock.

steps.title: Cook vegetables
steps.instruction: Add carrots, cabbage, eggplant, okra, and sweet potato or cassava if using. Simmer until just tender, then lift out with the fish.

steps.title: Cook rice
steps.instruction: Stir rinsed rice into the seasoned broth. Cover and cook low until the rice absorbs the sauce.

steps.title: Assemble platter
steps.instruction: Mound rice on a platter and arrange fish and vegetables over the top. Serve with chile paste or lemon.

collections.name: Sunday Specials
collections.description: Elaborate weekend dishes worth the extra effort. These are the recipes that make Sunday lunch special.

collections.name: One-Pot Wonders
collections.description: Hearty single-pot dishes that are easy to make and full of flavor. Less cleanup, more taste.

recipes.name: Chicken Tagine with Preserved Lemons & Olives
recipes.description: Moroccan chicken tagine with preserved lemons, olives, saffron, turmeric, onion, parsley, and cilantro.
recipes.story: The sauce should reduce into a glossy onion gravy. Add the preserved lemon and olives late so they perfume the dish without turning bitter.
recipes.occasion: Friday family lunch
recipes.best_served_with: Moroccan bread, cucumber salad, mint tea, or simple roasted vegetables
recipes.image_alt: Chicken Tagine with Preserved Lemons & Olives recipe from Morocco on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Marinate chicken
steps.instruction: Rub chicken with garlic, ginger, turmeric, saffron water if using, salt, pepper, half the herbs, and olive oil.

steps.title: Build tagine
steps.instruction: Spread onions in a tagine or heavy pot. Add chicken and any marinade, then pour in a little water or stock.

steps.title: Cook gently
steps.instruction: Cover and cook over medium-low heat until the chicken is tender and the onion sauce has reduced.

steps.title: Add lemon and olives
steps.instruction: Add preserved lemon rind and olives. Simmer uncovered until the sauce is glossy and concentrated.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Scatter remaining parsley and cilantro over the tagine and serve with bread for scooping.

collections.name: One-Pot Wonders
collections.description: Hearty single-pot dishes that are easy to make and full of flavor. Less cleanup, more taste.

recipes.name: Potjiekos
recipes.description: Namibian potjiekos, a slow layered cast-iron stew of beef, onions, roots, vegetables, stock, and gentle fire.
recipes.story: A potjie is not stirred like stew. Build the layers, manage the coals, and let the pot steam and braise until the meat gives way.
recipes.occasion: Outdoor gatherings
recipes.best_served_with: Rice, oshifima, fresh bread, or simple salad
recipes.image_alt: Potjiekos recipe from Namibia on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

ingredients.name: beef chuck, lamb, or game meat

steps.title: Brown the meat
steps.instruction: Heat oil in the potjie pot over medium-hot coals. Brown the beef cubes in batches until well-seared on all sides. Remove and set aside.
steps.tip: Good browning creates the flavor base for the entire dish.

steps.title: Layer the onions
steps.instruction: Spread the onion rings over the bottom of the pot. Place the browned meat on top of the onions. Season with salt and pepper.

steps.title: Layer the vegetables
steps.instruction: Add the carrots in an even layer over the meat. Follow with the green beans, then the potatoes on top. Season each layer lightly with salt and pepper.
steps.tip: The order matters — root vegetables near the bottom, delicate ones on top.

steps.title: Add liquid and seal
steps.instruction: Pour the beef stock and red wine gently down the side of the pot so as not to disturb the layers. Place the lid on tightly.
steps.tip: The lid should create a good seal — some cooks place foil under the lid for extra sealing.

steps.title: Slow cook
steps.instruction: Reduce the coals to maintain a very gentle simmer. Cook without lifting the lid for 2 to 2.5 hours. Resist the urge to stir — the layers must cook undisturbed.
steps.tip: If using a stovetop, keep the heat at the lowest possible setting.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Remove the lid, check that the meat is tender, and gently ladle out portions, keeping the layers visible. Serve with rice or fresh bread.
steps.tip: The bottom layer will have the richest, most concentrated flavors.

recipes.name: Dambou
recipes.description: Nigerien dambou, steamed millet or couscous grains mixed with moringa leaves, onion, chile, oil, and crushed peanuts.
recipes.story: Dambou works best when the grains stay separate. Steam, break the clumps, then fold in the greens and seasoning while everything is warm.
recipes.occasion: Everyday meals
recipes.best_served_with: Tomato sauce, grilled fish, roast meat, or extra chile oil
recipes.image_alt: Dambou recipe from Niger on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

ingredients.name: millet flour, rice flour, or fine couscous grains

steps.title: Form grains
steps.instruction: Sprinkle water into the millet flour or couscous while rubbing with your fingers to form tiny grains.

steps.title: Steam first
steps.instruction: Steam the grains in a lined steamer until firm and fluffy.

steps.title: Cook greens
steps.instruction: Blanch or steam moringa leaves until tender, then squeeze lightly if very wet.

steps.title: Mix and finish
steps.instruction: Break up the steamed grains and fold in moringa, onion, pepper, oil, crushed peanuts, and salt.

steps.title: Warm through
steps.instruction: Return to the steamer briefly so the flavors settle and the texture stays fluffy.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Jollof Rice
recipes.description: Nigerian party jollof rice cooked in a fried tomato-pepper base with stock, thyme, curry powder, bay leaves, and a smoky bottom.
recipes.story: The sauce must fry until it tastes rounded before the rice enters. Tight cover, low heat, and a little bottom char bring the party flavor.
recipes.occasion: Parties and celebrations
recipes.best_served_with: Fried plantain, chicken, beef, coleslaw, moin moin, or suya
recipes.image_alt: Jollof Rice recipe from Nigeria on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Reduce pepper base
steps.instruction: Blend tomatoes, red peppers, scotch bonnets, and one onion. Boil down until thick and not watery.

steps.title: Fry stew
steps.instruction: Heat oil, fry sliced onion, then add tomato paste and cook until darkened. Stir in the reduced pepper base.

steps.title: Season sauce
steps.instruction: Add curry powder, thyme, bay leaves, ginger-garlic if using, seasoning cubes if using, salt, and pepper. Fry until oil rises.

steps.title: Add rice
steps.instruction: Stir in washed rice until every grain is coated. Add enough stock to sit just below or level with the rice.

steps.title: Steam rice
steps.instruction: Cover tightly with foil and a lid. Cook low until the rice is tender, then fluff from the bottom.

steps.title: Smoke bottom
steps.instruction: Raise heat briefly at the end to toast the bottom lightly for party jollof flavor, then rest before serving.

collections.name: One-Pot Wonders
collections.description: Hearty single-pot dishes that are easy to make and full of flavor. Less cleanup, more taste.

recipes.name: Poulet à la Muambé
recipes.description: Congolese poulet à la muambé, chicken braised in palm butter sauce with tomato, garlic, chile, bay leaf, and okra.
recipes.story: The palm butter is the sauce, not a garnish. Brown the chicken first, then let it braise gently until the sauce turns glossy.
recipes.occasion: Celebrations, family gatherings, Sunday lunch
recipes.best_served_with: Chikwangue, white rice, fried plantain, saka-saka, or greens
recipes.image_alt: Poulet à la Muambé recipe from Republic of Congo on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Brown the chicken
steps.instruction: Season chicken with salt and pepper. Heat a large pot with a little oil and brown chicken pieces on all sides until golden. Remove and set aside.

steps.title: Sauté the aromatics
steps.instruction: In the same pot, cook the onion until soft. Add garlic and chilli flakes, stirring for 1 minute. Pour in the blended tomatoes and cook until reduced by half.

steps.title: Add palm butter
steps.instruction: Stir the palm butter into the tomato base until fully incorporated. Add bay leaves and 1 cup of water. Bring to a gentle simmer.
steps.tip: Palm butter can be thick — stir vigorously to avoid lumps.

steps.title: Braise the chicken
steps.instruction: Return the chicken pieces to the pot. Cover and cook on low heat for 35 minutes, turning the chicken halfway through.

steps.title: Add okra and finish
steps.instruction: Add the sliced okra and cook for another 10 minutes until tender. Remove bay leaves, taste for salt, and serve hot with chikwangue or rice.
steps.tip: Okra adds natural thickness to the sauce.

recipes.name: Calulu
recipes.description: São Toméan calulu, dried or smoked fish slowly stewed with okra, tomatoes, eggplant, greens, garlic, chile, and palm oil.
recipes.story: Calulu is a layered pot. Let the fish, okra, greens, and palm oil settle into each other before stirring from the bottom.
recipes.occasion: National celebrations and Sunday lunch
recipes.best_served_with: Angú de banana, mashed breadfruit, rice, or boiled plantain
recipes.image_alt: Calulu recipe from São Tomé and Príncipe on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

ingredients.name: water or fish soaking liquid
ingredients.prep_note: use only if not too salty

steps.title: Prepare the fish
steps.instruction: Soak smoked and dried fish separately in warm water for 30 minutes. Debone and break into large chunks.

steps.title: Layer the stew
steps.instruction: In a heavy pot, layer palm oil, onions, tomatoes, eggplant, okra, and both types of fish. Season each layer with salt and pepper.
steps.tip: Layering builds complex flavours — do not stir at this stage.

steps.title: Slow cook
steps.instruction: Cover tightly. Cook on the lowest heat for 45 minutes, shaking the pot occasionally but not stirring.
steps.tip: The stew cooks in its own juices — do not add water.

steps.title: Check and stir
steps.instruction: After 45 minutes, gently stir from the bottom. The okra should have thickened the sauce. Simmer 10 more minutes.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve over mashed breadfruit or plantain (angú).

recipes.name: Sao Tomean Hot Chocolate
recipes.description: São Tomé cacao hot chocolate made with dark chocolate or cacao mass, milk or water, cinnamon, vanilla, sugar, and a pinch of salt.
recipes.story: Let the cacao lead. Use enough liquid to make it drinkable, but simmer and whisk so it stays thick and glossy.
recipes.occasion: Morning drink and dessert
recipes.best_served_with: Banana fritters, coconut cake, roasted nuts, or fresh fruit
recipes.image_alt: Sao Tomean Hot Chocolate drink from São Tomé and Príncipe on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

ingredients.name: São Tomé dark chocolate, cacao mass, or cocoa powder
whole milk, oat milk, or water

steps.title: Heat liquid
steps.instruction: Warm milk or water with cinnamon until steaming but not boiling.

steps.title: Melt cacao
steps.instruction: Add chocolate or cacao and whisk until completely smooth.

steps.title: Sweeten
steps.instruction: Add sugar, vanilla if using, and a pinch of salt. Simmer gently until slightly thickened.

steps.title: Whisk and serve
steps.instruction: Remove cinnamon, whisk until glossy and foamy, and serve hot.

collections.name: Quick & Easy
collections.description: Delicious African dishes ready in 30 minutes or less. Perfect for busy weeknight dinners.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Thieboudienne
recipes.description: Senegalese thieboudienne, fish stuffed with rof and served over tomato rice cooked with cabbage, cassava, carrot, eggplant, okra, tamarind, and dried fish.
recipes.story: The rice is the prize. Once the fish and vegetables have flavored the broth, cook the rice in that same pot so every grain carries the sea and tomato.
recipes.occasion: Sunday family lunch
recipes.best_served_with: Lime wedges, bissap, chile paste, or extra vegetables from the pot
recipes.image_alt: Thieboudienne recipe from Senegal on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

ingredients.name: whole grouper, sea bass, or firm fish

steps.title: Stuff the fish
steps.instruction: Blend parsley, garlic, scotch bonnet, and lime juice to make the rof. Fill the scored slits with this paste.
steps.tip: Pack the rof tightly into the fish cuts.

steps.title: Fry the fish
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a large pot. Fry stuffed fish on both sides until browned. Remove and set aside.

steps.title: Build the sauce
steps.instruction: Fry onions until soft. Add tomato paste and cook 5 minutes. Add water, tamarind, and dried fish for depth.

steps.title: Cook the vegetables
steps.instruction: Add cassava, eggplant, and cabbage. Simmer until tender, about 20 minutes. Remove and set aside.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.instruction: Add washed rice to remaining sauce with enough water. Cover tightly and cook on low 25-30 minutes.

steps.title: Assemble and serve
steps.instruction: Mound rice on a large platter. Place fish in centre, surround with vegetables. Serve with lime wedges.

collections.name: Sunday Specials
collections.description: Elaborate weekend dishes worth the extra effort. These are the recipes that make Sunday lunch special.

collections.name: One-Pot Wonders
collections.description: Hearty single-pot dishes that are easy to make and full of flavor. Less cleanup, more taste.

recipes.name: Cassava Leaf Stew
recipes.description: Sierra Leonean cassava leaf stew with palm oil, beef or chicken, smoked fish, groundnut, onion, garlic, and chile.
recipes.story: Cassava leaves need time. The stew is ready when the greens are tender, the fish has seasoned the pot, and red oil rises gently at the edges.
recipes.occasion: Everyday meals and celebrations
recipes.best_served_with: White rice, plantain, fufu, or gari
recipes.image_alt: Cassava Leaf Stew recipe from Sierra Leone on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Cook meat
steps.instruction: Season meat with onion, salt, and pepper. Simmer until tender and reserve the stock.

steps.title: Start leaves
steps.instruction: Simmer cassava leaves in a little water until they soften and lose their raw edge.

steps.title: Build stew
steps.instruction: Heat palm oil and cook onion, garlic, chile, and ogiri if using. Add leaves, meat, smoked fish, and stock.

steps.title: Add groundnut
steps.instruction: Stir in ground peanuts or peanut butter and simmer until the stew thickens and oil rises.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Adjust salt and serve over white rice.

recipes.name: Bobotie
recipes.description: South African bobotie, curried mince with soaked bread, chutney, sultanas, bay leaves, and a golden egg custard topping.
recipes.story: Bobotie is all balance: curry warmth, fruit sweetness, a little acidity, and a custard top that sets softly over the mince.
recipes.occasion: Family dinner
recipes.best_served_with: Yellow rice, sambals, chutney, banana slices, or salad
recipes.image_alt: Bobotie recipe from South Africa on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Soak the bread
steps.instruction: Tear the bread slices into pieces and soak in 100ml milk for 10 minutes. Squeeze out excess milk and set aside, reserving the milk.

steps.title: Cook the meat filling
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a large pan over medium heat. Fry the onions until golden, about 5 minutes. Add curry powder and turmeric, stirring for 1 minute. Add the mince and cook until browned, breaking it up with a wooden spoon.

steps.title: Add flavor
steps.instruction: Stir in the soaked bread, chutney, sultanas, salt, and pepper. Mix well and cook for another 3 minutes until everything is combined. Remove from heat.
steps.tip: The soaked bread helps bind the mixture and keeps it moist during baking.

steps.title: Assemble the dish
steps.instruction: Preheat oven to 180°C. Spoon the meat mixture into a greased oven-safe dish and press down evenly. Tuck bay leaves into the top of the meat.

steps.title: Make the custard
steps.instruction: Whisk together the eggs and remaining milk with a pinch of turmeric. Pour evenly over the meat filling.
steps.tip: Adding a pinch of turmeric to the custard gives it a beautiful golden color.

steps.title: Bake
steps.instruction: Bake in the preheated oven for 35-40 minutes until the custard is set and golden brown on top.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Let the bobotie rest for 5 minutes, then serve with yellow rice, sambals, and a side salad.
steps.tip: Traditional accompaniments include banana slices, coconut flakes, and chutney as sambals.

recipes.name: Bunny Chow
recipes.description: Durban bunny chow, a hollowed loaf filled with lamb, chicken, bean, or vegetable curry and served with the bread lid for scooping.
recipes.story: The bread is the bowl and the utensil. The curry should be thick enough to soak the walls without collapsing the loaf.
recipes.occasion: Lunch
recipes.best_served_with: Carrot sambal, chopped onion, cucumber salad, achar, or extra gravy
recipes.image_alt: Bunny Chow recipe from South Africa on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

ingredients.name: lamb, chicken, beef, beans, or vegetables
unsliced white bread
ingredients.prep_note: cut into quarters or halves and hollowed

steps.title: Brown the meat
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a heavy pot over high heat. Season lamb with salt and brown in batches until deep golden on all sides. Remove and set aside.
steps.tip: Do not overcrowd the pot — browning in batches ensures a good sear.

steps.title: Cook the curry base
steps.instruction: Reduce heat to medium. Add onions and cook until golden brown, about 8 minutes. Add curry powder, garam masala, and curry leaves, stirring for 2 minutes until fragrant.

steps.title: Simmer the curry
steps.instruction: Add tomatoes and return the lamb to the pot. Add enough water to just cover the meat. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a low simmer. Cover and cook for 40 minutes.

steps.title: Add potatoes
steps.instruction: Add the potato quarters to the curry. Continue simmering for another 20 minutes until the potatoes are tender and the sauce has thickened.
steps.tip: The potatoes should be soft enough to break apart slightly, thickening the curry.

steps.title: Prepare the bread
steps.instruction: Cut the bread loaf in half. Hollow out each half, leaving about 2cm of bread on the sides and bottom. Keep the scooped-out bread for dipping.

steps.title: Assemble and serve
steps.instruction: Ladle the hot curry into the hollowed-out bread. Place the bread lid on top. Serve immediately, eating with your hands — tear pieces of bread and scoop up the curry.
steps.tip: Authentic bunny chow is always eaten with your hands, never cutlery.

recipes.name: Ful Medames
recipes.description: Sudanese ful medames, slow-cooked fava beans mashed with cumin, lemon, garlic, onion, chile, and sesame oil.
recipes.story: Ful should be creamy but never lifeless. Keep a little texture in the beans and let the sesame oil finish the bowl.
recipes.occasion: Breakfast, any day
recipes.best_served_with: Warm bread, boiled eggs, salad, feta, pickles, or extra lemon
recipes.image_alt: Ful Medames recipe from Sudan on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Cook the beans
steps.instruction: Drain soaked fava beans, place in a large pot, and cover with fresh water by 5cm. Bring to a boil, reduce to a gentle simmer, cover, and cook for about 2 hours until very soft and breaking apart.
steps.tip: The longer and slower you cook, the creamier the result. Some Sudanese cooks simmer overnight on the lowest heat.

steps.title: Mash and season
steps.instruction: Drain most of the water, reserving some. Mash the beans roughly with a fork or potato masher — leave some texture. Stir in the cumin, salt, and half the lemon juice.
steps.tip: Sudanese ful is mashed, not pureed — you want chunky bits.

steps.title: Garnish and serve
steps.instruction: Spoon into shallow bowls. Drizzle generously with sesame oil and remaining lemon juice. Top with diced onion, minced garlic, and sliced chilli. Serve immediately with warm bread.
steps.tip: In Sudan, the sesame oil is key — it gives ful its distinctive nutty flavour that sets it apart from Egyptian versions.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

collections.name: Street Food
collections.description: Popular street snacks and quick bites found at markets and roadside stalls across Africa.

recipes.name: Koklo Meme
recipes.description: Togolese koklo meme, charcoal-grilled chicken marinated with ginger, garlic, chile, lime, oil, salt, and pepper.
recipes.story: The marinade should get under the skin and into the cuts. Let the chicken sit, then grill until the edges char.
recipes.occasion: Street food and gatherings
recipes.best_served_with: Akoumé, rice, salad, piment sauce, or fried plantains
recipes.image_alt: Koklo Meme recipe from Togo on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make the marinade
steps.instruction: Blend ginger, garlic, scotch bonnets, lime juice, oil, salt, and pepper into a smooth paste.

steps.title: Marinate the chicken
steps.instruction: Rub marinade all over the chicken, under the skin and into every crevice. Refrigerate at least 2 hours.
steps.tip: Overnight gives the most flavour.

steps.title: Grill the chicken
steps.instruction: Grill over medium charcoal heat, turning regularly, for 30-35 minutes until skin is crispy and charred and juices run clear.
steps.tip: Baste with remaining marinade while grilling.

steps.title: Rest and serve
steps.instruction: Rest for 5 minutes, then cut into pieces. Serve with piment sauce and sides.

collections.name: Street Food
collections.description: Popular street snacks and quick bites found at markets and roadside stalls across Africa.

recipes.name: Lablabi
recipes.description: Tunisian lablabi, garlic-cumin chickpea broth ladled over torn bread with harissa, olive oil, lemon, capers, and optional egg or tuna.
recipes.story: The bowl is assembled, not just served. Bread first, hot chickpeas next, then each person tunes the heat, oil, and lemon.
recipes.occasion: Breakfast, cold winter mornings
recipes.best_served_with: Harissa, lemon, capers, tuna, soft egg, olives, or extra olive oil
recipes.image_alt: Lablabi recipe from Tunisia on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Cook the chickpeas
steps.instruction: Drain the soaked chickpeas and place in a large pot. Cover with fresh water, add 1 tsp baking soda, and bring to a boil. Reduce heat and simmer for 40 minutes until very tender.
steps.tip: The baking soda helps the chickpeas get extra soft and creamy.

steps.title: Season the broth
steps.instruction: Stir in the crushed garlic, harissa, cumin, salt, and pepper into the chickpea broth. Simmer for another 5 minutes to let the flavours infuse.
steps.tip: Adjust the harissa to your heat preference — Tunisians like it fiery.

steps.title: Prepare the bowls
steps.instruction: Place a generous handful of torn bread pieces in each serving bowl.
steps.tip: Day-old or stale bread works best as it absorbs the broth without disintegrating.

steps.title: Assemble and serve
steps.instruction: Ladle the hot chickpea broth and chickpeas over the bread. Drizzle generously with olive oil, squeeze lemon juice over the top, and scatter capers if using. Serve immediately.
steps.tip: Some like to crack a raw egg into the hot broth — the heat gently cooks it.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

collections.name: Street Food
collections.description: Popular street snacks and quick bites found at markets and roadside stalls across Africa.

recipes.name: Sahrawi Camel Meat Stew
recipes.description: Sahrawi-style camel meat stew with onions, garlic, tomatoes, potatoes, cumin, coriander, and slow desert cooking.
recipes.story: Camel needs patience. Brown it first, then let the pot simmer until the meat relaxes into the tomato-onion sauce.
recipes.occasion: Gatherings, celebrations
recipes.best_served_with: Flatbread, couscous, dates, or Sahrawi mint tea
recipes.image_alt: Sahrawi Camel Meat Stew recipe from Western Sahara on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Brown the meat
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a heavy pot over medium-high heat. Season the camel meat with salt, cumin, and coriander. Brown on all sides in batches, about 8 minutes total. Set aside.
steps.tip: Camel meat is lean — do not overcrowd the pot or it will steam instead of searing.

steps.title: Cook the onions
steps.instruction: In the same pot, add the sliced onions and cook over medium heat until deeply caramelised, about 12 minutes.
steps.tip: The caramelised onions provide the sweetness that balances the dish.

steps.title: Build the stew
steps.instruction: Return the meat to the pot. Add the chopped tomatoes, dried chilli, and enough water to just cover. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a very low simmer. Cover and cook for 90 minutes.
steps.tip: Low and slow is essential — camel meat becomes wonderfully tender with patience.

steps.title: Add potatoes
steps.instruction: Add the potatoes and continue cooking for another 25 minutes until the potatoes are tender and the sauce has thickened.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Taste and adjust seasoning. Serve in a communal bowl with flatbread for scooping, alongside strong, sweet Sahrawi mint tea.
steps.tip: In Sahrawi tradition, this is eaten communally from a shared plate.

recipes.name: Sahrawi Mint Tea
recipes.description: Sahrawi mint tea, strong gunpowder green tea with fresh mint and sugar, poured from height and served in three rounds.
recipes.story: Foam matters. Pour high, return the tea to the pot, and repeat until the glasses carry a pale frothy crown.
recipes.occasion: Daily hospitality, every gathering
recipes.best_served_with: Dates, nuts, flatbread, camel stew, or couscous
recipes.image_alt: Sahrawi Mint Tea drink from Western Sahara on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Rinse the tea
