steps.instruction: Place the green tea in a small teapot. Add a splash of boiling water, swirl, and discard. This removes the bitterness and dust from the leaves.
steps.tip: This rinse is essential — never skip it.

steps.title: First brew (strong)
steps.instruction: Add the boiling water and a third of the sugar to the teapot. Let it steep for 5 minutes. Add some mint leaves and pour from a height into small glasses, then pour back into the pot. Repeat the pouring 3-4 times to create foam.
steps.tip: The higher you pour, the better the foam. This takes practice.

steps.title: Serve first round
steps.instruction: Pour the first round into the glasses from height, creating a frothy top. This round is the strongest and most bitter. Serve and drink.
steps.tip: The first glass is said to be bitter like life.

steps.title: Second and third rounds
steps.instruction: Add more water, sugar, and fresh mint for the second round. Steep and pour the same way. Repeat for the third round with even more sugar and mint. Each round gets sweeter and milder.
steps.tip: Always accept all three rounds — it is a sign of respect and friendship.

steps.title: Present with care
steps.instruction: Serve each glass on a small tray. The tea master always tastes first to ensure quality before serving guests.
steps.tip: In Sahrawi culture, the youngest person present usually prepares the tea as a sign of respect to elders.

collections.name: Quick & Easy
collections.description: Delicious African dishes ready in 30 minutes or less. Perfect for busy weeknight dinners.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Dovi
recipes.description: Zimbabwean dovi, chicken stewed with peanut butter, tomatoes, onion, garlic, ginger, pepper, and pumpkin leaves or spinach.
recipes.story: Dovi is gentle comfort, but the peanut must cook through. Simmer until the sauce is creamy and no longer raw-tasting.
recipes.occasion: Family dinner
recipes.best_served_with: Sadza, rice, muriwo, roasted vegetables, or fresh tomato salad
recipes.image_alt: Dovi recipe from Zimbabwe on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Brown the chicken
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a large pot over medium-high heat. Season chicken pieces with salt and brown on all sides until golden. Remove and set aside.

steps.title: Cook onions and tomatoes
steps.instruction: In the same pot, fry the onions until softened. Add the tomatoes and cook until they break down into a thick sauce, about 8 minutes.

steps.title: Dissolve the peanut butter
steps.instruction: Mix the peanut butter with the water until smooth, ensuring there are no lumps. Pour this mixture into the pot with the tomato sauce and stir well.
steps.tip: Mixing the peanut butter with water separately first prevents clumping in the stew.

steps.title: Simmer with chicken
steps.instruction: Return the chicken to the pot. Bring to a gentle boil, then reduce heat to low. Cover and simmer for 35-40 minutes until the chicken is cooked through and the sauce is thick and creamy.
steps.tip: Stir occasionally to prevent the peanut butter from sticking to the bottom.

steps.title: Add greens
steps.instruction: If using, add the pumpkin leaves or spinach in the last 10 minutes of cooking. Stir them into the stew and cook until wilted.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Adjust seasoning with salt. Serve hot with sadza, scooping up the thick peanut gravy with each bite.
steps.tip: The stew thickens as it cools — add a splash of water when reheating leftovers.

recipes.name: Sadza neNyama
recipes.description: Zimbabwean sadza ne nyama, thick maize sadza served with slow-cooked beef, tomato, onion, and garlic gravy.
recipes.story: Sadza is plain by design, so the nyama must carry the sauce. Cook the beef until tender before you start the maize pot.
recipes.occasion: Everyday family meal
recipes.best_served_with: Muriwo, covo, tomato relish, beans, or avocado
recipes.image_alt: Sadza neNyama recipe from Zimbabwe on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Brown the beef
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a heavy pot over high heat. Brown the beef cubes in batches until well-seared. Remove and set aside.

steps.title: Cook the stew base
steps.instruction: In the same pot, fry the onions until golden. Add the tomatoes and cook until they form a thick sauce, about 8 minutes.

steps.title: Simmer the stew
steps.instruction: Return the beef to the pot. Add water and salt. Bring to a boil, then reduce heat and simmer covered for 45 minutes until the beef is very tender and the gravy has thickened.
steps.tip: The stew should be saucy but not watery — the gravy is meant to soak into the sadza.

steps.title: Start the sadza
steps.instruction: In a separate large pot, bring the water to a boil. Mix a cup of maize meal with cold water to form a thin paste. Pour this paste into the boiling water, stirring constantly. Cook for 5 minutes, stirring.
steps.tip: This first step creates a smooth base that prevents lumps.

steps.title: Thicken the sadza
steps.instruction: Gradually add the remaining maize meal, stirring vigorously with a wooden spoon after each addition. The sadza is ready when it is very thick and pulls away cleanly from the pot.
steps.tip: Use a strong wooden spoon — a flat paddle called a mugoti works best.

steps.title: Shape and serve
steps.instruction: Wet a bowl and scoop sadza into it, pressing to form a smooth dome. Invert onto a plate. Serve alongside the beef stew and greens.
steps.tip: Pinch off a small piece of sadza, flatten it in your palm, and use it to scoop up the stew.

recipes.name: Chakhchoukha
recipes.description: A rustic Algerian dish of hand-torn flatbread soaked in a rich, spiced tomato and lamb stew with chickpeas. A beloved comfort food from the eastern highlands.
recipes.story: Chakhchoukha hails from the Aures mountains and the high plateaux of eastern Algeria, particularly the cities of Biskra and Batna. It is the pride of the Chaoui Berber people. The dish gets its name from the sound of tearing the rougag flatbread into small pieces. Families prepare it for religious holidays, particularly Eid and Mawlid, gathering around a communal platter.
recipes.occasion: Eid, Mawlid, family gatherings
recipes.best_served_with: Buttermilk (lben)
recipes.image_alt: Chakhchoukha recipe from Algeria on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make the rougag
steps.instruction: Mix semolina with salt and enough water to form a smooth dough. Roll very thin and cook on a dry griddle until lightly spotted. Tear into small bite-sized pieces and set aside.
steps.tip: The flatbread should be paper-thin for the best texture.

steps.title: Start the stew
steps.instruction: In a large pot, heat oil and brown the lamb pieces on all sides. Add onions and cook until softened, about 5 minutes.

steps.title: Build the sauce
steps.instruction: Add tomato paste, ras el hanout, chilli flakes, salt, and pepper. Stir well. Add the chickpeas and enough water to cover. Bring to a boil, then reduce heat, cover, and simmer for 60 minutes until the lamb is very tender.
steps.tip: The sauce should be thick and rich — reduce uncovered for the last 10 minutes if needed.

steps.title: Assemble
steps.instruction: Place the torn flatbread pieces in a large serving dish. Ladle the hot stew and sauce over the bread, ensuring every piece is well soaked.
steps.tip: Let the bread absorb the sauce for a few minutes before serving.

steps.title: Steam and serve
steps.instruction: Cover the dish and let it sit for 5 minutes so the bread absorbs the broth. Serve on a communal platter with the meat arranged on top.
steps.tip: Traditionally eaten with spoons from a shared platter.

recipes.name: Chorba Frik
recipes.description: Algerian tomato, lamb, chickpea, herb, and cracked green wheat soup traditionally served to open Ramadan meals.
recipes.story: Chorba frik is the Algerian bowl many families wait for at iftar: lamb, tomato, chickpeas, cilantro, mint, and smoky cracked green wheat cooked into a soup that is hearty but still bright.
recipes.occasion: Best for Ramadan iftar, cold evenings, and first-course soup service.
recipes.best_served_with: Lemon wedges, bourek, dates, and mint tea.
recipes.regional_variations: Some homes use chicken, others lamb or beef. The frik can be coarse or fine, and the herb balance shifts by region.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Brown the meat
steps.instruction: Cook lamb, onion, and celery in oil until the meat loses its raw color and the onion softens.

steps.title: Make the red broth
steps.instruction: Add tomatoes, tomato paste, paprika, pepper, salt, chickpeas, and water, then simmer until the lamb is tender.

steps.title: Add frik
steps.instruction: Stir in rinsed frik and simmer gently, stirring often so the cracked wheat does not catch.

steps.title: Finish fresh
steps.instruction: Fold in cilantro and mint, rest briefly, and serve with lemon.

recipes.name: Dobara Biskra
recipes.description: Biskra-style chickpea and fava bean stew dressed with garlic, cumin, harissa, lemon, olive oil, and cilantro.
recipes.story: Dobara is the fiery bean bowl associated with Biskra, where tender chickpeas and fava beans are seasoned at the end with raw garlic, cumin, lemon, chili, oil, and herbs. It is cheap, filling, and built for bread.
recipes.occasion: Best for street-food lunches, winter bowls, and simple vegan meals.
recipes.best_served_with: Khobz, extra harissa, preserved lemon, or chopped fresh onion.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions use only chickpeas, while Biskra-style bowls often mix chickpeas and fava beans with a generous cumin and chili finish.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Cook the beans
steps.instruction: Simmer soaked chickpeas and fava beans in water until very tender.

steps.title: Thicken the broth
steps.instruction: Mash a cup of beans against the pot and stir them back in to make the stew creamy.

steps.title: Season boldly
steps.instruction: Stir in garlic, harissa, cumin, olive oil, lemon juice, and salt while the beans are hot.

steps.title: Rest and serve
steps.instruction: Let the stew stand briefly, then finish with cilantro and more chili if desired.

recipes.name: Makroud
recipes.description: Diamond-shaped semolina pastries filled with fragrant date paste and deep-fried until golden, then dipped in warm honey syrup. A treasured Algerian sweet.
recipes.story: Makroud is the jewel of Algerian patisserie, particularly associated with the city of Kairouan and the southern oasis towns. Every household prepares trays of makroud for Eid celebrations, weddings, and to welcome guests. The art lies in achieving the perfect ratio of crispy semolina shell to soft, spiced date filling, all glistening with honey.
recipes.occasion: Eid, weddings, celebrations
recipes.best_served_with: Mint tea or Arabic coffee
recipes.image_alt: Makroud dessert from Algeria on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make the date filling
steps.instruction: Process the dates with cinnamon and orange blossom water until you have a smooth, thick paste. Roll into long logs about 1.5cm in diameter. Set aside.
steps.tip: If dates are dry, add a tablespoon of warm water while processing.

steps.title: Prepare the dough
steps.instruction: Combine semolina with melted butter, orange blossom water, a pinch of salt, and enough warm water to form a firm but pliable dough. Knead for 5 minutes.
steps.tip: The dough should hold together without being sticky.

steps.title: Shape the makroud
steps.instruction: Divide dough into portions. Roll each into a log and make a groove down the centre. Place a date paste log inside and seal the dough around it. Flatten slightly and cut into diamond shapes.
steps.tip: Use a fork to press a decorative pattern on each piece.

steps.title: Fry until golden
steps.instruction: Heat oil to 170C (340F). Fry the makroud in batches for 3-4 minutes, turning once, until deep golden brown. Drain on paper towels.
steps.tip: Do not overcrowd the pan — fry in small batches for even cooking.

steps.title: Dip in honey
steps.instruction: Warm the honey gently. Dip each fried makroud into the warm honey, turning to coat all sides. Place on a rack to let excess honey drip off.
steps.tip: Warm honey coats more evenly than cold. Let them cool completely before storing.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Arrange on a platter and allow to cool completely. Makroud keep well in an airtight container for up to a week.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

collections.name: Sunday Specials
collections.description: Elaborate weekend dishes worth the extra effort. These are the recipes that make Sunday lunch special.

recipes.name: Mhadjeb
recipes.description: Algerian semolina flatbreads folded around a spicy tomato, onion, and pepper filling.
recipes.story: Mhadjeb, also called mahdjouba, is the Algerian griddle bread that turns a few pantry ingredients into a complete hand-held meal. Thin semolina dough is stretched, filled, folded, and cooked until blistered.
recipes.occasion: Best for breakfast, street food plates, school snacks, and tea-time meals.
recipes.best_served_with: Harissa, mint tea, olives, or a simple cucumber tomato salad.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks keep the filling strictly tomato and onion, while others add peppers, garlic, minced meat, or cheese.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Knead the dough
steps.instruction: Mix semolina, flour, salt, and water into a soft dough, then knead until smooth and elastic.

steps.title: Rest in oil
steps.instruction: Divide into balls, coat with oil, cover, and rest until the dough relaxes.

steps.title: Cook the filling
steps.instruction: Cook onions, peppers, tomatoes, garlic, harissa, paprika, and salt until thick and almost dry.

steps.title: Fill and griddle
steps.instruction: Stretch each dough ball thin, add filling, fold into a square, and cook on a hot griddle until spotted.

recipes.name: Rechta Algeroise
recipes.description: Algerian hand-cut noodles served with chicken, turnips, chickpeas, cinnamon, and a pale onion broth.
recipes.story: Rechta is one of Algiers' ceremonial noodle dishes, prized for its fine strands, white sauce, chickpeas, turnips, and gentle spice. It is usually cooked for family gatherings, Eid tables, weddings, and long Sunday meals.
recipes.occasion: Best for Eid, family Sundays, and winter lunches.
recipes.best_served_with: Extra broth, lemon, harissa, or a crisp cucumber salad.
recipes.regional_variations: Algiers versions keep the sauce pale with onion, cinnamon, chickpeas, and turnips, while some households add courgette, potato, or meatballs.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Build the white sauce
steps.instruction: Brown chicken lightly in oil and smen, then add onions, cinnamon, pepper, salt, and enough water to cover.

steps.title: Simmer the chicken
steps.instruction: Add chickpeas and cook until the chicken is nearly tender and the broth tastes sweet from the onions.

steps.title: Steam the noodles
steps.instruction: Rub noodles with a little oil and steam over the broth. Fluff, moisten with broth, and steam again.

steps.title: Finish with turnips
steps.instruction: Add turnips to the broth and simmer until tender, then serve noodles soaked with broth and topped with chicken.

recipes.name: Tajine Zitoun
recipes.description: Algerian chicken and green olive stew with carrots, onion, lemon, cinnamon, and a lightly thickened sauce.
recipes.story: Tajine zitoun is Algeria's olive-stew showpiece: chicken simmered with onion, carrots, spices, and briny green olives until the sauce turns glossy and balanced. It is elegant enough for guests but practical enough for family lunch.
recipes.occasion: Best for guest lunches, Eid tables, and weekend chicken dinners.
recipes.best_served_with: Khobz, rice, couscous, or boiled potatoes.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions include mushrooms, meatballs, preserved lemon, or a flour and lemon liaison to finish the sauce.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Brown the chicken
steps.instruction: Brown chicken with oil, onion, garlic, cinnamon, turmeric, salt, and pepper.

steps.title: Simmer
steps.instruction: Add water to halfway cover, then simmer until the chicken is tender.

steps.title: Add carrots and olives
steps.instruction: Add carrots and blanched olives and cook until the carrots are tender and the sauce is savory.

steps.title: Gloss the sauce
steps.instruction: Stir in lemon juice and a little cornstarch slurry, then simmer until lightly thickened.

recipes.name: Calulu de Peixe
recipes.description: Angolan fish calulu with fresh fish, dried fish, okra, eggplant, tomato, greens, and palm oil layered into a deep, savory stew.
recipes.story: Calulu de Peixe is a layered Angolan fish stew where fresh fish and dried fish season the pot together. Okra, eggplant, greens, tomato, and palm oil bring the thick, coastal-meets-inland character.
recipes.occasion: Best for a slow lunch, family supper, or a table where funge needs a serious sauce.
recipes.best_served_with: Funge de bombo, rice, boiled cassava, or plantain.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions use sweet potato leaves or spinach, some add zucchini or sweet potato, and the balance of fresh to dried fish changes by cook.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Soak the dried fish
steps.instruction: Soak the dried fish in warm water, then rinse and break into large pieces.
steps.tip: Taste a small piece after soaking. If it is still very salty, rinse again.

steps.title: Layer the pot
steps.instruction: In a heavy pot, layer onions, tomatoes, garlic, eggplant, dried fish, fresh fish, greens, okra, palm oil, chile, and water.

steps.title: Steam and simmer
steps.instruction: Cover and cook over medium-low heat until the vegetables release liquid and the fish is cooked through.
steps.tip: Shake the pot gently instead of stirring hard.

steps.title: Thicken the sauce
steps.instruction: Uncover and simmer until the okra and eggplant thicken the sauce. Adjust seasoning and serve hot.

recipes.name: Funge de Bombo
recipes.description: A smooth Angolan cassava swallow made by stirring cassava flour into hot water until thick, stretchy, and ready for stew.
recipes.story: Funge de Bombo is the Angolan cassava flour swallow that turns a stew into a meal. It is simple, but the technique matters: steady heat, strong stirring, and enough water to make it smooth rather than stiff and grainy.
recipes.occasion: Best with saucy stews, grilled fish, beans, or chicken muamba.
recipes.best_served_with: Muamba de Galinha, calulu de peixe, beans, or grilled fish.
recipes.regional_variations: Cassava flour funge is especially common in northern Angola, while cornmeal versions are also cooked in other regions.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

ingredients.name: water
ingredients.prep_note: divided, plus hot water if needed

steps.title: Make a slurry
steps.instruction: Mix the cassava flour with 1 cup cold water until smooth and pourable.
steps.tip: The slurry method helps prevent lumps.

steps.title: Heat the water
steps.instruction: Bring the remaining 3 cups water and salt to a boil in a sturdy pot, then lower to medium heat.

steps.title: Cook and beat
steps.instruction: Pour in the slurry while stirring constantly with a wooden spoon. Keep stirring and folding until the mixture thickens, turns smooth, and pulls from the sides.
steps.tip: If it gets too stiff before it is smooth, splash in hot water and keep beating.

steps.title: Shape and serve
steps.instruction: Wet a spoon or small bowl, shape the funge into mounds, and serve hot beside stew.

recipes.name: Muamba de Galinha
recipes.description: Angolan chicken stewed with red palm oil, okra, pumpkin, tomatoes, onion, garlic, and chile until the sauce turns rich and silky.
recipes.story: Muamba de Galinha is one of Angola's best-known chicken dishes. The heart of the pot is red palm oil, okra, and chicken juices, with pumpkin or squash softening into the sauce and funge waiting beside it.
recipes.occasion: Best for a family weekend meal, celebration table, or any day when chicken stew should feel generous.
recipes.best_served_with: Funge de bombo, white rice, or boiled cassava.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks use palm nut sauce instead of straight red palm oil. Pumpkin, squash, and okra amounts shift by household, but peanuts belong more naturally to muamba de ginguba than this chicken version.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Season the chicken
steps.instruction: Rub the chicken with lemon juice, garlic, salt, and pepper. Let it sit while you prep the vegetables.
steps.tip: A short rest is enough, but 1 hour in the fridge gives a fuller flavor.

steps.title: Brown and build the base
steps.instruction: Warm the palm oil in a heavy pot over medium heat. Brown the chicken in batches, then add onions and tomatoes and cook until the tomatoes soften.
steps.tip: Do not scorch the palm oil. If it smokes hard, lower the heat.

steps.title: Simmer the stew
steps.instruction: Return all chicken to the pot. Add stock, pumpkin, and the whole chile. Cover and simmer gently until the chicken is nearly tender.
steps.tip: The pumpkin should soften into the sauce without disappearing completely.

steps.title: Finish with okra
steps.instruction: Add okra and simmer uncovered until the chicken is tender and the sauce is glossy. Adjust salt and pepper before serving.
steps.tip: Add the okra late so it keeps shape and gives the sauce body.

recipes.name: Atassi Watche
recipes.description: Beninese rice and beans cooked tender and served with a concentrated tomato dja sauce, eggs, fish, or wagasi.
recipes.story: Atassi in southern Benin and Watche in the north is one of the country's everyday anchors: rice and beans cooked together, then lifted by dja, pepper, fish, egg, cheese, or meat.
recipes.occasion: Best for lunch plates, market-style meals, and meal prep.
recipes.best_served_with: Dja tomato sauce, fried fish, boiled eggs, wagasi cheese, or chili.
recipes.regional_variations: Northern versions may use potash and a firmer bean texture, while southern plates often lean into oily tomato dja and fried accompaniments.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Cook the beans
steps.instruction: Simmer soaked beans in water until just tender but not falling apart.

steps.title: Add the rice
steps.instruction: Stir in rinsed rice and salt, then cook uncovered until the rice is tender and the beans hold their shape.

steps.title: Build the dja
steps.instruction: Cook onions in oil, add tomatoes, tomato paste, dried shrimp, ginger, and garlic, then simmer into a thick red sauce.

steps.title: Serve market-style
steps.instruction: Spoon rice and beans onto plates and top with dja, fish, egg, wagasi, or chili.

recipes.name: Dakouin
recipes.description: Mono-region Beninese fish, tomato, and gari dish where cassava granules thicken a deeply seasoned fish broth.
recipes.story: Dakouin is practical coastal cooking: fresh fish becomes broth, tomatoes give body, and gari turns the pot into a spoonable cassava meal.
recipes.occasion: Best for fresh-fish days, family lunch, and rainy-season comfort.
recipes.best_served_with: Extra moyo, sliced onion, or a small pepper sauce.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions use tilapia, others use the day's catch, and the gari can be loose like couscous or thick like a soft mash.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Start the broth
steps.instruction: Simmer tomatoes, onion, garlic, pepper, oil, and water until the tomatoes collapse.

steps.title: Cook the fish
steps.instruction: Add fish pieces and simmer gently until cooked through.

steps.title: Fold in gari
steps.instruction: Lower the heat and sprinkle in gari gradually, stirring until the broth thickens but stays moist.
steps.tip: Stop before it becomes stiff; it firms as it rests.

steps.title: Rest and serve
steps.instruction: Cover for a few minutes, adjust salt, and serve hot with extra sauce.

recipes.name: Grilled Wagasi
recipes.description: Beninese soft white cheese grilled until golden and served with a spicy piment sauce. A unique West African cheese snack with a satisfying chewy texture.
recipes.story: Wagasi is one of the few traditional cheeses in West Africa, made by the Fula (Fulani) herders from fresh cow's milk. Grilled on roadside stalls across Benin, it is an important source of protein and a beloved snack.
recipes.occasion: Street snack
recipes.best_served_with: Piment sauce and sliced onions
recipes.image_alt: Grilled Wagasi recipe from Benin on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Prepare the cheese
steps.instruction: Cut wagasi into slices about 2cm thick. Pat dry with paper towels.

steps.title: Grill the cheese
steps.instruction: Brush with oil and grill on a hot grill pan or barbecue for 3-4 minutes per side until golden brown with grill marks.
steps.tip: Do not move the cheese too early — let it develop a crust.

steps.title: Make piment sauce
steps.instruction: Mix blended scotch bonnets with chopped onion, a squeeze of lime, and salt.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve the grilled wagasi hot with the piment sauce for dipping.

collections.name: Quick & Easy
collections.description: Delicious African dishes ready in 30 minutes or less. Perfect for busy weeknight dinners.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

collections.name: Street Food
collections.description: Popular street snacks and quick bites found at markets and roadside stalls across Africa.

recipes.name: Poisson au Moyo
recipes.description: Beninese grilled fish with a bright raw-style tomato, onion, lemon, herb, and chili moyo sauce.
recipes.story: Moyo is the sauce that makes simple grilled fish feel like a full coastal plate: tomato, onion, chile, lemon, herbs, and enough salt to wake up the fish.
recipes.occasion: Best for weekend grills, beach-style lunches, and quick seafood dinners.
recipes.best_served_with: Atassi, ablo, akassa, fried plantain, or steamed rice.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks keep moyo raw and sharp, while others briefly warm the tomato-onion mixture with oil or smoked fish.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Season the fish
steps.instruction: Rub fish with oil, paprika, salt, and pepper, including the cuts along the sides.

steps.title: Make the moyo
steps.instruction: Mix tomatoes, onion, chilies, lemon juice, herbs, oil, and salt. Let it stand while the fish cooks.

steps.title: Grill the fish
steps.instruction: Grill or broil the fish until the skin chars lightly and the flesh flakes cleanly.

steps.title: Spoon and serve
steps.instruction: Top the fish with moyo and serve immediately while the sauce is fresh.

recipes.name: Morogo
recipes.description: Traditional wild spinach greens cooked with tomatoes, onions, and peanut powder — a nutritious everyday dish that connects Batswana to their foraging heritage.
recipes.story: Morogo refers to a variety of wild leafy greens that have been gathered and eaten across Botswana for centuries. These nutrient-dense greens were traditionally foraged from the wild after the rains, dried for preservation, and cooked throughout the year. Today, morogo remains a staple in Batswana homes, valued for both its nutrition and its deep cultural significance.
recipes.occasion: Everyday meal
recipes.best_served_with: Pap or bogobe and seswaa
recipes.image_alt: Morogo recipe from Botswana on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Sauté onion
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a pot over medium heat. Add the chopped onion and cook until softened, about 3 minutes.

steps.title: Add tomatoes
steps.instruction: Add the diced tomatoes and cook until they break down into a sauce, about 5 minutes, stirring occasionally.

steps.title: Cook the greens
steps.instruction: Add the chopped morogo to the pot. Stir well, cover, and cook for 10 minutes until the greens are tender and wilted, stirring occasionally.
steps.tip: If using regular spinach, reduce the cooking time to 5 minutes as it wilts faster.

steps.title: Add peanut powder
steps.instruction: Sprinkle the peanut powder over the greens and stir thoroughly. Cook for another 3 minutes to allow the peanut flavor to meld with the vegetables. Season with salt.
steps.tip: The peanut powder adds protein and a subtle nutty flavor that is characteristic of this dish.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve the morogo warm as a side dish alongside pap, bogobe, or seswaa.
steps.tip: Morogo can also be dried and stored for months — rehydrate in water before cooking.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Vetkoek with Mince
recipes.description: Golden deep-fried bread rolls with a crispy exterior and soft, fluffy interior, split open and filled with savory spiced mince — a beloved Southern African street food.
recipes.story: Known as magwinya in Botswana and vetkoek in South Africa, these deep-fried dough balls are one of the most popular street foods across Southern Africa. Vendors selling freshly fried magwinya can be found at bus stations, markets, and school gates. They can be filled with savory mince, jam, cheese, or simply eaten plain with butter.
recipes.occasion: Street food or snack
recipes.best_served_with: Atchar (pickle) and chilli sauce
recipes.image_alt: Vetkoek with Mince recipe from Botswana on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make the dough
steps.instruction: Mix flour, yeast, sugar, and salt in a large bowl. Add warm water gradually, mixing to form a soft, slightly sticky dough. Knead for 8 minutes until smooth and elastic.
steps.tip: The dough should be softer than bread dough — slightly sticky is perfect.

steps.title: Let dough rise
steps.instruction: Cover the bowl with a damp cloth and leave in a warm place to rise for 1 hour until doubled in size.

steps.title: Prepare the filling
steps.instruction: While the dough rises, brown the mince with diced onion in a pan over medium-high heat. Season with salt, pepper, and a pinch of curry powder. Cook until done, about 10 minutes. Set aside.

steps.title: Shape the vetkoek
steps.instruction: Punch down the dough and divide into 8 equal portions. Roll each into a smooth ball. Let rest for 10 minutes.

steps.title: Deep fry
steps.instruction: Heat oil to 170°C in a deep pot. Carefully lower 2-3 dough balls at a time into the oil. Fry for 3-4 minutes per side until deep golden brown and cooked through. Drain on paper towels.
steps.tip: Do not let the oil get too hot or the outside will burn before the inside cooks through.

steps.title: Fill and serve
steps.instruction: Slice each vetkoek open along one side. Spoon in the warm mince filling. Serve immediately with chilli sauce or atchar on the side.
steps.tip: For a sweet version, fill with apricot jam or syrup instead of mince.

collections.name: Street Food
collections.description: Popular street snacks and quick bites found at markets and roadside stalls across Africa.

recipes.name: Babenda
recipes.description: A nourishing porridge of millet flour and leafy greens simmered with dawadawa for umami depth. A staple of everyday Burkinabé cooking.
recipes.story: Babenda is the soul food of Burkina Faso, eaten daily by millions. It celebrates the Sahelian pantry of millet, leafy greens, and fermented seasonings. Every family has their own version passed down through generations.
recipes.occasion: Daily meals
recipes.best_served_with: Toh or boiled yam
recipes.image_alt: Babenda recipe from Burkina Faso on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Prepare the greens
steps.instruction: Wash and chop the leafy greens. Boil in salted water for 10 minutes until tender. Drain, reserving the cooking liquid.

steps.title: Make millet paste
steps.instruction: Mix millet flour with 1 cup cold water to form a smooth paste.

steps.title: Cook the base
steps.instruction: In a pot, heat shea butter and fry onion until soft. Add dawadawa and stir. Add 3 cups of the reserved greens liquid and bring to a boil.

steps.title: Combine and cook
steps.instruction: Pour in the millet paste, stirring constantly. Add the cooked greens and stir well. Cook on low heat for 15-20 minutes, stirring frequently, until thick and porridge-like.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve hot in bowls. Babenda should have the consistency of a thick, hearty porridge.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Benga
recipes.description: Burkinabe rice and beans plate, simple, nourishing, and usually finished with tomato-onion sauce or chili oil.
recipes.story: Benga means beans in Moore and the dish carries that simplicity proudly: beans, rice, sauce, pepper, and a pot that can feed a household.
recipes.occasion: Best for everyday lunch, school-night cooking, and market plates.
recipes.best_served_with: Tomato sauce, chili oil, fried onions, or grilled chicken.
recipes.regional_variations: Some households cook rice and beans together, while others serve tender beans over rice with sauce.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Cook beans
steps.instruction: Simmer soaked beans in water until tender.

steps.title: Add rice
steps.instruction: Add rinsed rice and enough hot water to cover. Cook until the rice is tender and liquid is absorbed.

steps.title: Make sauce
steps.instruction: Cook onions in oil, add tomatoes, tomato paste, pepper, and salt, then simmer into a spoonable sauce.

steps.title: Plate
steps.instruction: Serve rice and beans with the sauce spooned over the top.

recipes.name: Gonre
recipes.description: Burkinabe steamed bean cakes made from cowpea paste, peanut, leafy greens, and maize couscous.
recipes.story: Gonre is a Mossi bean dish with two moods: smooth in the dry season and leaf-rich when bean leaves are available.
recipes.occasion: Best for breakfast markets, snack plates, and ceremonial family cooking.
recipes.best_served_with: Tomato sauce, chili oil, or a light onion relish.
recipes.regional_variations: Leafy versions include bean leaves or greens; simpler versions are mostly cowpea paste and seasoning.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

ingredients.name: finely chopped bean leaves or spinach

steps.title: Blend the beans
steps.instruction: Blend peeled beans with onion, garlic, chili, and just enough water to make a thick paste.

steps.title: Season and rest
steps.instruction: Fold in peanut powder, greens, maize couscous, oil, and salt. Let the mixture hydrate.

steps.title: Steam cakes
steps.instruction: Spoon into oiled cups or leaves and steam until firm through the center.

steps.title: Serve warm
steps.instruction: Unmold and serve with tomato sauce or chili oil.

recipes.name: Poulet Bicyclette
recipes.description: Grilled free-range chicken marinated in a spicy mustard-lemon mix. Named after the free-range chickens that roam like bicycles through Burkinabé villages.
recipes.story: Poulet Bicyclette gets its name from the lean, free-range chickens that roam Burkinabé villages and towns on foot. Their meat is firmer and more flavourful than factory-farmed birds. Grilled over charcoal, it is the king of Ouagadougou's maquis (open-air bars).
recipes.occasion: Evening gatherings at maquis
recipes.best_served_with: Attiéké, fries, or riz gras
recipes.image_alt: Poulet Bicyclette recipe from Burkina Faso on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make the marinade
steps.instruction: Mix mustard, lime juice, garlic, cayenne, oil, salt, and pepper into a paste.

steps.title: Marinate the chicken
steps.instruction: Rub the marinade all over the chicken, getting under the skin. Refrigerate for at least 2 hours, ideally overnight.
steps.tip: Getting marinade under the skin is key to flavour.

steps.title: Grill the chicken
steps.instruction: Grill over medium charcoal heat, turning regularly, for about 35-40 minutes until the skin is crispy and the juices run clear.
steps.tip: Keep the heat medium to cook through without burning.

steps.title: Rest and serve
steps.instruction: Let the chicken rest for 5 minutes before cutting into pieces. Serve with your choice of sides.

recipes.name: Zamne
recipes.description: Burkinabe Acacia seed dish simmered until tender, then dressed with onion, tomato, oil, and chile.
recipes.story: Zamne turns a wild Sahelian seed into a celebration food: long-cooked, chewy, nutty, and often treated like a prized local ingredient.
recipes.occasion: Best for special meals, ceremonies, and vegetarian sharing plates.
recipes.best_served_with: Rice, couscous, grilled meat, or a tomato-onion relish.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks serve zamne as a salad, some simmer it with meat, and some dress it simply with oil, onion, tomato, and pepper.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Boil the seeds
steps.instruction: Rinse soaked zamne and boil in plenty of water until tender.

steps.title: Drain and season
steps.instruction: Drain the seeds and salt them lightly while hot.

steps.title: Cook relish
steps.instruction: Cook onions in oil until soft, then add tomato, pepper, and soumbala until jammy.

steps.title: Combine
steps.instruction: Fold zamne through the relish and simmer briefly so the seeds absorb the sauce.

recipes.name: Burundian Isombe
recipes.description: Burundi's version of pounded cassava leaves simmered with palm oil, onions, and a touch of dried fish.
recipes.story: Isombe is shared across Rwanda and Burundi but each country has its own twist. Burundian isombe often includes dried fish for an umami depth and is simmered longer until silky smooth. It is a dish of resilience, made from cassava leaves that grow abundantly even in difficult times, nourishing families across the country.
recipes.image_alt: Burundian Isombe recipe from Burundi on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Prepare Leaves
steps.instruction: If using fresh cassava leaves, pound in a mortar to a fine paste. If using frozen, thaw and drain.
steps.tip: The finer you pound the leaves, the smoother and creamier the final dish.

steps.title: Cook Base
steps.instruction: Heat palm oil in a pot. Saute onion and garlic until golden. Add tomatoes and eggplant, cook until softened.

steps.title: Add Leaves and Fish
steps.instruction: Add pounded cassava leaves, dried fish, and water. Stir well to combine.
steps.tip: The dried fish dissolves during cooking and adds deep savory flavor.

steps.title: Simmer
steps.instruction: Bring to a boil, then reduce heat. Cover and simmer, stirring occasionally, until leaves are very tender and sauce is thick.
steps.tip: Burundian cooks simmer isombe for at least 40 minutes — patience makes it silky.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Season with salt. Serve with ubugari (cassava or maize porridge) and beans.
steps.tip: Isombe and beans together provide complete protein — a traditional Burundian combination.

recipes.name: Mukeke
recipes.description: Lake Tanganyika's prized fish grilled whole with a zesty lemon-garlic marinade — Burundi's freshwater treasure.
recipes.story: Mukeke is a freshwater fish from Lake Tanganyika, the second deepest lake in the world. It is the most prized fish in Burundian cuisine, known for its firm white flesh and clean flavor. Fishermen bring their catch to the lakeside markets of Bujumbura where mukeke commands the highest prices.
recipes.image_alt: Mukeke recipe from Burundi on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Marinate Fish
steps.instruction: Score fish deeply on both sides. Mix lemon juice, garlic, ginger, oil, salt, pepper, and paprika. Rub marinade all over and inside the fish.
steps.tip: Let the fish marinate at least 30 minutes for the lemon and garlic to penetrate.

steps.title: Heat Grill
steps.instruction: Heat charcoal grill until coals are white-hot. Oil the grate to prevent sticking.

steps.title: Grill Fish
steps.instruction: Grill fish over medium-hot coals, turning once carefully, until skin is charred and flesh is opaque and flakes easily.
steps.tip: Use a fish basket or two spatulas to flip — mukeke breaks apart if handled roughly.

steps.title: Prepare Garnish
steps.instruction: Arrange sliced tomatoes and onions on a plate. Season with a squeeze of lemon and salt.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Place grilled fish on the bed of tomatoes and onions. Serve with ubugari or fried plantains.
steps.tip: In Bujumbura, grilled mukeke by the lake at sunset is a quintessential Burundian experience.

recipes.name: Ubugari
recipes.description: Burundi's cassava flour porridge — smooth, stretchy, and the daily staple that anchors every meal.
recipes.story: Ubugari is the daily bread of Burundi, made from cassava flour cooked into a thick, smooth porridge. It serves as the base of virtually every meal, paired with beans, isombe, or fish stew. In Burundian culture, refusing ubugari is considered rude, and the quality of one's ubugari is a mark of cooking skill.
recipes.image_alt: Ubugari recipe from Burundi on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Boil Water
steps.instruction: Bring water to a rolling boil in a heavy pot. Add salt.

steps.title: Cook Ubugari
steps.instruction: Reduce heat to medium. Add cassava flour gradually, stirring constantly and vigorously with a wooden spoon until thick, smooth, and elastic.
steps.tip: Cassava flour thickens faster than maize — add it slowly to control the consistency.

steps.title: Shape
steps.instruction: Wet a plate and turn ubugari out in a dome shape. Cover to keep warm.

steps.title: Prepare Beans
steps.instruction: Heat palm oil in a pan. Saute onion until soft. Add tomatoes and cook until saucy. Add cooked beans and salt. Simmer 10 minutes.
steps.tip: Palm oil gives the beans their distinctive orange color and rich flavor.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Place ubugari dome on plate with bean stew alongside. Eat by pinching off pieces and dipping.
steps.tip: In Burundi, ubugari and beans together are considered a complete, balanced meal.

collections.name: Quick & Easy
collections.description: Delicious African dishes ready in 30 minutes or less. Perfect for busy weeknight dinners.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Eru
recipes.description: A hearty stew of shredded eru leaves and waterleaf cooked with palm oil, smoked fish, and cow skin. A signature dish of the South-West Cameroon region.
recipes.story: Eru is the soul food of the anglophone South-West region of Cameroon, especially among the Bayangi people. The wild eru vine (Gnetum africanum) grows in the dense equatorial forest and its leaves are hand-shredded into thin strips. Combined with the wilting waterleaf, generous palm oil, and smoked proteins, it creates a dish that is simultaneously light and deeply flavourful.
recipes.occasion: Family meals, ceremonies, Sunday lunch
recipes.best_served_with: Garri (cassava granules soaked in water) or fufu corn
recipes.image_alt: Eru recipe from Cameroon on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Prepare the leaves
steps.instruction: Wash the eru leaves thoroughly. If using fresh, shred into very thin strips. Wash and roughly chop the waterleaf.
steps.tip: Eru leaves should be cut as thinly as possible — this is key to the texture.

steps.title: Cook the waterleaf
steps.instruction: Place the waterleaf in a dry pot over medium heat. Cook, stirring occasionally, until it wilts and releases all its water, about 10 minutes. Drain off the excess liquid.
steps.tip: Waterleaf releases a lot of water — draining it prevents a watery final dish.

steps.title: Add palm oil and proteins
steps.instruction: Add palm oil to the wilted waterleaf. Stir in the smoked fish, cow skin, and ground crayfish. Cook for 5 minutes to meld the flavours.

steps.title: Add eru leaves
steps.instruction: Add the shredded eru leaves and stir to combine. The eru should absorb the palm oil and soften slightly. Cook on low heat for 20 minutes, stirring occasionally.
steps.tip: Do not add water — the dish should be dry and oily, not soupy.

steps.title: Season and serve
steps.instruction: Season with salt and stir. Serve hot with garri soaked in cold water or fufu corn.
steps.tip: Eru is traditionally eaten with the fingers, using garri as a scoop.

recipes.name: Poulet DG
recipes.description: Cameroonian chicken with fried plantains, carrots, green beans, peppers, tomatoes, ginger, garlic, and herbs.
recipes.story: Poulet DG, often read as 'director general chicken', is Cameroonian abundance in one pan: chicken, ripe plantain, and vegetables cooked glossy and generous enough for guests.
recipes.occasion: Best for parties, Sunday lunch, and any table that wants one colorful centerpiece.
recipes.best_served_with: Serve as a complete plate or with rice for a larger meal.
recipes.regional_variations: Vegetable choices vary, with carrots, green beans, peppers, celery, leeks, and tomatoes all common. Plantains are usually fried before joining the sauce.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Season and brown
steps.instruction: Season chicken with salt, pepper, garlic, and ginger. Brown in a wide pot until golden on several sides.

steps.title: Fry the plantains
steps.instruction: Fry plantain slices in hot oil until golden on both sides. Drain and set aside.
steps.tip: Firm ripe plantains hold their shape best.

steps.title: Make the vegetable sauce
steps.instruction: Cook onions, tomatoes, carrots, green beans, and peppers with the chicken until the sauce thickens and the chicken is cooked through.

steps.title: Fold together
steps.instruction: Add fried plantains and herbs. Toss gently until glossy and hot, then serve.

recipes.name: Buzio Caboverdiano
recipes.description: Cape Verdean sea snail or conch stew slow-cooked with onion, garlic, tomato, pepper, bay, potatoes, and herbs.
recipes.story: Buzio brings Cape Verde's coastline to the pot: a slow seafood stew where tough conch turns tender and the sauce gathers tomato, bay, and island herbs.
recipes.occasion: Best for seafood weekends, coastal menus, and celebration lunches.
recipes.best_served_with: Rice, boiled root vegetables, bread, or xerem.
recipes.regional_variations: Depending on the island, cooks may use conch, whelk, mixed shellfish, potatoes, carrots, wine, or soy sauce.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Tenderize shellfish
steps.instruction: Simmer or pressure-cook conch in salted water until it begins to soften.

steps.title: Build sauce
steps.instruction: Cook onion, garlic, tomato, pepper, and bay in oil until the tomato breaks down.

steps.title: Stew
steps.instruction: Add conch, potatoes, carrots, and stock. Simmer until vegetables and shellfish are tender.

steps.title: Finish herbs
steps.instruction: Adjust salt, add herbs, and rest briefly before serving.

recipes.name: Canja de Galinha
recipes.description: A comforting Cape Verdean chicken and rice soup with vegetables and a squeeze of lime. The Cape Verdean version of the Portuguese classic, adapted with local spices.
recipes.story: Canja was brought to Cape Verde by Portuguese colonists and has become a cherished comfort food. It is the first dish offered to the sick, to new mothers, and to anyone in need of nourishment and warmth.
recipes.occasion: Comfort food and recovery meals
recipes.best_served_with: Crusty bread
recipes.image_alt: Canja de Galinha recipe from Cape Verde on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Cook the chicken
steps.instruction: Place chicken in a pot with 6 cups water, onion, garlic, and salt. Boil until tender, about 25 minutes. Remove chicken, shred the meat, discard bones.

steps.title: Add rice and vegetables
steps.instruction: Add rice and carrots to the broth. Simmer for 15 minutes until rice is cooked and carrots are tender.

steps.title: Return chicken
steps.instruction: Add shredded chicken back to the soup. Season with salt, pepper, and a generous squeeze of lime.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Ladle into bowls and serve hot with extra lime wedges and crusty bread.

collections.name: One-Pot Wonders
collections.description: Hearty single-pot dishes that are easy to make and full of flavor. Less cleanup, more taste.

recipes.name: Jagacida
recipes.description: Cape Verdean beans and rice cooked with onion, garlic, bay, and linguiça-style sausage or kept vegetarian.
recipes.story: Jagacida, often shortened to jag, is the kind of Cape Verdean rice dish that can be a side, a quick lunch, or the base for a bigger plate.
recipes.occasion: Best for weeknight dinners, cookouts, and family sides.
recipes.best_served_with: Grilled fish, cachupa leftovers, fried eggs, or hot sauce.
recipes.regional_variations: Some families use kidney beans, lima beans, or peas; some add linguiça while others keep it vegetarian.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

ingredients.name: cooked kidney beans or lima beans

steps.title: Brown aromatics
steps.instruction: Cook onion, garlic, and sausage in oil until fragrant and lightly browned.

steps.title: Coat the rice
steps.instruction: Stir in rice, beans, bay leaves, salt, and pepper so the grains are glossy.

steps.title: Simmer
steps.instruction: Add water or stock, cover, and cook until rice is tender.

steps.title: Steam and fluff
steps.instruction: Rest off heat, covered, then fluff gently and serve.

recipes.name: Pastel com Diablo Dentro
recipes.description: Cape Verdean fried pastries stuffed with a fiery tuna and tomato filling. The name means "pastry with the devil inside" due to the spicy filling.
recipes.story: These fiery little pastries are a staple of Cape Verdean street food and celebrations. The "devil inside" is the combination of malagueta peppers and spiced tuna that gives an unexpected kick with every bite.
recipes.occasion: Street food and celebrations
recipes.best_served_with: On its own as a snack
recipes.image_alt: Pastel com Diablo Dentro recipe from Cape Verde on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make the dough
steps.instruction: Mix flour, oil, salt, and warm water into a smooth dough. Knead for 5 minutes until elastic. Rest 30 minutes covered.

steps.title: Make the filling
steps.instruction: Fry onion until soft. Add tomatoes and peppers, cook 5 minutes. Add tuna and cook 5 more minutes until dry. Season well.
steps.tip: The filling must be dry — wet filling makes soggy pastels.

steps.title: Assemble
steps.instruction: Roll dough thin. Cut into circles. Place a spoonful of filling in the centre, fold into half-moons, and seal edges with a fork.

steps.title: Fry
steps.instruction: Deep fry in oil at 180C until golden brown and crispy, about 3-4 minutes.
steps.tip: Fry in batches without crowding.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Drain the fried pastries on paper towels for a minute, then serve them hot while the crust is crisp and the spicy filling is still warm.

collections.name: Street Food
collections.description: Popular street snacks and quick bites found at markets and roadside stalls across Africa.

recipes.name: Xerem
recipes.description: Cape Verdean cracked-corn porridge or stew, cooked savory with bay, butter, beans, greens, and smoked meat or fish.
recipes.story: Xerem shows Cape Verde's corn table from another angle: not cachupa, but a thick cracked-corn dish that can be simple or feast-day rich.
recipes.occasion: Best for cool evenings, family pots, and rustic one-bowl meals.
recipes.best_served_with: Braised greens, fish stew, beans, or hot sauce.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions are plain and buttery, while feast versions include pork, beans, squash, cabbage, or greens.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Start the pot
steps.instruction: Cook onion, garlic, and smoked meat in butter or oil until aromatic.

steps.title: Simmer corn
steps.instruction: Add cracked corn, bay leaves, and stock. Simmer, stirring often, until the corn softens.

steps.title: Add beans and greens
steps.instruction: Stir in beans and greens and continue cooking until the pot is thick and cohesive.

steps.title: Rest
steps.instruction: Season, rest covered, and serve warm.

recipes.name: Kanda ti Nyma
recipes.description: Savoury beef meatballs simmered in a thick peanut and tomato sauce. A hearty dish from the Central African Republic that is packed with protein and flavour.
recipes.story: Kanda ti nyma — meaning "meat balls" in Sango — is a festive dish reserved for special occasions and honoured guests. The meatballs are often made with hand-minced beef and bound with ground peanuts, which give them a distinctive nutty flavour and firm texture. Simmered in a rich tomato-peanut sauce, this is comfort food at its finest in the Central African Republic.
recipes.occasion: Special occasions, guest hospitality, celebrations
recipes.best_served_with: Gozo, rice, or boiled plantains
recipes.image_alt: Kanda ti Nyma recipe from Central African Republic on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make the meatballs
steps.instruction: Combine beef mince, ground peanuts, finely diced onion, and salt in a bowl. Mix well with your hands until the mixture holds together. Shape into golf ball-sized meatballs.
steps.tip: The ground peanuts act as a binder — no egg needed.

steps.title: Brown the meatballs
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a large pan and brown the meatballs on all sides, about 6 minutes total. Remove and set aside.
steps.tip: Do not overcrowd the pan — brown in batches for the best crust.

steps.title: Make the sauce
steps.instruction: In the same pan, sauté the diced onion until soft. Add the blended tomatoes and cook for 10 minutes until thickened. Stir in the peanut butter and 1 cup of water, mixing until smooth.

steps.title: Simmer meatballs in sauce
steps.instruction: Return the meatballs to the sauce. Cover and simmer gently for 25 minutes, turning the meatballs once halfway through.
steps.tip: Keep the heat low — vigorous boiling will break the meatballs apart.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Taste the sauce and adjust seasoning. Serve the meatballs and sauce over gozo or rice.

recipes.name: Sauce Graine
recipes.description: A rich palm fruit sauce simmered with smoked fish, tomatoes, and hot peppers. The quintessential accompaniment to gozo in the Central African Republic.
recipes.story: Sauce graine — literally "seed sauce" — is made from the pulp of palm fruits, which are boiled and pressed to extract a thick, rich cream. In the Central African Republic, this sauce is the most common pairing with gozo. The addition of smoked fish and chilli creates layers of flavour that make this simple sauce remarkably complex.
recipes.occasion: Everyday meals, family dining
recipes.best_served_with: Gozo (cassava porridge) or rice
recipes.image_alt: Sauce Graine recipe from Central African Republic on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Prepare the palm cream
steps.instruction: If using fresh palm fruits, boil for 20 minutes, then pound and strain to extract the cream. If using canned palm cream, open and stir until smooth.
steps.tip: Canned palm cream is widely available and saves significant preparation time.

steps.title: Sauté the aromatics
steps.instruction: Heat a tablespoon of palm oil in a pot. Cook the onion until soft and translucent, about 5 minutes. Add the chopped tomatoes and cook until they break down.

steps.title: Add the palm cream
steps.instruction: Stir in the palm cream and water. Bring to a simmer, stirring well to prevent separation.

steps.title: Simmer with fish
steps.instruction: Add the flaked smoked fish and whole scotch bonnet peppers. Cover and simmer for 30 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the sauce is rich and thickened with orange oil floating on top.
steps.tip: The orange oil on top is a sign the sauce is ready.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Remove the scotch bonnet peppers. Taste for salt and serve hot over gozo or rice.

recipes.name: Boule
recipes.description: A smooth, firm millet or sorghum porridge shaped for scooping up Chadian sauces and stews.
recipes.story: Boule is the quiet anchor of many Chadian meals. Millet or sorghum flour is worked into boiling water until it turns smooth, firm, and elastic enough to tear by hand and scoop through stews.
recipes.occasion: Every meal, daily staple
recipes.best_served_with: Daraba, okra sauce, meat stews
recipes.regional_variations: Millet flour is common, but sorghum or maize flour may be used depending on the household and region.
recipes.image_alt: Boule recipe from Chad on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Start the slurry
steps.instruction: Bring most of the water to a boil in a heavy pot. In a bowl, mix 1 cup of millet flour with 1 cup cold water until smooth.
steps.tip: A cold slurry helps stop lumps before they start.

steps.title: Cook the base
steps.instruction: Pour the slurry into the boiling water while stirring. Cook until it thickens and turns glossy.

steps.title: Beat in the flour
steps.instruction: Add the remaining flour gradually, stirring hard with a strong wooden spoon until the mixture becomes stiff.
steps.tip: This is arm work. The smoother the stirring, the better the boule.

steps.title: Finish the boule
steps.instruction: Keep stirring over low heat until the boule pulls away from the pot and tastes fully cooked.

steps.title: Shape and serve
steps.instruction: Wet a bowl or spoon, shape the boule into rounds, and serve hot with stew or sauce.

collections.name: Quick & Easy
collections.description: Delicious African dishes ready in 30 minutes or less. Perfect for busy weeknight dinners.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: La Bouillie
recipes.description: A warm Chadian millet porridge sweetened with sugar and finished with milk, vanilla, and lemon zest.
recipes.story: La Bouillie is morning comfort in a bowl. The millet slurry cooks into a smooth porridge, then milk, sugar, vanilla, and lemon make it gentle enough for breakfast or a light evening meal.
recipes.occasion: Breakfast, snack time
recipes.best_served_with: Bread, beignets, or on its own
recipes.image_alt: La Bouillie recipe from Chad on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make the slurry
steps.instruction: Mix the millet flour with 1 cup of cold water until smooth, ensuring there are no lumps.

steps.title: Cook the porridge
steps.instruction: Bring the remaining 3 cups of water to a boil. Slowly pour in the millet slurry while stirring constantly. Reduce heat to medium-low and cook, stirring, for 10 minutes until thickened.
steps.tip: Constant stirring prevents lumps and scorching.

steps.title: Add sweetness and flavour
steps.instruction: Stir in the sugar, milk, vanilla extract, and lemon zest. Continue cooking for another 5 minutes until everything is well combined and the porridge reaches your desired consistency.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Ladle into bowls and serve warm. The porridge can be made thinner or thicker by adjusting the water.
steps.tip: Add more milk for a creamier texture, or thin with water for a drinkable consistency.

collections.name: Quick & Easy
collections.description: Delicious African dishes ready in 30 minutes or less. Perfect for busy weeknight dinners.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Langouste a la Vanille
recipes.description: Comorian lobster finished in a delicate vanilla cream sauce, elegant without hiding the sweetness of the seafood.
recipes.story: Langouste a la Vanille works because the vanilla is gentle. It perfumes the cream, softens the wine and shallots, and lets the lobster taste like the sea.
recipes.best_served_with: Steamed rice, sauteed greens, or pilao ya nazi
recipes.image_alt: Langouste a la Vanille recipe from Comoros on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

ingredients.name: lobster tails
ingredients.substitution: small lobster, langoustines, shrimp, or scallops

steps.title: Sear the lobster
steps.instruction: Brush lobster with oil and sear flesh-side down until lightly golden. Remove before it is fully cooked.

steps.title: Start the sauce
steps.instruction: Melt butter in the pan. Cook shallots until soft, then add white wine and reduce by half.

steps.title: Infuse the vanilla
steps.instruction: Add vanilla seeds, pods, and cream. Simmer gently until the sauce lightly coats a spoon.

steps.title: Finish the lobster
steps.instruction: Return lobster to the pan and cook gently until opaque and just firm.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Remove vanilla pods, season with salt and white pepper, and add a little lime if the sauce needs brightness.

recipes.name: Mkatra Foutra
recipes.description: Comorian coconut flatbread made with yeast, coconut milk, and sesame, cooked on a hot pan until soft and golden.
recipes.story: Mkatra Foutra should feel like bread from the skillet, not cake from the oven. Coconut milk makes the dough tender, yeast gives it lift, and sesame seeds toast against the pan.
recipes.occasion: Breakfast, tea time, curry side
recipes.best_served_with: Tea, honey, butter, fish curry, rougaille, or coconut-based stews
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks make smaller rounds, others cook one larger flatbread and cut it into wedges.
recipes.image_alt: Mkatra Foutra recipe from Comoros on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Mix the dough
steps.instruction: Mix flour, yeast, sugar, and salt. Add warm coconut milk and knead until soft and smooth.

steps.title: Let it rise
steps.instruction: Cover and leave in a warm place until puffy and roughly doubled.

steps.title: Shape the breads
steps.instruction: Divide into 8 pieces. Flatten each into a round and press sesame seeds into one side.

steps.title: Cook on the pan
steps.instruction: Lightly oil a heavy pan and cook each round over medium heat until golden on both sides and cooked through.

steps.title: Serve warm
steps.instruction: Serve warm with tea, honey, butter, fish curry, or a coconut relish.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Pilao ya Nazi
recipes.description: Comorian coconut rice pilaf infused with cardamom, cloves, and cinnamon — fragrant and creamy.
recipes.story: Pilao ya Nazi lets coconut do the quiet work. The rice steams with warm spices until the grains are fragrant, creamy at the edges, and ready to carry seafood or stew.
recipes.best_served_with: Langouste a la Vanille, grilled fish, chicken curry, or tomato relishes
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions add meat or fish, while this one stays as a coconut rice side.
recipes.image_alt: Pilao ya Nazi recipe from Comoros on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Bloom the spices
steps.instruction: Heat oil and cook onion until golden. Add cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, and turmeric, then stir until fragrant.

steps.title: Toast the rice
steps.instruction: Add rinsed rice and stir so every grain is coated in the spiced oil.

steps.title: Steam
steps.instruction: Add coconut milk, water, and salt. Bring to a boil, cover tightly, and cook on the lowest heat until the liquid is absorbed.

steps.title: Rest
steps.instruction: Take the pot off the heat and rest covered so the grains finish steaming.

steps.title: Fluff and serve
steps.instruction: Fluff gently, remove whole spices if desired, and serve with seafood, chicken, or vegetable stew.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

collections.name: One-Pot Wonders
collections.description: Hearty single-pot dishes that are easy to make and full of flavor. Less cleanup, more taste.

recipes.name: Liboke ya Mbika
recipes.description: Ground pumpkin seeds steamed in banana leaves with tomatoes, onions, and spices, creating a rich and fragrant vegetarian parcel. A classic Congolese preparation.
recipes.story: Liboke is the art of letting banana leaves season the food while they protect it. Here, ground pumpkin seeds steam into a rich mbika parcel with tomato, onion, palm oil, and chile.
recipes.occasion: Everyday meals, Lent, plant-based dining
recipes.best_served_with: Fufu or boiled plantains
recipes.regional_variations: Fish, pork, cassava leaves, and pumpkin seed parcels all sit inside the wider liboke family.
recipes.image_alt: Liboke ya Mbika recipe from Democratic Republic of Congo on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Grind the seeds
steps.instruction: Toast pumpkin seeds in a dry pan for 3 minutes until fragrant. Grind to a fine paste in a blender or food processor, adding a splash of water if needed.
steps.tip: Do not over-toast — the seeds should be lightly golden, not dark.

steps.title: Mix the filling
steps.instruction: Combine the ground pumpkin seed paste with diced tomatoes, chopped onion, palm oil, minced scotch bonnet, and salt. Mix until everything is well incorporated.

steps.title: Wrap in banana leaves
steps.instruction: Soften banana leaves by briefly passing them over an open flame or blanching in hot water. Place a generous scoop of the mixture in the centre of each leaf and fold into a secure parcel, tying with kitchen string or strips of banana leaf.
steps.tip: Double-wrap the parcels to prevent leaking during steaming.

steps.title: Steam the parcels
steps.instruction: Place parcels in a steamer basket or in a pot with a small amount of water at the bottom. Cover and steam for 40-45 minutes until the filling is firm and cooked through.
steps.tip: Check water level halfway through — do not let the pot run dry.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Unwrap the parcels at the table for a dramatic presentation. Serve with fufu or boiled plantains.
steps.tip: The banana leaf aroma is part of the experience — serve immediately after unwrapping.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Saka-Saka
recipes.description: Finely shredded cassava leaves slow-cooked with palm oil, onions, and dried fish until meltingly tender. A beloved Congolese staple rich in iron and flavour.
recipes.story: Saka-Saka is a patient pot. Cassava leaves need time to soften, palm oil gives them depth, dried fish brings smoke, and peanut paste makes the greens round and satisfying.
recipes.occasion: Everyday meals, communal dining
recipes.best_served_with: Fufu, boiled cassava, or rice
recipes.image_alt: Saka-Saka recipe from Democratic Republic of Congo on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Prepare the leaves
steps.instruction: Pound or blend fresh cassava leaves very finely. If using frozen leaves, thaw and squeeze lightly.

steps.title: Boil the greens
steps.instruction: Simmer cassava leaves with water until they begin to soften.
steps.tip: Cassava leaves are not a quick green. Give them time.

steps.title: Build the flavor
steps.instruction: In another pan, heat palm oil and cook onion until golden. Add garlic and fish, then stir into the cassava leaves.

steps.title: Add peanut and slow-cook
steps.instruction: Loosen peanut butter with hot cooking liquid and stir it into the pot. Add the whole chile if using and simmer until the leaves are tender and rich.

steps.title: Finish
steps.instruction: Remove the whole chile, season with salt, and serve with fufu, boiled cassava, plantain, or rice.

recipes.name: Djiboutian Laxoox
recipes.description: Djibouti's spongy fermented crepe made with sorghum and wheat flour — served with honey, ghee, or savory stews.
recipes.story: Laxoox is breakfast bread with bubbles. The batter ferments until lively, then cooks on one side so the top stays spongy enough to catch honey, ghee, or stew.
recipes.best_served_with: Honey and ghee, spiced tea, fah-fah, or tomato stews
recipes.image_alt: Djiboutian Laxoox recipe from Djibouti on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Mix the batter
steps.instruction: Whisk flours, yeast, sugar, salt, and warm water until smooth.

steps.title: Ferment
steps.instruction: Cover and let the batter stand until bubbly and lightly tangy.
steps.tip: A warm kitchen gives better bubbles.

steps.title: Cook one side
steps.instruction: Pour a thin layer into a warm non-stick pan. Cook without flipping until the top is full of small holes and the edges lift.

steps.title: Stack
steps.instruction: Lift the laxoox carefully and stack on a plate. Repeat with the remaining batter.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve with honey and ghee for breakfast, or use it to scoop soup or stew.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Fah-Fah
recipes.description: Djibouti's hearty goat soup with vegetables, cumin, and chili — a restorative broth for all occasions.
recipes.story: Fah-Fah is broth with backbone. Bone-in goat gives the soup strength, potatoes and carrots make it a meal, and lime at the table wakes the whole bowl up.
recipes.best_served_with: Laxoox, crusty bread, rice, or lime wedges
recipes.image_alt: Fah-Fah recipe from Djibouti on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Start the broth
steps.instruction: Place goat pieces in a large pot with water. Bring to a boil and skim the foam.

steps.title: Simmer the meat
steps.instruction: Add onion, garlic, cumin, coriander, turmeric, and the whole chile. Simmer until the meat is tender.

steps.title: Add vegetables
steps.instruction: Add carrots and potatoes and keep simmering until the vegetables are tender.

steps.title: Season
steps.instruction: Remove the whole chile, season with salt, and check that the broth tastes full.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Ladle into bowls with meat and vegetables. Finish with cilantro and lime.

recipes.name: Skoudehkaris
recipes.description: Djibouti's national dish — spiced rice with lamb, caramelized onions, cardamom, and cumin, a one-pot feast.
recipes.story: Skoudehkaris should smell like the port cities of the Horn: lamb, rice, tomato, cardamom, cumin, cinnamon, and a little chile all settling into one pot.
recipes.best_served_with: Banana, salad, yogurt, or chile sauce
recipes.image_alt: Skoudehkaris recipe from Djibouti on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Brown the lamb
steps.instruction: Season lamb with salt and black pepper. Brown it in hot oil until the edges take color.

steps.title: Cook the tomato base
steps.instruction: Add onions and cook until soft. Stir in garlic, tomatoes, tomato paste, cardamom, cumin, cinnamon, turmeric, and chile.

steps.title: Simmer the meat
steps.instruction: Add water or stock, cover, and simmer until the lamb is almost tender.

steps.title: Steam the rice
steps.instruction: Add drained rice to the pot. Cover tightly and cook on low until the rice absorbs the broth.

steps.title: Rest and finish
steps.instruction: Rest covered, fluff gently, scatter cilantro over the top, and serve with banana or salad.

collections.name: One-Pot Wonders
collections.description: Hearty single-pot dishes that are easy to make and full of flavor. Less cleanup, more taste.

recipes.name: Feteer Meshaltet
recipes.description: Layered Egyptian pastry made from stretched dough, ghee, and oil, baked into flaky golden sheets.
recipes.story: Feteer meshaltet is Egypt's layered pastry of celebration and generosity. The dough is rested, stretched paper-thin with ghee, folded into layers, and baked until puffed and flaky.
recipes.occasion: Best for breakfast tables, holidays, guest visits, and tea service.
recipes.best_served_with: Honey, molasses, clotted cream, feta, olives, or mish.
recipes.regional_variations: Plain feteer is served sweet or savory, while stuffed versions can include cheese, sausage, sugar, coconut, or custard.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Make the dough
steps.instruction: Knead flour, salt, and warm water into a soft elastic dough.

steps.title: Rest the balls
steps.instruction: Divide into balls, coat with oil, cover, and rest until very relaxed.

steps.title: Stretch and layer
steps.instruction: Stretch each ball thin, brush with ghee, fold into layers, and stack into a round.

steps.title: Bake hot
steps.instruction: Bake in a very hot oven until puffed, golden, and flaky.

recipes.name: Ful Medames
recipes.description: Slow-cooked fava beans mashed with garlic, lemon, and cumin, drizzled with olive oil and served with warm pita. Egypt's beloved everyday breakfast.
recipes.story: Ful Medames is everyday Egyptian comfort. The beans should be creamy but not erased, bright with lemon, warm with cumin, and glossy with olive oil.
recipes.occasion: Daily breakfast
recipes.best_served_with: Warm pita bread, pickled turnips, and fresh vegetables
recipes.image_alt: Ful Medames recipe from Egypt on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Cook the beans
steps.instruction: Drain the soaked fava beans, place in a large pot, and cover with fresh water. Bring to a boil, then reduce heat and simmer for about 1 hour until the beans are very soft and creamy. Reserve some cooking liquid before draining.
steps.tip: For an authentic texture, some beans should be falling apart while others stay whole.

steps.title: Mash and season
steps.instruction: Partially mash the beans with a fork or potato masher, leaving some whole for texture. Add garlic, cumin, lemon juice, and salt. Stir well, adding cooking liquid as needed to reach your desired consistency.
steps.tip: The ful should be creamy but not a smooth puree — texture is key.

steps.title: Warm through
steps.instruction: Return the mashed beans to low heat and warm through for 5 minutes, stirring gently to let the garlic and cumin infuse.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Spoon into shallow bowls. Drizzle generously with olive oil, sprinkle with cumin and parsley, and serve with warm pita bread, pickled turnips, and sliced tomatoes.
steps.tip: Every diner should customise their bowl with extra lemon, cumin, or chilli to taste.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

collections.name: Street Food
collections.description: Popular street snacks and quick bites found at markets and roadside stalls across Africa.

recipes.name: Hawawshi
recipes.description: Egyptian baladi bread stuffed with spiced minced beef, onion, pepper, parsley, and chili, then baked until crisp.
recipes.story: Hawawshi is Cairo comfort in a pocket: spiced meat pressed inside bread and baked until the outside crisps and the juices season the loaf from within. Alexandrian versions may use a dough casing, while many homes use baladi or pita bread.
recipes.occasion: Best for quick dinners, street-food spreads, and game-day plates.
recipes.best_served_with: Pickles, tahini, tomato cucumber salad, or chili sauce.
recipes.regional_variations: Cairo hawawshi is often stuffed into bread, while Alexandrian hawawshi can be baked in fresh dough with a hotter spice profile.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Mix the filling
steps.instruction: Combine beef, onion, peppers, parsley, spices, salt, and pepper until evenly seasoned.

steps.title: Stuff the bread
steps.instruction: Open each bread pocket and spread a thin layer of meat all the way to the edges.

steps.title: Bake until crisp
steps.instruction: Brush with oil and bake on a hot tray until the bread crisps and the beef is cooked through.

steps.title: Rest and slice
steps.instruction: Rest briefly so the juices settle, then cut into wedges and serve hot.

recipes.name: Mahshi
recipes.description: Egyptian vegetables stuffed with herbed tomato rice and simmered in a light tomato broth.
recipes.story: Mahshi is the Egyptian stuffed-vegetable craft that fills tables and kitchens with herbs. Rice, tomato, dill, parsley, cilantro, onion, and spice are tucked into zucchini, peppers, eggplant, cabbage, or grape leaves.
recipes.occasion: Best for Friday lunches, Ramadan tables, and family gatherings.
recipes.best_served_with: Roast chicken, yogurt, salad, or soup.
recipes.regional_variations: Vegetable choice changes by household. Some versions include minced meat, while many Egyptian home versions keep the stuffing rice-forward and herb-heavy.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Make the stuffing
steps.instruction: Mix rice, onion, tomato sauce, herbs, oil, cumin, mint, salt, and pepper.

steps.title: Fill the vegetables
steps.instruction: Stuff vegetables loosely, leaving room for the rice to expand.

steps.title: Arrange and broth
steps.instruction: Pack vegetables upright in a pot and pour in seasoned broth to come partway up the sides.

steps.title: Simmer gently
steps.instruction: Cover and simmer on low heat until the vegetables are tender and the rice is cooked.

recipes.name: Molokhia
recipes.description: A vibrant green soup made from finely chopped jute leaves simmered in chicken broth, finished with a sizzling garlic and coriander dressing. Egypt's most iconic home-cooked meal.
recipes.story: Molokhia is all about texture and aroma. The jute leaves make the soup silky, then hot ghee, garlic, and coriander hit the pot and announce dinner.
recipes.occasion: Everyday family dinner
recipes.best_served_with: White rice, vermicelli rice, roasted chicken, lemon, and bread
recipes.image_alt: Molokhia recipe from Egypt on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make the broth
steps.instruction: Poach chicken with onion, bay leaves, cardamom, salt, and pepper until cooked. Remove the chicken and strain the broth.

steps.title: Cook the molokhia
steps.instruction: Bring broth to a simmer, add chopped molokhia, and cook gently until the soup is silky. Do not boil hard.

steps.title: Make the tash-a
steps.instruction: Heat ghee, add garlic until golden, then stir in coriander for a few seconds until fragrant.
steps.tip: Garlic moves from golden to burnt quickly, so stay close.

steps.title: Finish the pot
steps.instruction: Pour the sizzling garlic-coriander mixture into the molokhia and stir.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve with rice, chicken, lemon wedges, and bread.

recipes.name: Sayadeya
recipes.description: Egyptian fish and rice cooked with caramelized onions, cumin, tomato, warm spices, and seafood broth.
