recipes.story: Sayadeya is the fisherman's rice of Egypt's coastal cities, especially Alexandria and Port Said. Deeply browned onions color the rice, while fish broth and spices make it taste like the sea.
recipes.occasion: Best for seafood Fridays, coastal-style dinners, and family lunch.
recipes.best_served_with: Tahini sauce, salad baladi, pickles, and fried fish.
recipes.regional_variations: Some households fry the fish separately, others nestle it into the rice. Tomato may be light or more pronounced depending on the coast.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Season fish
steps.instruction: Season fish with lemon, salt, pepper, cumin, and coriander while the onions cook.

steps.title: Caramelize onions
steps.instruction: Cook onions in oil until deep brown but not burned.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.instruction: Stir in tomato paste, rice, spices, and fish stock, then simmer until the rice is nearly tender.

steps.title: Steam fish on top
steps.instruction: Place fish over the rice, cover, and steam until just cooked through.

recipes.name: Ta'ameya
recipes.description: Egyptian fava bean falafel made with soaked split fava beans, fresh herbs, garlic, spices, and a sesame crust before frying.
recipes.story: Ta'ameya is Egypt's green, herb-heavy answer to falafel, commonly made from dried split fava beans rather than chickpeas. The best patties are crisp outside, fluffy inside, and bright with parsley, cilantro, dill, garlic, cumin, and coriander.
recipes.occasion: Best for breakfast, mezze, street-food-style snacks, or a vegetarian sandwich spread.
recipes.best_served_with: Baladi bread, tahini sauce, pickles, chopped salad, and ful medames.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks add leek or scallion, some coat heavily with sesame, and Alexandria-style versions may hide a spicy chile filling.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Soak the beans
steps.instruction: Soak split fava beans overnight in plenty of water, then drain very well before grinding.
steps.tip: Dry beans are essential for texture. Canned beans will make the batter collapse.

steps.title: Grind the batter
steps.instruction: Pulse fava beans, herbs, scallions, garlic, cumin, coriander, salt, and pepper until finely ground but not wet or pureed.

steps.title: Season and shape
steps.instruction: Mix in baking powder. Shape small patties and press sesame seeds onto one side.

steps.title: Fry until crisp
steps.instruction: Fry in hot oil until deep golden and crisp, turning once. Drain and serve hot.
steps.tip: Fry a test patty first and adjust salt before shaping the rest.

recipes.name: Umm Ali
recipes.description: Egyptian baked bread pudding with pastry, milk, cream, nuts, raisins, coconut, and a golden top.
recipes.story: Umm Ali is Egypt's warm pastry pudding, a dessert that turns flaky bread or puff pastry into milk-soaked comfort with nuts, raisins, coconut, and cream browned on top.
recipes.occasion: Best for Ramadan nights, winter desserts, and family celebrations.
recipes.best_served_with: Mint tea, coffee, or extra warm milk.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks use puff pastry, others use roaa, croissants, or baked bread. Pistachios, almonds, hazelnuts, raisins, and coconut vary by household.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Toast the pastry
steps.instruction: Spread pastry pieces in a baking dish and toast until crisp and lightly golden.

steps.title: Heat the milk
steps.instruction: Warm milk with sugar and vanilla until the sugar dissolves.

steps.title: Soak and top
steps.instruction: Pour milk over pastry, add raisins, nuts, and coconut, then spoon cream over the top.

steps.title: Bake golden
steps.instruction: Bake until bubbling, then broil briefly for a browned cream top.

recipes.name: Jollof Rice Equatoguineano
recipes.description: A smoky Equatoguinean-style tomato rice cooked with peppers, palm oil, bay leaf, and a gentle Spanish paprika accent.
recipes.story: This is tomato rice with one foot in the Gulf of Guinea and one foot in Equatorial Guinea's Spanish-speaking kitchen. Cook the sauce down until it tastes rounded before the rice goes in.
recipes.occasion: Parties, celebrations, Sunday meals
recipes.best_served_with: Fried plantains, grilled fish, roast chicken, or a crisp tomato salad
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks use palm oil for a deeper color, while others use vegetable oil and keep the tomato-pepper base lighter.
recipes.image_alt: Jollof Rice Equatoguineano recipe from Equatorial Guinea on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

ingredients.name: long-grain rice
ingredients.prep_note: rinsed until the water runs clear

steps.title: Cook the base
steps.instruction: Heat the oil in a heavy pot. Fry the sliced onion until soft, then add tomato paste and cook until it darkens slightly.

steps.title: Reduce the sauce
steps.instruction: Add the blended tomatoes, peppers, onion, garlic, bay leaves, paprika if using, and scotch bonnet. Simmer until the sauce thickens and the oil begins to show.
steps.tip: The sauce should taste cooked before rice enters the pot.

steps.title: Add rice
steps.instruction: Stir in the rinsed rice until every grain is coated. Add hot water or stock and season with salt and black pepper.

steps.title: Steam gently
steps.instruction: Bring to a boil, then cover tightly and cook on low heat until the rice absorbs the liquid and becomes tender.
steps.tip: Keep the lid closed so the rice steams instead of drying out.

steps.title: Rest and serve
steps.instruction: Turn off the heat and rest the pot for 10 minutes. Remove bay leaves, fluff gently, and serve hot.

collections.name: One-Pot Wonders
collections.description: Hearty single-pot dishes that are easy to make and full of flavor. Less cleanup, more taste.

recipes.name: Pepesoup
recipes.description: A hot Equatorial Guinean fish pepesup with green plantain, tomato, ginger, chile, and a clear peppery broth.
recipes.story: Pepesup should wake the table up. Keep the broth clean, let the plantain soften, and lower the fish in gently so the pieces stay whole.
recipes.occasion: Celebrations, recovery meals, cold evenings
recipes.best_served_with: Steamed rice, boiled cassava, or extra green plantain
recipes.image_alt: Pepesoup recipe from Equatorial Guinea on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Season the fish
steps.instruction: Season the fish with salt, a spoon of citrus juice if using, and a pinch of pepper. Rest while the broth starts.

steps.title: Start the broth
steps.instruction: Bring water, onion, tomato, garlic, ginger, ground spices, and scotch bonnet to a boil.

steps.title: Cook the plantain
steps.instruction: Add green plantain chunks and simmer until just tender.

steps.title: Add the fish
steps.instruction: Lower the fish pieces into the broth, reduce to a steady simmer, and cook until opaque.
steps.tip: Swirl the pot instead of stirring hard so the fish stays intact.

steps.title: Finish hot
steps.instruction: Taste for salt and citrus. Break a pepper only if you want stronger heat, then serve the soup steaming hot.

recipes.name: Eritrean Ful Medames
recipes.description: Warm mashed fava beans finished with olive oil, lemon, cumin, chile, tomato, and berbere for an Eritrean breakfast bowl.
recipes.story: Ful is the kind of breakfast that can hold you until late afternoon. Keep some beans whole, mash some into the broth, then finish generously with oil, lemon, and fresh toppings.
recipes.best_served_with: Crusty bread rolls, injera, tomato, green chile, onion, tea, or coffee
recipes.regional_variations: Some ful houses add hard-boiled egg, yogurt, feta, awaze, or extra berbere at the table.
recipes.image_alt: Eritrean Ful Medames recipe from Eritrea on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Warm the beans
steps.instruction: Heat fava beans with a splash of their cooking liquid until hot. Mash about half the beans so the bowl turns creamy but still textured.

steps.title: Season
steps.instruction: Stir in garlic, cumin, berbere if using, and salt. Cook briefly so the garlic softens.

steps.title: Finish with oil and lemon
steps.instruction: Take off the heat and stir in olive oil and lemon juice. The ful should be loose enough to scoop with bread.

steps.title: Top and serve
steps.instruction: Top with tomato, chile, onion or scallion, and egg if using. Serve warm with bread or injera.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Eritrean Shiro
recipes.description: Silky Eritrean chickpea-flour shiro simmered with onion, garlic, berbere, tomato, and oil until spoonable over injera.
recipes.story: Good shiro is smooth, not heavy. Whisk the powder in slowly, keep the heat gentle, and stop when it pours like thick cream over injera.
recipes.best_served_with: Injera, salad, sauteed greens, or a little extra berbere oil
recipes.regional_variations: Fasting versions use oil. Non-fasting versions may finish with tesmi or niter kibbeh.
recipes.image_alt: Eritrean Shiro recipe from Eritrea on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

ingredients.name: shiro powder or roasted chickpea flour

steps.title: Cook the onion
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a pot. Cook onion gently until soft and sweet, then add garlic and berbere.

steps.title: Make the sauce
steps.instruction: Stir in tomato paste and hot water. Bring to a simmer and season lightly with salt.

steps.title: Whisk in shiro
steps.instruction: Sprinkle in shiro powder slowly while whisking so it does not clump.
steps.tip: If it thickens too fast, add hot water a splash at a time.

steps.title: Simmer smooth
steps.instruction: Cook on low heat, stirring often, until glossy and creamy.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Finish with niter kibbeh only if you are not keeping it fasting-style. Spoon over injera and serve hot.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Zigini with Injera
recipes.description: A slow Eritrean beef stew with berbere, tomato, onions, garlic, ginger, and a thick red sauce for injera.
recipes.story: Zigini is not a quick red sauce. Brown the beef, let the onion and berbere bloom, then braise until the meat gives way and the sauce clings to injera.
recipes.best_served_with: Injera, salad, lentils, or sauteed greens
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks use tesmi or niter kibbeh, while many home versions use oil and keep the stew dairy-free.
recipes.image_alt: Zigini with Injera recipe from Eritrea on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Brown the beef
steps.instruction: Season beef with salt and pepper. Brown it in hot oil, then remove from the pot.

steps.title: Cook the onion base
steps.instruction: Add onions to the same pot and cook until soft and beginning to brown. Stir in garlic, ginger, and berbere.

steps.title: Build the sauce
steps.instruction: Add tomato paste, tomatoes, and stock or water. Stir until the sauce is smooth.

steps.title: Braise
steps.instruction: Return beef to the pot, cover, and simmer gently until tender and the sauce is thick.

steps.title: Finish
steps.instruction: Taste for salt. Stir in tesmi or niter kibbeh only if using, then serve over injera.

recipes.name: Incwancwa
recipes.description: A tangy Swazi fermented maize porridge, served softly spoonable or thinned into a refreshing sour drink.
recipes.story: Incwancwa is all about patience. Cook the maize porridge, let it cool, then give fermentation time to bring the clean sour edge.
recipes.occasion: Hot weather refreshment
recipes.best_served_with: Served as a standalone drink or light meal
recipes.regional_variations: Some households serve it thick like porridge; others thin it with water and drink it chilled or at room temperature.
recipes.image_alt: Incwancwa drink from Eswatini on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Cook the porridge
steps.instruction: Mix the maize meal with 500ml of cold water to form a smooth paste. Bring the remaining litre of water to a boil. Gradually stir the paste into the boiling water. Cook for 15 minutes, stirring frequently, until thickened.

steps.title: Cool down
steps.instruction: Remove from heat and let the porridge cool to lukewarm. It should be warm to the touch but not hot.
steps.tip: The temperature is important — too hot and it will kill the fermenting agents.

steps.title: Add malt and ferment
steps.instruction: Stir in the sorghum malt until well combined. Cover with a clean cloth and leave at room temperature for 24-48 hours, stirring once or twice during this time.
steps.tip: The longer you ferment, the more sour it becomes — 24 hours gives a mild tang, 48 hours is more pronounced.

steps.title: Strain and serve
steps.instruction: Once fermented to your liking, stir well and add water to thin to a drinkable consistency. Add sugar to taste if desired. Serve at room temperature or slightly chilled.
steps.tip: Incwancwa should be the consistency of a thin smoothie — pourable but with some body.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Atkilt Wat
recipes.description: Ethiopian cabbage, potato, and carrot stew seasoned with turmeric, onion, garlic, and ginger.
recipes.story: Atkilt wat is a humble vegetable stew that earns its place beside bolder wots. Cabbage, potato, and carrot cook gently with turmeric until soft and sweet.
recipes.occasion: Best for fasting platters, family vegetables, and gentle sides.
recipes.best_served_with: Injera, lentil stews, shiro, or grilled meats.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks add green beans or chile, but cabbage, carrot, potato, turmeric, and onion are the steady base.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Cook aromatics
steps.instruction: Cook onion in oil until soft, then add garlic, ginger, and turmeric.

steps.title: Start potatoes
steps.instruction: Add potatoes and carrots with a splash of water. Cover and cook until partly tender.

steps.title: Add cabbage
steps.instruction: Add cabbage, salt, and pepper. Cover and cook until all vegetables are tender.

steps.title: Dry finish
steps.instruction: Uncover and cook briefly so excess moisture evaporates.

recipes.name: Genfo
recipes.description: Ethiopian thick barley or wheat porridge served with a well of spiced butter and berbere in the center.
recipes.story: Genfo is a thick Ethiopian breakfast porridge shaped with a crater of spiced butter and berbere. It is simple, filling, and deeply warming.
recipes.occasion: Best for breakfast, postpartum meals, and cold mornings.
recipes.best_served_with: Niter kibbeh, berbere, yogurt, or plain milk.
recipes.regional_variations: Barley flour, wheat flour, or mixed grain flour may be used depending on household and region.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Boil water
steps.instruction: Bring water and salt to a steady boil.

steps.title: Add flour
steps.instruction: Lower heat and add flour gradually while stirring hard.

steps.title: Cook thick
steps.instruction: Keep stirring until the porridge becomes thick, smooth, and pulls from the pot.

steps.title: Shape and finish
steps.instruction: Shape in a bowl, make a center well, and fill with spiced butter and berbere.

recipes.name: Gomen
recipes.description: Ethiopian collard greens sauteed with onion, garlic, ginger, chile, and spiced oil until tender and fragrant.
recipes.story: Gomen brings green depth to an Ethiopian platter. The greens are cooked until tender, not mushy, with onion, ginger, garlic, and just enough heat.
recipes.occasion: Best as a vegetable side for injera platters and fasting meals.
recipes.best_served_with: Injera, misir wat, kik alicha, tibs, or doro wat.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions use niter kibbeh, some stay vegan, and the chile level changes by table.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Cook the base
steps.instruction: Cook onion in oil until soft, then add garlic, ginger, and chile.

steps.title: Wilt greens
steps.instruction: Add collards by handfuls, stirring until wilted.

steps.title: Cook tender
steps.instruction: Cover and cook until the greens are tender but still green.

steps.title: Finish
steps.instruction: Season with salt and optional lemon juice.

recipes.name: Injera
recipes.description: A tangy Ethiopian teff flatbread fermented for two to three days, finished with absit, and cooked on one side until full of eyes.
recipes.story: Injera is patient bread. Fermentation gives the tang, absit gives the soft elastic texture, and the covered pan gives the lacy eyes.
recipes.best_served_with: Doro wat, shiro, misir wat, tibs, kitfo, salads, and vegetable stews
recipes.regional_variations: Diaspora cooks often blend teff with wheat, barley, rice, or sorghum flour depending on climate and flour quality.
recipes.image_alt: Injera recipe from Ethiopia on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

ingredients.name: sourdough starter or reserved injera batter

steps.title: Ferment the batter
steps.instruction: Whisk teff flour with lukewarm water and starter if using. Cover loosely and ferment at room temperature until bubbly and pleasantly sour.
steps.tip: Two days is mild; three days is tangier.

steps.title: Make absit
steps.instruction: Whisk about 1/2 cup fermented batter with 1 cup water in a small pot. Cook, stirring, until it turns glossy and thick. Cool until warm.

steps.title: Finish the batter
steps.instruction: Stir the cooled absit back into the fermented batter. Add salt and enough water for a thin crepe-like pour.

steps.title: Cook one side
steps.instruction: Heat a mitad or nonstick pan. Pour the batter in a spiral, cover, and cook until the surface is set and full of eyes. Do not flip.

steps.title: Cool and stack
steps.instruction: Lift the injera carefully onto a cloth. Cool fully before stacking so the breads do not stick.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Use one injera as the platter and roll extra pieces for scooping.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

collections.name: Sunday Specials
collections.description: Elaborate weekend dishes worth the extra effort. These are the recipes that make Sunday lunch special.

recipes.name: Kik Alicha
recipes.description: Mild Ethiopian yellow split pea stew with turmeric, onion, garlic, ginger, and a soft buttery texture.
recipes.story: Kik alicha is the gentle side of the Ethiopian platter: yellow split peas cooked until creamy with turmeric, onion, garlic, and ginger. It balances hotter stews beautifully.
recipes.occasion: Best for vegetarian platters, fasting meals, and mild family meals.
recipes.best_served_with: Injera, misir wat, gomen, shiro, or doro wat.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions use niter kibbeh, while fasting versions use oil. Texture ranges from loose stew to thick puree.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Cook aromatics
steps.instruction: Cook onion in oil until soft, then add garlic, ginger, and turmeric.

steps.title: Add peas
steps.instruction: Stir in split peas and water or stock.

steps.title: Simmer tender
steps.instruction: Simmer until the peas are soft and beginning to break down.

steps.title: Mash and finish
steps.instruction: Mash lightly, season with salt, and simmer until creamy.

recipes.name: Kitfo
recipes.description: Finely minced Ethiopian beef seasoned with warm niter kibbeh and mitmita, served tere, leb leb, or fully cooked with ayib and gomen.
recipes.story: Kitfo is a confidence dish. The mince must be fine, the butter warm but not scorching, and the mitmita strong enough to perfume every bite.
recipes.best_served_with: Ayib, gomen, kocho, injera, or extra mitmita
recipes.regional_variations: Tere is raw, leb leb is briefly warmed, and yebesele is cooked through for guests who prefer it that way.
recipes.image_alt: Kitfo recipe from Ethiopia on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Prepare the beef
steps.instruction: Use very fresh beef from a trusted butcher. Trim away sinew, keep it chilled, and mince finely with a sharp knife.
steps.tip: If you are not comfortable serving raw beef, choose leb leb or fully cooked kitfo.

steps.title: Make gomen
steps.instruction: Saute onion and garlic in oil. Add chopped greens and cook until tender, then season with salt.

steps.title: Prepare ayib
steps.instruction: Season cottage cheese or ayib lightly with salt and lemon if needed. Keep it cool until serving.

steps.title: Season kitfo
steps.instruction: Warm niter kibbeh just until melted. Mix beef with niter kibbeh, mitmita, cardamom if using, and salt.

steps.title: Choose the doneness
steps.instruction: Serve raw for tere, warm briefly in a pan for leb leb, or cook through for yebesele.
steps.tip: Serve immediately once seasoned.

steps.title: Plate
steps.instruction: Mound the kitfo with ayib and gomen beside it. Serve with injera or kocho.

collections.name: Quick & Easy
collections.description: Delicious African dishes ready in 30 minutes or less. Perfect for busy weeknight dinners.

recipes.name: Misir Wat
recipes.description: Ethiopian red lentil stew cooked with onion, garlic, ginger, berbere, and spiced oil until soft and fiery.
recipes.story: Misir wat is one of the dishes that makes an Ethiopian platter feel complete: red lentils, berbere heat, slow onions, and enough simmering for the lentils to collapse into sauce.
recipes.occasion: Best for vegetarian platters, fasting meals, and make-ahead cooking.
recipes.best_served_with: Injera, shiro wat, gomen, and mild vegetable sides.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks use niter kibbeh for richness, while fasting versions use oil and remain vegan.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Cook onions
steps.instruction: Cook onions in oil until soft and sweet, then add garlic, ginger, and berbere.

steps.title: Add lentils
steps.instruction: Stir in rinsed lentils and coat them in the spiced onion mixture.

steps.title: Simmer
steps.instruction: Add water or stock and simmer until the lentils collapse into a thick stew.

steps.title: Finish
steps.instruction: Season with salt and loosen with hot water if needed before serving.

recipes.name: Shiro Wat
recipes.description: Ethiopian chickpea flour stew simmered with onion, garlic, berbere, and oil until smooth, spicy, and ready for injera.
recipes.story: Shiro wat is one of Ethiopia's everyday comfort stews, especially important on fasting tables. Chickpea flour thickens quickly, so the cook's job is steady stirring and patient seasoning.
recipes.occasion: Best for fasting meals, quick dinners, and injera platters.
recipes.best_served_with: Injera, gomen, misir wat, or atkilt wat.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions are mild and garlicky, while others are rich with berbere, niter kibbeh, or tomato.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

ingredients.name: neutral oil
ingredients.substitution: niter kibbeh for a non-vegan version

steps.title: Cook the onion
steps.instruction: Cook onion in oil until soft and lightly golden, then add garlic and berbere.

steps.title: Whisk the shiro
steps.instruction: Whisk shiro powder with 1 cup warm water until smooth.

steps.title: Simmer smooth
steps.instruction: Add the shiro mixture and remaining water to the pot while stirring. Simmer until thick and glossy.
steps.tip: Keep the heat moderate so the flour cooks without sticking.

steps.title: Adjust and serve
steps.instruction: Adjust salt and thickness with hot water, then serve with injera.

recipes.name: Tibs
recipes.description: Ethiopian-style sauteed beef with onion, garlic, rosemary, berbere, niter kibbeh, and optional awaze for heat.
recipes.story: Tibs sits between a quick saute and a feast dish: hot pan, tender meat, onion, chile, rosemary, niter kibbeh, and berbere or awaze depending on the cook. It should taste bold but still let the browned meat lead.
recipes.occasion: Best for a fast celebratory supper, injera platter, or special lunch.
recipes.best_served_with: Injera, awaze, salad, shiro, or mild vegetable sides.
recipes.regional_variations: Some tibs are mild and buttery, some are spicy with berbere or awaze, and some are served sizzling with peppers and onions.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

ingredients.name: niter kibbeh
ingredients.substitution: ghee with a pinch of cumin and cardamom

steps.title: Season the meat
steps.instruction: Pat the meat dry and season with salt, pepper, and half the berbere.
steps.tip: Dry meat browns faster and stays juicier.

steps.title: Sear hot
steps.instruction: Heat half the niter kibbeh in a wide pan. Sear the meat in batches until browned outside but not fully cooked through.

steps.title: Cook the aromatics
steps.instruction: Add remaining niter kibbeh, onion, garlic, ginger, chile, rosemary, and tomato if using. Cook until the onion softens.

steps.title: Finish the tibs
steps.instruction: Return the meat to the pan with remaining berbere and optional awaze. Toss over high heat until glossy and just cooked.
steps.tip: Serve immediately so the meat stays tender.

recipes.name: Smoked Fish with Plantains
recipes.description: A simple Gabonese-style plate of fried ripe plantains topped with smoked fish simmered in tomato, onion, garlic, and chile.
recipes.story: This is everyday coastal cooking: smoky fish, sweet plantain, and a quick tomato sauce that makes the plate feel complete.
recipes.occasion: Weeknight dinner, everyday meals
recipes.best_served_with: Steamed rice, cassava, cucumber salad, or extra fried plantain
recipes.regional_variations: Some homes keep the sauce dry and chunky; others loosen it with fish stock and serve it over rice.
recipes.image_alt: Smoked Fish with Plantains recipe from Gabon on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Fry plantains
steps.instruction: Fry plantain slices in oil until golden on both sides. Drain and keep warm.

steps.title: Cook the sauce
steps.instruction: Pour off excess oil, leaving a little in the pan. Cook onion until soft, then add garlic, tomato, chile, salt, and pepper.

steps.title: Add smoked fish
steps.instruction: Add smoked fish and water or stock. Simmer gently until the fish flavors the sauce.
steps.tip: Stir gently so the fish stays in flakes.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Arrange plantains on a plate and spoon the smoked fish sauce over the top.

recipes.name: Benachin
recipes.description: Gambian one-pot tomato rice cooked with fish, cassava, sweet potato, cabbage, carrot, chile, and a rich seasoned broth.
recipes.story: Benachin means one pot, and the pot should taste complete before the rice goes in. Fry the fish, build a tomato broth, cook the vegetables, then let the rice absorb everything.
recipes.occasion: Family meals
recipes.best_served_with: Lime wedges, fresh chile sauce, cucumber salad, or tapalapa bread
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks use fish, others use chicken or meat. Vegetables change with the market, but cassava, cabbage, carrot, and sweet potato are common companions.
recipes.image_alt: Benachin recipe from Gambia on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

ingredients.name: sweet potato
ingredients.prep_note: peeled and cut into large chunks

ingredients.name: cassava
ingredients.prep_note: peeled and cut into large chunks

steps.title: Season and fry fish
steps.instruction: Season fish with salt and pepper. Fry in hot oil until browned on both sides, then remove.

steps.title: Build tomato broth
steps.instruction: In the same pot, cook onions until soft. Add garlic and tomato paste, then tomatoes, hot pepper, and water or stock.

steps.title: Cook vegetables
steps.instruction: Add cassava, sweet potato, carrot, and cabbage. Simmer until just tender, then lift the vegetables out and keep warm.

steps.title: Cook rice
steps.instruction: Stir rice into the broth. Place fish and vegetables on top, cover tightly, and cook on low until the rice absorbs the broth.

steps.title: Serve family style
steps.instruction: Rest 10 minutes, then serve the rice on a platter with fish and vegetables arranged over it. Add lime if using.

collections.name: One-Pot Wonders
collections.description: Hearty single-pot dishes that are easy to make and full of flavor. Less cleanup, more taste.

recipes.name: Gambian Chicken Yassa
recipes.description: Gambian chicken marinated in mustard, vinegar or lemon, onion, garlic, pepper, then fried and simmered with onion sauce.
recipes.story: Yassa belongs to the Senegambian table, and in The Gambia it is a trusted chicken-and-rice plate built on sharp onions, mustard, and a good fry before the simmer.
recipes.occasion: Best for Sunday rice, gatherings, and onion lovers.
recipes.best_served_with: White rice, cucumber salad, or fried plantain.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks use vinegar, some use lemon, some add carrots or green pepper, and fish yassa is also common.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Marinate chicken
steps.instruction: Mix chicken with onions, mustard, lemon or vinegar, garlic, ginger, pepper, salt, and black pepper.

steps.title: Brown chicken
steps.instruction: Lift chicken from the onions and fry until golden on both sides.

steps.title: Cook onion sauce
steps.instruction: Cook the marinated onions in the same pot until soft and lightly caramelized.

steps.title: Simmer together
steps.instruction: Return chicken to the pot with stock and simmer until tender and coated in onion sauce.

recipes.name: Nyambeh Nyebeh
recipes.description: Gambian cassava and beans served with a spicy onion-pepper stew, often eaten for breakfast or dinner.
recipes.story: Nyambeh means cassava and nyebeh means beans, and that tells the truth of the plate: humble roots and legumes made generous with stew.
recipes.occasion: Best for breakfast plates, dinner bowls, and street-food style meals.
recipes.best_served_with: Fried fish, onion gravy, pepper sauce, or roasted sweet potato.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks boil cassava and beans separately, some add sweet potato or plantain, and some finish the stew with fish.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Boil beans
steps.instruction: Simmer soaked beans until tender, then drain and season lightly.

steps.title: Boil cassava
steps.instruction: Boil cassava in salted water until tender but still holding shape.

steps.title: Make stew
steps.instruction: Cook onions, garlic, tomato paste, tomatoes, green pepper, and hot pepper into a thick sauce.

steps.title: Plate
steps.instruction: Serve cassava and beans with the spicy stew spooned generously over the top.

recipes.name: Plasas
recipes.description: Gambian leafy stew with spinach or cassava leaves, peanut, palm oil, smoked fish, and chili.
recipes.story: Plasas is Gambian leaf stew comfort: greens cooked down with peanut richness, smoked fish depth, and enough pepper to wake up rice or fufu.
recipes.occasion: Best for rice plates, family lunch, and greens-heavy dinners.
recipes.best_served_with: White rice, fufu, boiled cassava, or plantain.
recipes.regional_variations: Cassava leaves, spinach, bitter leaves, fish, chicken, beef, or vegetarian peanut versions all appear across households.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Soften aromatics
steps.instruction: Cook onion, garlic, and pepper in palm oil until fragrant.

steps.title: Cook the greens
steps.instruction: Add greens and stock, then simmer until tender and reduced.

steps.title: Add peanut and fish
steps.instruction: Stir in peanut butter and smoked fish. Simmer until thick and rich.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Adjust seasoning and serve hot over rice or with fufu.

recipes.name: Supakanja
recipes.description: Gambian okra soup with palm oil, dried fish, shrimp, chile, and a silky draw texture for rice or fufu.
recipes.story: Supakanja should be silky and generous. Slice the okra finely, let it draw in the palm oil, then add fish and seafood without overcooking them.
recipes.occasion: Everyday meals
recipes.best_served_with: White rice, fufu, cassava, or rice balls
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks add leafy greens, crab, smoked fish, beef, or extra dried seafood.
recipes.image_alt: Supakanja recipe from Gambia on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Draw the okra
steps.instruction: Heat palm oil and add sliced okra. Cook, stirring, until glossy and beginning to draw.

steps.title: Add fish and aromatics
steps.instruction: Add onion, dried fish, and peppers. Stir gently until fragrant.

steps.title: Simmer
steps.instruction: Add water or fish stock and simmer until the soup thickens. Add greens if using.

steps.title: Finish seafood
steps.instruction: Add shrimp or seafood and cook just until done. Season with salt.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve hot with rice or fufu, making sure each bowl has fish, okra, and broth.

recipes.name: Banku and Tilapia
recipes.description: A Ghanaian plate of smooth fermented corn and cassava dough served with seasoned grilled tilapia and pepper sauce.
recipes.story: Banku is a Ghanaian staple made by cooking fermented corn and cassava dough into a smooth, elastic mound. Paired with grilled tilapia and pepper sauce, it becomes one of Ghana's most recognizable restaurant and roadside plates.
recipes.occasion: Best for weekend grilling, coastal-style meals, and Ghanaian restaurant plates.
recipes.best_served_with: Shito, fresh pepper sauce, sliced onion, tomatoes, and cucumber.
recipes.regional_variations: Banku may be served with okra stew, soup, pepper sauce, grilled fish, or fried fish. The tilapia seasoning varies by cook.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Season the fish
steps.instruction: Rub scored tilapia with ginger, garlic, onion, scotch bonnet if using, oil, and salt. Let it sit while you start the banku.

steps.title: Start the banku
steps.instruction: Whisk corn dough, cassava dough, water, and salt in a heavy pot until smooth. Cook over medium heat, stirring constantly.

steps.title: Steam the banku
steps.instruction: When the dough thickens, reduce heat, splash in a little water, cover briefly, then stir again until smooth and elastic.
steps.tip: A strong wooden spoon helps. Banku should be smooth, not grainy.

steps.title: Grill the tilapia
steps.instruction: Grill or broil the tilapia, turning once, until cooked through and lightly charred.

steps.title: Plate
steps.instruction: Shape banku into balls and serve with grilled tilapia, shito or pepper sauce, and sliced vegetables.

recipes.name: Fufu with Light Soup
recipes.description: Ghanaian cassava-plantain fufu served with peppery tomato-based light soup and goat, chicken, or fish.
recipes.story: Fufu and light soup is a Ghanaian classic built on contrast: elastic swallow, thin peppery soup, and deeply simmered meat or fish.
recipes.occasion: Best for Sunday lunch, celebratory family meals, and cold-weather soup days.
recipes.best_served_with: Goat light soup, chicken light soup, or fish light soup.
recipes.regional_variations: Fufu may use cassava with plantain, yam, or cocoyam, and light soup can be goat, chicken, fish, or mixed meat.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Simmer meat
steps.instruction: Cook meat with onion, ginger, garlic, salt, and water until it begins to soften.

steps.title: Blend soup base
steps.instruction: Simmer tomatoes and peppers in the pot, lift them out, blend with onion, then return to the soup.

steps.title: Finish light soup
steps.instruction: Continue simmering until meat is tender and the broth is peppery but still light.

steps.title: Make fufu
steps.instruction: Boil cassava and plantain until tender, then pound or process into a smooth elastic mound.

recipes.name: Ga Kenkey with Fried Fish
recipes.description: Ga-style fermented corn dumplings served with fried fish, pepper sauce, and fresh tomato-onion relish.
recipes.story: Ga kenkey is Accra street-food royalty: fermented corn dough, steamed tight, then eaten with fish, pepper, and fresh relish.
recipes.occasion: Best for weekend street-food plates, fish lunches, and coastal Ghana menus.
recipes.best_served_with: Fried fish, shito, fresh pepper sauce, tomato, onion, and avocado.
recipes.regional_variations: Ga kenkey is usually salted and wrapped, while Fante kenkey is often less salty and wrapped differently.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Cook a dough starter
steps.instruction: Cook one-third of the corn dough with water into a thick porridge, then mix it back into the raw dough with salt.

steps.title: Wrap and steam
steps.instruction: Shape dough portions, wrap in corn husks or foil, and steam until firm.

steps.title: Fry fish
steps.instruction: Season fish with salt and fry until crisp outside and cooked through.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve kenkey warm with fried fish, pepper sauce, tomato, and onion.

recipes.name: Groundnut Soup
recipes.description: Ghanaian groundnut soup with chicken, peanut paste, tomato, ginger, chile, and a silky broth for fufu or rice balls.
recipes.story: Groundnut soup should pour like soup, not sit like stew. Cook the tomato base down, loosen the peanut paste with stock, then simmer until the oil shines gently on top.
recipes.occasion: Everyday meals
recipes.best_served_with: Fufu, omo tuo, rice balls, boiled yam, or kokonte
recipes.regional_variations: Chicken, goat, fish, smoked fish, garden eggs, or okra may move through the pot depending on the household.
recipes.image_alt: Groundnut Soup recipe from Ghana on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

ingredients.name: natural peanut butter or groundnut paste

steps.title: Season chicken
steps.instruction: Season chicken with salt, pepper, onion, garlic, and ginger. Simmer with stock or water until partly tender.

steps.title: Cook tomato base
steps.instruction: Blend tomatoes and chile. Cook with tomato paste until reduced and no longer raw.

steps.title: Loosen groundnut
steps.instruction: Whisk peanut paste with hot stock until smooth, then add it to the soup.

steps.title: Simmer soup
steps.instruction: Return chicken and remaining stock to the pot. Simmer gently until the chicken is tender and the soup is silky.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Taste for salt and serve hot with fufu, omo tuo, or yam.

recipes.name: Kelewele
recipes.description: Ghanaian ripe plantain cubes marinated with ginger, garlic, chile, cloves, nutmeg, and salt, then fried until caramelized.
recipes.story: Kelewele should hit sweet, hot, and warm-spiced at once. Let the plantain sit briefly in the paste so the edges fry dark and fragrant.
recipes.occasion: Evening snack
recipes.best_served_with: Roasted peanuts, grilled meat, beans, red red, or a cold malt drink
recipes.regional_variations: Some spice pastes include onion, anise, grains of selim, or calabash nutmeg.
recipes.image_alt: Kelewele recipe from Ghana on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make spice paste
steps.instruction: Mix ginger, garlic, onion if using, chile, cloves, nutmeg, and salt into a rough paste.

steps.title: Season plantain
steps.instruction: Toss plantain cubes with the spice paste and rest briefly.

steps.title: Fry in batches
steps.instruction: Heat oil and fry plantain in batches until dark golden and crisp at the edges.

steps.title: Drain and serve
steps.instruction: Drain well and serve hot with roasted peanuts or beans.

collections.name: Quick & Easy
collections.description: Delicious African dishes ready in 30 minutes or less. Perfect for busy weeknight dinners.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

collections.name: Street Food
collections.description: Popular street snacks and quick bites found at markets and roadside stalls across Africa.

recipes.name: Kontomire Stew
recipes.description: Ghanaian cocoyam leaf stew with palm oil, egusi, smoked fish, tomatoes, onion, chile, and tender greens.
recipes.story: Kontomire stew is one of Ghana's beloved green stews, often cooked with cocoyam leaves, palm oil, egusi, smoked fish, and a tomato-pepper base. It is earthy, rich, and built for yam, plantain, rice, or ampesi.
recipes.occasion: Best for weekday family meals, yam and plantain plates, or a hearty lunch.
recipes.best_served_with: Boiled yam, ripe plantain, rice, ampesi, or gari.
recipes.regional_variations: Spinach can stand in for cocoyam leaves when needed. Some cooks use salted fish, eggs, snails, beef, or no animal protein at all.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

ingredients.name: egusi or agushi seeds
ingredients.prep_note: ground and mixed with a little water

steps.title: Prep the greens
steps.instruction: Rinse and chop the kontomire leaves. If using mature leaves, blanch briefly and drain.

steps.title: Cook the tomato base
steps.instruction: Heat palm oil and cook onions until soft. Add tomato, chile, ginger, and garlic, then simmer until the sauce thickens.

steps.title: Set the egusi
steps.instruction: Spoon in the egusi paste and let it steam for a few minutes before gently stirring into curds.
steps.tip: Letting egusi set first gives the stew body.

steps.title: Finish the stew
steps.instruction: Add smoked fish and greens. Cover and cook until tender, then adjust salt and serve.

recipes.name: Omo Tuo
recipes.description: Ghanaian rice balls cooked very soft, beaten smooth, and served with groundnut soup or palm nut soup.
recipes.story: Omo Tuo is simple in ingredients but serious in texture: soft rice beaten until smooth enough to roll into balls that drink soup properly.
recipes.occasion: Best for Sunday groundnut soup, palm nut soup, and family lunch.
recipes.best_served_with: Nkate nkwan, palm nut soup, chicken, goat, or fish.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks use broken rice, some use long-grain rice cooked with extra water, and ball size changes by household.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Cook rice soft
steps.instruction: Boil rice with extra water until very soft and slightly overcooked.

steps.title: Beat rice
steps.instruction: Lower the heat and beat the rice firmly with a wooden spoon until sticky and smooth.

steps.title: Shape
steps.instruction: Use wet hands or a wet bowl to shape the rice into smooth balls.

steps.title: Serve with soup
steps.instruction: Serve hot with groundnut soup or palm nut soup.

recipes.name: Red Red
recipes.description: A Ghanaian black-eyed pea stew cooked with red palm oil and served with fried ripe plantain.
recipes.story: Red Red gets its name from the red palm oil and the red-orange fried plantain often served beside the beans. The stew should be hearty and glossy, with black-eyed peas carrying onion, tomato, chile, garlic, and ginger.
recipes.occasion: Best for lunch, vegetarian plates, street-food style meals, and hearty weekday dinners.
recipes.best_served_with: Fried ripe plantain, gari, rice, avocado, or boiled egg.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks add fish powder, smoked fish, or shrimp powder, while vegan versions keep the stew bean-forward.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Cook the beans
steps.instruction: Simmer black-eyed peas in water until tender but not collapsed. Reserve a little cooking liquid, then drain.

steps.title: Build the stew base
steps.instruction: Heat palm oil, then cook onion, garlic, ginger, scotch bonnet, tomatoes, and tomato paste until the sauce thickens.

steps.title: Stew the beans
steps.instruction: Stir beans into the sauce with a splash of reserved cooking liquid. Simmer until glossy and well coated. Season with salt.

steps.title: Fry the plantain
steps.instruction: Fry ripe plantain slices in oil until caramelized and golden. Serve beside the beans.

recipes.name: Tuo Zaafi with Ayoyo Soup
recipes.description: Northern Ghanaian maize swallow served with slippery ayoyo soup and a tomato-pepper stew.
recipes.story: Tuo Zaafi is northern Ghana on a plate: smooth maize dough, ayoyo's draw, and a stew that carries meat, pepper, and dawadawa depth.
recipes.occasion: Best for lunch, northern Ghanaian family meals, and soup-and-swallow nights.
recipes.best_served_with: Ayoyo soup, tomato stew, goat, beef, or fish.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks use maize flour only, some add cassava flour, and ayoyo may be cooked with dawadawa, okra, or local potash.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Cook stew
steps.instruction: Simmer meat with onion, tomato, pepper, oil, and salt until tender and saucy.

steps.title: Cook ayoyo
steps.instruction: Simmer ayoyo with dawadawa, pepper, and a little water until slippery and bright green.

steps.title: Make tuo
steps.instruction: Whisk maize flour into water, cook to a porridge, then beat in cassava flour until smooth and stretchy.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Shape Tuo Zaafi into mounds and serve with ayoyo soup and stew.

recipes.name: Waakye
recipes.description: A Ghanaian rice and beans dish cooked with dried sorghum or millet leaves for its signature reddish-brown color.
recipes.story: Waakye is one of Ghana's great rice-and-beans dishes, sold from street carts and cooked at home. The signature color comes from sorghum or millet leaves, with baking soda sometimes used as a substitute when leaves are unavailable.
recipes.occasion: Best for breakfast, lunch packs, street-food plates, and weekend family meals.
recipes.best_served_with: Shito, tomato stew, gari, spaghetti, boiled egg, fried plantain, salad, fish, beef, or chicken.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks use red beans rather than black-eyed peas. Accompaniments vary from simple shito and egg to fully loaded street plates.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

ingredients.name: baking soda
ingredients.substitution: use only if waakye leaves are unavailable

steps.title: Color the cooking water
steps.instruction: Rinse the sorghum leaves and simmer them in water until the water turns reddish-brown.

steps.title: Cook the beans
steps.instruction: Add black-eyed peas to the colored water and simmer until nearly tender.

steps.title: Add rice
steps.instruction: Remove the leaves, stir in rice and salt, cover tightly, and cook on low until the rice is tender and the beans are cooked through.

steps.title: Rest and serve
steps.instruction: Let the pot rest covered, then fluff gently and serve with shito, stew, eggs, plantain, or protein.

recipes.name: Fouti
recipes.description: Light steamed fonio shaped into a soft mound for Guinean sauces, especially leaf sauce or peanut sauce.
recipes.story: Fouti is a gentle staple. Rinse the fonio well, steam it lightly, and shape it only enough to carry the sauce.
recipes.occasion: Honoured guest meals
recipes.best_served_with: Sauce feuille, peanut sauce, fish stew, or tomato vegetable sauce
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks enrich the fonio with extra fat, but oil keeps the dish plant-based.
recipes.image_alt: Fouti recipe from Guinea on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Wash the fonio
steps.instruction: Rinse fonio in several changes of water until the water runs clear. Drain thoroughly.
steps.tip: Use a fine-mesh sieve because fonio is tiny.

steps.title: Cook the fonio
steps.instruction: Bring water to a boil with salt and vegetable oil. Add the fonio, stir once, cover, and reduce heat to low.

steps.title: Steam and fluff
steps.instruction: Remove from heat and let steam covered. Fluff gently so the grains stay separate and light.

steps.title: Shape and serve
steps.instruction: Pack fonio into a wet bowl and turn out as a neat mound. Serve alongside sauces and stews.

collections.name: Quick & Easy
collections.description: Delicious African dishes ready in 30 minutes or less. Perfect for busy weeknight dinners.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Sauce Feuille
recipes.description: Guinean leaf sauce with sweet potato leaves, palm oil, smoked fish, ground peanuts, garlic, and hot pepper.
recipes.story: Sauce feuille should be green, smoky, and rounded. Cook the leaves until tender, then let peanuts and palm oil thicken the pot.
recipes.occasion: Everyday meals
recipes.best_served_with: White rice, fouti, cassava, or boiled plantain
recipes.regional_variations: Cassava leaves, sweet potato leaves, spinach, or mixed greens can be used depending on what is available.
recipes.image_alt: Sauce Feuille recipe from Guinea on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

ingredients.name: sweet potato leaves or cassava leaves

steps.title: Cook leaves
steps.instruction: Simmer chopped leaves in salted water until tender, then drain lightly.

steps.title: Start the sauce
steps.instruction: Heat palm oil. Cook onion and garlic until soft, then add smoked fish.

steps.title: Add peanuts
steps.instruction: Mix ground peanuts with water or stock, then stir into the pot with the leaves and peppers.

steps.title: Simmer thick
steps.instruction: Cook gently until the sauce thickens and the greens are fully tender.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Taste for salt and pepper. Serve hot over rice or with fouti.

recipes.name: Caldo de Chabeu
recipes.description: Guinea-Bissau palm-oil broth with okra, tomato, onion, chile, and fish or meat, served over rice.
recipes.story: Chabeu is not rice pretending to be jollof. It is the sauce, red from palm oil and thickened with okra, made to ladle generously over rice.
recipes.occasion: Family meals
recipes.best_served_with: Plain white rice, cassava, or boiled plantain
recipes.regional_variations: Fish, dried fish, chicken, beef, or shellfish can anchor the broth depending on the coast and household.
recipes.image_alt: Chabéu recipe from Guinea-Bissau on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Start the palm base
steps.instruction: Heat palm oil and cook onions until soft. Add garlic, tomatoes, and hot pepper.

steps.title: Add okra
steps.instruction: Stir in okra and cook until it begins to thicken the oil and tomato base.

steps.title: Simmer broth
steps.instruction: Add water or fish stock and bring to a simmer. Add fish gently and season with salt and pepper.

steps.title: Finish
steps.instruction: Cook until the fish is done and the broth is glossy red. Keep it spoonable, not dry.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Ladle the chabeu over plain white rice or serve with cassava.

collections.name: One-Pot Wonders
collections.description: Hearty single-pot dishes that are easy to make and full of flavor. Less cleanup, more taste.

recipes.name: Cashew Rice
recipes.description: Long-grain rice folded with toasted cashews, onion, and light aromatics as a Guinea-Bissau-inspired side.
recipes.story: Cashews are one of Guinea-Bissau's defining crops. Keep this rice simple so the toasted nuts stay the point.
recipes.occasion: Everyday side dish
recipes.best_served_with: Grilled chicken, fish stew, caldo de mancarra, or tomato salad
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks use plain oil, others enrich the rice with butter or stock.
recipes.image_alt: Cashew Rice recipe from Guinea-Bissau on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Toast cashews
steps.instruction: Toast cashews in a dry pan until golden and fragrant. Remove and reserve.

steps.title: Start rice
steps.instruction: Heat oil and cook onion until soft. Add rice and stir until lightly coated.

steps.title: Cook
steps.instruction: Add water or stock and salt. Cover and cook until the rice is tender.

steps.title: Finish
steps.instruction: Rest 10 minutes, fluff, and fold in toasted cashews. Add butter only if using.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

collections.name: One-Pot Wonders
collections.description: Hearty single-pot dishes that are easy to make and full of flavor. Less cleanup, more taste.

recipes.name: Alloco
recipes.description: Ivorian fried ripe plantain served hot with onion-tomato piment, a street-food plate that works as snack or side.
recipes.story: Alloco should taste sweet, salty, and hot. Use plantains with black spots, fry them in batches, and do not skip the piment.
recipes.occasion: Street-food snacks
recipes.best_served_with: Grilled fish, boiled eggs, roasted peanuts, or extra piment
recipes.regional_variations: Some sellers serve alloco with fried fish, hard-boiled eggs, peanuts, or a raw onion and chile relish.
recipes.image_alt: Alloco recipe from Ivory Coast on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Slice plantain
steps.instruction: Peel the ripe plantains and slice them diagonally about 1 cm thick. Sprinkle lightly with salt.

steps.title: Fry in batches
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a deep pan. Fry plantain slices in batches until both sides are deep golden, then drain on paper.

steps.title: Make piment
steps.instruction: Mix tomato, onion, scotch bonnet, garlic if using, salt, and a squeeze of lime. Keep it fresh and sharp.

steps.title: Serve hot
steps.instruction: Pile alloco onto a platter and serve with piment on the side or spooned over the top.

collections.name: Quick & Easy
collections.description: Delicious African dishes ready in 30 minutes or less. Perfect for busy weeknight dinners.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

collections.name: Street Food
collections.description: Popular street snacks and quick bites found at markets and roadside stalls across Africa.

recipes.name: Foutou Sauce Graine
recipes.description: Ivorian pounded plantain or yam foutou served with rich palm nut sauce, smoked meat, fish, and chile.
recipes.story: Foutou sauce graine is one of the plates that makes an Ivorian table feel complete: elastic pounded starch and a red palm sauce with smoke, chile, and depth.
recipes.occasion: Best for Sunday lunch, maquis-style meals, and special family cooking.
recipes.best_served_with: Foutou banane, foutou igname, placali, or rice.
recipes.regional_variations: The sauce may include smoked beef, agouti, fish, crab, adjovan, or green chilies depending on place and budget.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

ingredients.name: palm nut juice or diluted palm cream

steps.title: Cook protein
steps.instruction: Simmer smoked meat with onion, tomato, pepper, and a little water until tender.

steps.title: Add palm base
steps.instruction: Add palm nut juice and smoked fish, then simmer uncovered until the sauce thickens.

steps.title: Make foutou
steps.instruction: Boil plantain and yam until tender, then pound together into a smooth elastic dough.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve foutou with hot sauce graine and extra pepper on the side.

recipes.name: Garba
recipes.description: Abidjan street-food plate of attieke, fried tuna, raw onion-tomato-pepper relish, oil, and seasoning.
recipes.story: Garba is Abidjan speed, attitude, and satisfaction: sour cassava couscous, fried tuna, fresh pepper, onion, and that unmistakable street-food oil gloss.
recipes.occasion: Best for street-food nights, fast lunches, and game-day plates.
recipes.best_served_with: Extra chili, sliced onion, and chilled bissap.
recipes.regional_variations: Some vendors dust tuna with flour, some keep it plain, and the attieke may be heavily oiled and seasoned or kept lighter.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Steam attieke
steps.instruction: Sprinkle attieke with water and steam until fluffy, then toss with a little oil and salt.

steps.title: Season tuna
steps.instruction: Salt tuna, dust lightly with flour, and shake off excess.

steps.title: Fry tuna
steps.instruction: Fry tuna until golden outside and just cooked through.

steps.title: Assemble
steps.instruction: Serve attieke with tuna, onion, tomato, chili, oil, and seasoning.

recipes.name: Placali Sauce Kope
recipes.description: Ivorian fermented cassava placali served with slippery okra sauce, crab, smoked fish, meat, and chile.
recipes.story: Placali and sauce kope is texture food at its finest: sour-soft cassava paste meeting okra's draw and the deep savor of crab, smoked fish, and meat.
recipes.occasion: Best for weekend Ivorian tables and serious soup-and-swallow plates.
recipes.best_served_with: Extra chili, smoked fish, crab, or kplo if available.
recipes.regional_variations: Sauce kope may include crab, snails, beef feet, smoked fish, or only fish; placali can be bought ready-made or cooked from fermented cassava dough.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Cook proteins
steps.instruction: Simmer meat or seafood with onion, tomato, pepper, and stock until tender.

steps.title: Add okra
steps.instruction: Stir in grated okra and palm oil, then simmer until the sauce becomes glossy and lightly stretchy.

steps.title: Cook placali
steps.instruction: Whisk cassava dough with water over medium heat, stirring constantly until translucent and elastic.

steps.title: Serve hot
steps.instruction: Shape placali and serve with sauce kope spooned around it.

recipes.name: Githeri
recipes.description: A Kenyan maize and beans dish, often simmered plain or finished with onion, tomato, and vegetables.
recipes.ethnic_group: Gikuyu, Meru, Mbeere, Embu and wider Kenya
recipes.story: Githeri is a Kenyan staple built on boiled maize and beans. Many homes eat it simply salted, while contemporary versions fry onion, tomato, vegetables, and spices around the cooked maize and beans.
recipes.occasion: Best for weekday meals, school-style bowls, and batch cooking.
recipes.best_served_with: Kachumbari, avocado, sukuma wiki, or a little chile sauce.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions remain plain maize and beans; others add potatoes, carrots, greens, curry powder, or tomato.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

ingredients.name: beans
ingredients.prep_note: kidney beans or rose coco, soaked if dry

steps.title: Boil maize and beans
steps.instruction: Simmer maize and beans in water until both are tender. Add more water if needed and season with salt near the end.
steps.tip: Dry maize and dry beans can take much longer. Pre-cooked maize and beans shorten the recipe.

steps.title: Make the tomato finish
steps.instruction: Heat oil, then fry onion until soft. Add tomatoes, carrots if using, and curry powder if using. Cook until saucy.

steps.title: Combine
steps.instruction: Add drained maize and beans to the tomato finish. Stir with a splash of cooking liquid and simmer until flavors come together.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Finish with coriander if using and serve hot with kachumbari or avocado.

recipes.name: Kenyan Pilau
recipes.description: Kenyan beef pilau with browned onions, garlic, ginger, whole spices, pilau masala, and rice steamed in rich stock.
recipes.story: Good pilau starts before the rice. Build a proper beef stock, brown the onions patiently, and let the rice steam without too much stirring.
recipes.occasion: Family celebrations
recipes.best_served_with: Kachumbari, lemon wedges, mango pickle, or plain cabbage salad
recipes.regional_variations: Coastal pilau leans aromatic and spice-forward; some Kenyan homes add potatoes, tomatoes, or extra chile.
recipes.image_alt: Kenyan Pilau recipe from Kenya on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Cook beef stock
steps.instruction: Simmer beef with half the garlic, half the ginger, salt, pepper, and enough water to cover until nearly tender. Reserve the meat and stock.

steps.title: Brown onions
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a heavy pot and cook onions slowly until deep golden brown.

steps.title: Bloom spices
steps.instruction: Add remaining garlic and ginger, pilau masala, cumin, cardamom, cinnamon, and cloves. Stir until fragrant.

steps.title: Coat rice
steps.instruction: Return beef to the pot. Add drained rice and stir gently until each grain is coated in the spice oil.

steps.title: Steam pilau
steps.instruction: Pour in hot stock, adjust salt, bring to a boil, then cover tightly and cook on the lowest heat until the rice absorbs the liquid.

steps.title: Rest and fluff
steps.instruction: Rest covered off heat, then fluff from the edges inward. Serve with kachumbari.

collections.name: Party Favorites
collections.description: Festive dishes that bring people together. These crowd-pleasers are served at celebrations across Africa.

collections.name: One-Pot Wonders
collections.description: Hearty single-pot dishes that are easy to make and full of flavor. Less cleanup, more taste.

recipes.name: Mukimo
recipes.description: A Kenyan mashed potato dish with maize, peas or beans, and green vegetables, often served with stew or nyama choma.
recipes.story: Mukimo is a comforting Kenyan mash associated especially with central Kenya. Potatoes give it body, maize brings chew, peas or beans add sweetness, and pumpkin leaves, spinach, or amaranth give the mash its green lift.
recipes.occasion: Best for family meals, ceremonies, roast meat plates, and stew dinners.
recipes.best_served_with: Beef stew, nyama choma, chicken stew, kachumbari, or sauteed greens.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions use peas, others beans. Pumpkin leaves are traditional, while spinach is a practical substitute.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Boil potatoes and peas
steps.instruction: Boil potatoes and peas in salted water until the potatoes are tender.

steps.title: Cook the greens
steps.instruction: Add chopped pumpkin leaves or spinach and cook until tender and bright green.

steps.title: Mash
steps.instruction: Drain excess water, add boiled maize, then mash until combined. Leave some maize kernels whole for texture.

steps.title: Finish
steps.instruction: If using onion, fry it in butter or oil and fold into the mash. Taste for salt and serve hot.

recipes.name: Nyama Choma
recipes.description: Kenyan charcoal-grilled goat or beef, salted simply, roasted slowly, chopped hot, and served with ugali and kachumbari.
recipes.story: Nyama choma is about good meat, patient fire, and a fresh table. Keep the seasoning simple enough for the smoke to speak.
recipes.occasion: Weekend gatherings
recipes.best_served_with: Ugali, kachumbari, sukuma wiki, and pili pili
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks add garlic, ginger, paprika, or lemon, but the classic Kenyan approach is salt, good meat, and charcoal.
recipes.image_alt: Nyama Choma recipe from Kenya on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Salt the meat
steps.instruction: Rub meat with salt, pepper if using, and a little lemon or oil if you like. Let it sit while the fire settles.

steps.title: Prepare charcoal
steps.instruction: Build a charcoal fire and wait until the coals are glowing and ashed over. Set up a medium heat zone.

steps.title: Grill slowly
steps.instruction: Grill the meat slowly, turning often, until browned outside and cooked through near the bone.

steps.title: Make kachumbari
steps.instruction: Toss tomatoes, onion, chile if using, cilantro, lime juice, and salt. Keep it crisp and fresh.

steps.title: Rest and chop
steps.instruction: Rest the meat briefly, then chop into pieces and serve with kachumbari and ugali.

collections.name: Sunday Specials
collections.description: Elaborate weekend dishes worth the extra effort. These are the recipes that make Sunday lunch special.

recipes.name: Motoho
recipes.description: Basotho sour sorghum porridge made by fermenting a sorghum slurry overnight, then cooking it smooth for breakfast.
recipes.story: Motoho needs time more than fuss. Let the sorghum sour gently overnight, then cook it slowly until the raw edge disappears.
recipes.occasion: Cold highland mornings
recipes.best_served_with: Plain, lightly sweetened, or with milk offered separately
recipes.regional_variations: Some households back-slop with a little previous motoho; others use sorghum malt or natural fermentation.
recipes.image_alt: Motoho recipe from Lesotho on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

ingredients.name: sorghum malt or previous motoho starter

steps.title: Make slurry
steps.instruction: Whisk sorghum flour with lukewarm water until smooth. Stir in sorghum malt or starter if using.

steps.title: Ferment
steps.instruction: Cover loosely and leave in a warm place until pleasantly sour and lightly bubbly.

steps.title: Cook porridge
steps.instruction: Bring the remaining water to a boil. Whisk in the fermented slurry slowly and reduce the heat.

steps.title: Simmer smooth
steps.instruction: Simmer, stirring often, until the porridge is smooth and the raw sorghum taste is gone.

steps.title: Finish
steps.instruction: Season lightly with salt or sugar if using. Serve warm, or cool to drink as a thinner sour porridge.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Nyekoe
recipes.description: Basotho bean, sorghum, and pumpkin stew simmered slowly until thick, earthy, and gently sweet.
recipes.story: Nyekoe is patient mountain food. Soak the beans, simmer the sorghum, and add pumpkin late so it softens without disappearing completely.
recipes.occasion: Cold-weather family meals
recipes.best_served_with: Papa, moroho, chile relish, or sour milk on the side
recipes.regional_variations: Some homes use samp or cracked maize in place of sorghum; pumpkin or potatoes are common additions for sweetness and body.
recipes.image_alt: Nyekoe recipe from Lesotho on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Soak beans
steps.instruction: Cover beans with plenty of water and soak overnight. Drain before cooking.

steps.title: Start grains
steps.instruction: Combine beans, sorghum, onion, oil, and water in a heavy pot. Bring to a boil.

steps.title: Simmer slowly
steps.instruction: Reduce heat and simmer until the beans and sorghum are nearly tender, adding water as needed.

steps.title: Add pumpkin
steps.instruction: Stir in pumpkin and continue cooking until it is soft and the stew is thick.

steps.title: Season and mash
steps.instruction: Season with salt and pepper. Mash a small portion against the pot to make the stew creamy.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Dumboy
recipes.description: Liberian fresh cassava swallow boiled, pounded smooth, and served with pepper soup, palm butter, or palava sauce.
recipes.story: Dumboy is Liberia's fresh cassava answer to swallow: boiled, pounded, glossy, and ready for any serious soup.
recipes.occasion: Best for pepper soup, palm butter soup, and weekend Liberian meals.
recipes.best_served_with: Pepper soup, palm butter soup, palava sauce, or okra stew.
recipes.regional_variations: Some households pound by hand, others use modern processors, and texture ranges from soft to very elastic.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Boil cassava
steps.instruction: Boil cassava in water until fully tender.

steps.title: Remove fibers
steps.instruction: Drain, remove woody centers, and keep cassava warm.

steps.title: Pound smooth
steps.instruction: Pound or process hot cassava until smooth, elastic, and cohesive.

steps.title: Shape and serve
steps.instruction: Shape into mounds and serve with soup or palm oil.

recipes.name: GB Soup
recipes.description: Liberian pepper soup for dumboy, usually built with meat or fish, okra, bitter balls, onion, tomato, and hot pepper.
recipes.story: GB soup is the partner that makes dumboy sing: peppery broth, meat or fish, okra body, and a clean heat that cuts through cassava.
recipes.occasion: Best for dumboy meals, rainy days, and pepper soup cravings.
recipes.best_served_with: Dumboy, fufu, boiled cassava, or rice.
recipes.regional_variations: Goat, chicken, beef, smoked fish, shrimp, okra, and bitter balls can all appear depending on household and county.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Start broth
steps.instruction: Simmer protein with onion, tomato, pepper, garlic, ginger, salt, and water.

steps.title: Skim and soften
steps.instruction: Skim the broth and continue cooking until the protein is tender.

steps.title: Add vegetables
steps.instruction: Add okra and bitter balls or eggplant and simmer until just tender.

steps.title: Serve brothy
steps.instruction: Adjust pepper and salt, then serve with dumboy.

recipes.name: Liberian Jollof Rice
recipes.description: Liberian jollof rice with chicken, smoked turkey, tomato stew, bell pepper, stock, thyme, bay, and mixed vegetables.
recipes.story: Liberian jollof is generous. The rice should taste of tomato stew and meat stock, with vegetables folded through the pot.
recipes.occasion: Family rice meals
recipes.best_served_with: Fried plantain, pepper sauce, boiled egg, or cabbage salad
recipes.regional_variations: Proteins vary by home: chicken, smoked turkey, beef, shrimp, fish, or a meatless vegetable version.
recipes.image_alt: Liberian Jollof Rice recipe from Liberia on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Cook protein
steps.instruction: Season chicken with salt and pepper, simmer until nearly tender, and reserve the stock. Add smoked turkey or fish later so it does not break apart.

steps.title: Fry tomato base
steps.instruction: Heat oil and cook onions and bell pepper until soft. Add garlic and tomato paste and fry until the paste darkens.

steps.title: Cook stew
steps.instruction: Add tomatoes, thyme, bay leaves, chile if using, and some stock. Simmer until the sauce thickens.

steps.title: Add rice
steps.instruction: Stir rice into the sauce, then add chicken, smoked protein, vegetables, and enough stock to cook the rice.

steps.title: Steam rice
steps.instruction: Cover tightly and cook on low until the rice is tender and the liquid is absorbed.

steps.title: Rest and serve
steps.instruction: Rest covered, fluff gently, and serve with fried plantain and pepper sauce.

collections.name: One-Pot Wonders
collections.description: Hearty single-pot dishes that are easy to make and full of flavor. Less cleanup, more taste.

recipes.name: Liberian Palava Sauce
recipes.description: Liberian leafy stew of greens, okra, chicken, peanut, tomato, and pepper, served with rice or dumboy.
recipes.story: Liberian palava sauce is a leafy stew that knows how to be both practical and lush: greens, okra, pepper, chicken, and peanut body.
recipes.occasion: Best for rice plates, dumboy meals, and family stews.
recipes.best_served_with: White rice, dumboy, fufu, or boiled plantain.
recipes.regional_variations: Greens can be spinach, collards, cassava leaves, or jute; protein can be chicken, smoked fish, beef, or none.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Brown chicken
steps.instruction: Season and brown chicken in oil, then remove briefly.

steps.title: Build sauce
steps.instruction: Cook onion, garlic, ginger, and tomatoes until softened.

steps.title: Simmer stew
steps.instruction: Return chicken with stock and simmer until nearly tender.

steps.title: Finish greens
steps.instruction: Add greens, okra, and loosened peanut butter. Simmer until thick and seasoned.

recipes.name: Asida
recipes.description: Libyan asida, a smooth wheat-flour mound served warm with a well of melted butter, date syrup, or honey.
recipes.story: Asida is humble but not careless. Stir hard until the flour turns elastic, then shape it while warm and let the syrup pool in the center.
recipes.occasion: Mawlid, births, celebrations
recipes.best_served_with: Mint tea, Arabic coffee, date syrup, or honey
recipes.image_alt: Asida dessert from Libya on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Boil the water
steps.instruction: Bring 3 cups of water with a pinch of salt to a rolling boil in a heavy-bottomed pot.

steps.title: Add the flour
steps.instruction: Reduce heat to low. Gradually add the flour while stirring vigorously and continuously with a strong wooden spoon. Keep stirring for about 10 minutes until the mixture forms a thick, smooth, elastic dough that pulls away from the sides of the pot.
steps.tip: Stir with force — the dough needs to be completely lump-free and smooth.

steps.title: Shape the asida
steps.instruction: Wet a serving plate. Turn the asida out onto the plate and shape it into a smooth dome using a wet spoon or wet hands.
steps.tip: Wetting your tools prevents sticking.

steps.title: Create the well
steps.instruction: Make a deep well in the centre of the dome using the back of a spoon. Pour the melted butter into the well, then drizzle honey generously over the top.
steps.tip: The butter and honey pool should be generous — it is the soul of the dish.

steps.title: Serve communally
steps.instruction: Serve warm on a communal plate. Each person tears off a piece of the asida and dips it into the honey-butter well.
steps.tip: Asida is best eaten warm while the butter and honey are still flowing.

collections.name: Quick & Easy
collections.description: Delicious African dishes ready in 30 minutes or less. Perfect for busy weeknight dinners.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Hraime Libyan Fish
recipes.description: Libyan Jewish-style fish simmered in a fiery tomato, garlic, caraway, cumin, paprika, and chili sauce.
recipes.story: Hraime is a Libyan fish dish with a loud, generous sauce: tomato, garlic, paprika, cumin, caraway, lemon, and chili. The fish is simmered just until tender so the sauce stays bright and powerful.
recipes.occasion: Best for Friday night dinners, seafood lunches, and quick spicy mains.
recipes.best_served_with: Bread, couscous, rice, lemon, and chopped cilantro.
recipes.regional_variations: Libyan Jewish versions often emphasize caraway and chili, while nearby North African versions may add more tomato, coriander, or preserved lemon.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Bloom spices
steps.instruction: Cook garlic in oil, then add tomato paste, paprika, cumin, caraway, cayenne, and salt.

steps.title: Make the sauce
steps.instruction: Add crushed tomatoes and water, then simmer until the sauce thickens and smells sweet.

steps.title: Simmer fish
steps.instruction: Nestle fish into the sauce, cover, and simmer gently until just cooked.

steps.title: Finish bright
steps.instruction: Add lemon juice and cilantro, then rest briefly before serving.

recipes.name: Mbakbaka
recipes.description: Libyan one-pot pasta cooked directly in a spicy tomato sauce with lamb or chicken, chickpeas, and warm spices.
recipes.story: Mbakbaka is one of Libya's great everyday one-pot meals. Pasta cooks in the sauce itself, absorbing tomato, chili, meat juices, spices, and chickpeas until every spoonful tastes seasoned all the way through.
recipes.occasion: Best for weeknight family meals, quick guest food, and camping pots.
recipes.best_served_with: Simple salad, olives, pickles, or extra chili.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks use lamb, others chicken or no meat. Pasta shape, chickpeas, potatoes, and chili strength change by household.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Brown the meat
steps.instruction: Cook meat, onion, and garlic in oil until fragrant and lightly browned.

steps.title: Build the sauce
steps.instruction: Add tomato paste, tomatoes, chickpeas, spices, salt, and hot water, then simmer until the meat is nearly tender.

steps.title: Cook pasta in the pot
steps.instruction: Add pasta and simmer, stirring often, until the pasta is tender and saucy.

steps.title: Rest before serving
steps.instruction: Cover off heat for a few minutes so the sauce clings to the pasta.

recipes.name: Mbatten
recipes.description: Libyan stuffed potato wedges filled with spiced minced meat and herbs, coated, and fried until crisp.
recipes.story: Mbatten turns potatoes into a celebration snack: each wedge is slit, packed with spiced meat and parsley, dipped, and fried. It often appears on Libyan Ramadan and party tables.
recipes.occasion: Best for Ramadan spreads, appetizers, and weekend snacks.
recipes.best_served_with: Soup, salad, lemon, chili sauce, or bread.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks add egg, breadcrumbs, or tomato paste to the filling, and spice levels vary from mild to very peppery.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

ingredients.name: potatoes
ingredients.prep_note: peeled and cut into slit wedges

steps.title: Prepare potatoes
steps.instruction: Cut potato wedges with a deep pocket, keeping each wedge attached at one end.

steps.title: Mix filling
steps.instruction: Combine meat, onion, parsley, garlic, spices, salt, and pepper.

steps.title: Stuff and coat
steps.instruction: Pack filling into potato pockets, dust with flour, and dip in egg.

steps.title: Fry until cooked
steps.instruction: Fry in batches until the potatoes are tender, the filling is cooked, and the outside is crisp.

recipes.name: Rishta Kiskas
recipes.description: Libyan hand-cut noodles steamed over chickpea, potato, and tomato stew, then tossed with sauce.
recipes.story: Rishta kiskas is Libya's steamed-noodle comfort dish. Thin noodles are steamed over a bubbling tomato stew so they catch the aroma before being tossed with the sauce and served with chickpeas, potato, and meat.
recipes.occasion: Best for family lunches, winter meals, and weekend cooking.
recipes.best_served_with: Lemon, chili paste, salad, or yogurt.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions use lamb or chicken, while meatless versions rely on chickpeas, potatoes, pumpkin, or seasonal vegetables.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Start the stew
steps.instruction: Cook meat, onion, tomato paste, tomatoes, spices, salt, and water until the meat is nearly tender.

steps.title: Add chickpeas and potatoes
steps.instruction: Add chickpeas and potatoes and simmer until the potatoes soften.

steps.title: Steam noodles
steps.instruction: Place noodles in a steamer over the stew and steam until tender and aromatic.

steps.title: Toss and serve
steps.instruction: Toss noodles with a little sauce and oil, then top with meat, chickpeas, potatoes, and more stew.

recipes.name: Sharba Libiya
recipes.description: Libyan lamb and orzo soup with tomato paste, chickpeas, cinnamon, turmeric, dried mint, lemon, and fresh herbs.
recipes.story: Sharba Libiya is the soup people mean when they simply say sharba. It should be meaty, tomato-red, minty, and bright with lemon.
recipes.occasion: Ramadan iftar
recipes.best_served_with: Bread, dates, lemon wedges, and extra dried mint
recipes.image_alt: Sharba Libiya recipe from Libya on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Brown meat
steps.instruction: Heat olive oil and brown the lamb or beef. Season lightly with salt and pepper.

steps.title: Build base
steps.instruction: Add onion, tomato paste, turmeric, cinnamon, and half the dried mint. Cook until the paste darkens.

steps.title: Simmer soup
