steps.instruction: Add chickpeas and water or stock. Simmer until the meat is tender.

steps.title: Add pasta
steps.instruction: Stir in orzo and cook until tender, stirring often so it does not stick.

steps.title: Finish
steps.instruction: Stir in lemon juice, parsley or cilantro, and remaining dried mint. Adjust salt and serve hot.

recipes.name: Usban
recipes.description: Libyan stuffed sausage parcels filled with rice, lamb, herbs, liver, and spices, then simmered until tender.
recipes.story: Usban is special-occasion cooking across Libya and parts of the Maghreb. Cleaned casings are filled with rice, herbs, lamb, liver, and spices, then simmered into rich, sliceable parcels.
recipes.occasion: Best for Eid al-Adha, weddings, and large family feasts.
recipes.best_served_with: Couscous, tomato sauce, salad, or soup from the cooking broth.
recipes.regional_variations: Libyan homes may add lamb liver, heart, herbs, rice, tomato, and chili, while Tunisian and Algerian versions vary the offal mix and spice level.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

ingredients.name: cleaned lamb casing or tripe pockets

steps.title: Clean the casings
steps.instruction: Rinse and inspect casings or tripe pockets carefully, then drain.

steps.title: Mix filling
steps.instruction: Combine rice, lamb, liver, onions, herbs, tomato paste, spices, salt, and pepper.

steps.title: Stuff loosely
steps.instruction: Fill casings loosely, sew or tie closed, and prick once or twice with a needle.

steps.title: Simmer gently
steps.instruction: Simmer in salted water or stock until the rice and meat are cooked through.

recipes.name: Mofo Gasy
recipes.description: Malagasy sweet rice-flour breakfast cakes, fermented lightly, cooked in round molds, and served warm with coffee or tea.
recipes.story: Mofo gasy should be softly sweet and lightly yeasted. Give the batter time to wake up before it hits the hot mold.
recipes.best_served_with: Coffee, tea, honey, or fruit jam
recipes.image_alt: Mofo Gasy recipe from Madagascar on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make Batter
steps.instruction: Dissolve yeast and sugar in lukewarm water. Let stand 5 minutes until foamy. Mix rice flour, coconut milk, salt, vanilla, and yeast mixture into a smooth batter.

steps.title: Ferment
steps.instruction: Cover batter and let rest in a warm place for 1 hour until bubbly and slightly risen.
steps.tip: The fermentation gives mofo gasy its characteristic light tang.

steps.title: Heat Molds
steps.instruction: Heat a mofo gasy mold, aebleskiver pan, or mini muffin tin over medium heat. Brush each well generously with oil.
steps.tip: An aebleskiver pan is the closest substitute for a traditional mofo gasy mold.

steps.title: Cook Pancakes
steps.instruction: Fill each mold three-quarters full with batter. Cover and cook until bottoms are golden and tops are set.
steps.tip: Listen for a sizzle — if the oil is not hot enough, the mofo gasy will stick.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Turn out onto a plate. Serve warm with coffee or tea for a traditional Malagasy breakfast.
steps.tip: Best eaten fresh and warm — mofo gasy lose their crispness as they cool.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

collections.name: Street Food
collections.description: Popular street snacks and quick bites found at markets and roadside stalls across Africa.

recipes.name: Ravitoto
recipes.description: Malagasy pounded cassava leaves braised slowly with pork, garlic, ginger, and coconut milk until deep and earthy.
recipes.story: Ravitoto rewards patience. Cook the cassava leaves until their rough edge softens and the pork becomes part of the sauce.
recipes.best_served_with: Steamed rice and tomato rougail
recipes.image_alt: Ravitoto recipe from Madagascar on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Brown pork
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a heavy pot and brown pork until the fat begins to render.

steps.title: Cook aromatics
steps.instruction: Add onion, garlic, and ginger. Cook until soft, then add tomatoes if using.

steps.title: Add cassava leaves
steps.instruction: Stir in pounded cassava leaves and water. Mix well with the pork and aromatics.

steps.title: Braise slowly
steps.instruction: Cover and simmer on low, stirring occasionally, until the leaves are very tender and the pork is soft.

steps.title: Finish coconut
steps.instruction: Stir in coconut milk and simmer uncovered until the sauce thickens.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Adjust salt and serve hot over white rice with tomato rougail.

recipes.name: Romazava
recipes.description: Madagascar's beef and greens stew with tomato, onion, ginger, and anamalaho or paracress when available.
recipes.story: Romazava is clean, green, and brothy. Let the beef become tender before the greens go in, then keep the final taste fresh.
recipes.best_served_with: Steamed white rice and tomato rougail
recipes.image_alt: Romazava recipe from Madagascar on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Brown Beef
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a pot. Brown beef cubes on all sides over high heat.

steps.title: Saute Aromatics
steps.instruction: Add onion, garlic, and ginger. Cook until softened. Add tomatoes and cook until they break down.

steps.title: Simmer Broth
steps.instruction: Add water and bring to a boil. Reduce heat and simmer until beef is tender.
steps.tip: The broth should remain relatively clear — this is a light stew, not a thick one.

steps.title: Add Greens
steps.instruction: Add mustard greens first and cook 5 minutes. Then add spinach and watercress. Cook until all greens are wilted.
steps.tip: Add hardier greens first, delicate ones last, so nothing is overcooked.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Season with salt. Ladle into bowls over steamed white rice. Serve with rougail (tomato chili condiment) on the side.
steps.tip: In Madagascar, romazava is always served with vary (rice) — the rice is the main event, the stew is the accompaniment.

recipes.name: Kondowole
recipes.description: Northern Malawian cassava-flour staple cooked into a sticky, elastic mound and served with fish or vegetable relish.
recipes.story: Kondowole is simple but physical. Add the cassava flour gradually and stir with confidence until it becomes smooth and stretchy.
recipes.best_served_with: Chambo, dried fish relish, mustard greens, or tomato-onion ndiwo
recipes.image_alt: Kondowole recipe from Malawi on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Boil Water
steps.instruction: Bring 3 cups of water to a boil. Add salt.

steps.title: Make Kondowole
steps.instruction: Reduce heat to medium. Add cassava flour gradually, stirring vigorously. The mixture will become very thick and stretchy. Keep stirring until it forms a cohesive, elastic mass.
steps.tip: Kondowole requires more arm strength than nsima — the cassava makes it very sticky and resistant.

steps.title: Cook Greens
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a pan. Saute onions until soft. Add tomatoes and cook down. Add greens and cook until wilted and tender.

steps.title: Shape
steps.instruction: Wet a plate and turn kondowole onto it. Shape into a dome with a wet spoon.
steps.tip: Wet everything — kondowole is much stickier than maize-based porridges.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve kondowole with the greens relish and dried fish if available.
steps.tip: Kondowole pairs especially well with dried fish (usipa) from Lake Malawi.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Mandasi
recipes.description: Malawian mandasi, lightly sweet fried dough made with coconut milk, yeast, cardamom, and a proper rise before frying.
recipes.story: Mandasi should not be rushed. Let the dough puff, then fry gently so the inside cooks before the outside gets too dark.
recipes.best_served_with: Tea, coffee, fruit jam, or powdered sugar
recipes.image_alt: Mandasi recipe from Malawi on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make Dough
steps.instruction: Mix flour, sugar, yeast, cardamom, nutmeg, and salt. Add warm coconut milk and oil. Knead into a soft, smooth dough.
steps.tip: The dough should be soft but not sticky — add flour if too wet.

steps.title: Rise
steps.instruction: Cover dough and let rise in a warm place for 1 hour until doubled in size.
steps.tip: Warm coconut milk activates the yeast faster for a better rise.

steps.title: Shape
steps.instruction: Punch down dough. Pull off golf ball-sized pieces and shape into balls or flatten into small discs.

steps.title: Fry
steps.instruction: Heat oil to 170C. Fry mandasi in batches, turning once, until golden brown on all sides. Drain on paper towels.
steps.tip: Do not overcrowd the oil — fry 3-4 at a time for even cooking.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve warm with tea or coffee. Optionally dust with powdered sugar.
steps.tip: Mandasi are best eaten warm and fresh — they lose their puffiness as they cool.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

collections.name: Street Food
collections.description: Popular street snacks and quick bites found at markets and roadside stalls across Africa.

recipes.name: Nsima with Chambo
recipes.description: Malawian nsima served with grilled chambo or tilapia, tomato-onion ndiwo, lemon, and a firm maize-meal mound.
recipes.story: The plate is simple and proud: firm nsima, fresh lake fish, and a relish bright enough to pull everything together.
recipes.best_served_with: Tomato-onion ndiwo, mustard greens, lemon wedges, or Nali-style hot sauce
recipes.image_alt: Nsima with Chambo recipe from Malawi on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Season Fish
steps.instruction: Score fish on both sides with deep diagonal cuts. Rub with lemon juice, garlic, salt, pepper, and oil. Let marinate 20 minutes.
steps.tip: Deep scores allow seasoning to penetrate and help the fish cook evenly.

steps.title: Grill Fish
steps.instruction: Grill fish over hot charcoal or broil in oven, turning once, until skin is crispy and flesh flakes easily.
steps.tip: Oil the grill grate first to prevent sticking — or use a fish grilling basket.

steps.title: Make Ndiwo
steps.instruction: Saute onions in oil until soft. Add tomatoes and cook into a thick relish. Season with salt.

steps.title: Cook Nsima
steps.instruction: Boil water, add maize flour gradually while stirring vigorously. Cook until thick and smooth, pulling away from sides.
steps.tip: Nsima should be smoother and slightly softer than Zambian nshima.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Place nsima dome on plate with grilled chambo and tomato ndiwo alongside. Serve with a lemon wedge.
steps.tip: In Malawi, the fish head is considered the best part and is offered to the guest of honor.

recipes.name: Djabadji
recipes.description: Malian onion-rich red sauce with meat, tomato, garlic, and chile, served over rice or with toh.
recipes.story: Djabadji, also called nandji in some references, is the kind of Malian sauce where onions do the heavy lifting and tomato gives the pot its red body.
recipes.occasion: Best for rice bowls, family lunch, and sauce rotation.
recipes.best_served_with: White rice, fonio, toh, or boiled plantain.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions use beef, some chicken, and some add vegetables like carrot, cabbage, or eggplant.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Brown protein
steps.instruction: Season and brown the meat or chicken in oil.

steps.title: Cook onions
steps.instruction: Add onions and cook until soft, sweet, and reduced.

steps.title: Simmer sauce
steps.instruction: Add tomatoes, tomato paste, garlic, pepper, and stock. Simmer until protein is tender.

steps.title: Finish
steps.instruction: Adjust salt and serve over rice or with toh.

recipes.name: Djouka de Fonio
recipes.description: Malian steamed fonio mixed with roasted peanut powder, okra, onion, and a peppery vegetable garnish.
recipes.story: Djouka is fonio with substance: steamed grains, peanut richness, okra, and a garnish that turns an ancient grain into a full meal.
recipes.occasion: Best for family lunches, fasting tables, and grain-forward menus.
recipes.best_served_with: Eggplant-onion garnish, tomato sauce, grilled fish, or roasted vegetables.
recipes.regional_variations: Mali and Burkina Faso both claim versions; some use potash, some add gombo, and some serve it with a separate sauce.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Steam fonio
steps.instruction: Moisten fonio lightly and steam until the grains begin to soften.

steps.title: Add peanut and okra
steps.instruction: Work in peanut powder, okra, and salt, then steam again until fluffy.

steps.title: Cook garnish
steps.instruction: Fry onions, eggplant, and chilies in oil until soft and savory.

steps.title: Combine
steps.instruction: Fold garnish through the fonio or spoon it over the top.

recipes.name: Fakoye
recipes.description: Northern Malian fakoye leaf sauce with jute or molokhia leaves, dried fish, soumbala, onion, chile, and optional okra.
recipes.story: Fakoye is a leaf sauce first. The pot should turn silky and deep, with soumbala and dried fish giving it its old-school backbone.
recipes.occasion: Everyday meals
recipes.best_served_with: Toh, rice, millet paste, or steamed fonio
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions use more okra for a thicker draw, while others add extra dried fish or chilli depending on the household.
recipes.image_alt: Fakoye recipe from Mali on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Start base
steps.instruction: Heat palm oil or shea butter and cook onion until soft. Stir in soumbala.

steps.title: Add liquid and protein
steps.instruction: Add water or stock, dried fish, and hot pepper. Simmer to season the broth.

steps.title: Add leaves
steps.instruction: Stir in fakoye leaves or powder. Cook until the sauce darkens and becomes silky.

steps.title: Finish texture
steps.instruction: Add okra if using and simmer until the sauce is lightly thickened. Adjust salt.

recipes.name: Toh
recipes.description: Malian toh, a firm millet or sorghum paste stirred smooth and shaped into a mound for sauces.
recipes.story: Toh looks simple, but the spoon work matters. Build it with a slurry, then stir until glossy and firm.
recipes.occasion: Daily staple
recipes.best_served_with: Tigadegena, fakoye, okra sauce, leaf sauce, or meat stew
recipes.regional_variations: Millet, sorghum, maize, or blended flours may be used depending on region and availability. Texture ranges from soft to very firm by household preference.
recipes.image_alt: Toh recipe from Mali on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make a slurry
steps.instruction: Mix half the millet flour with 1 cup cold water to form a smooth paste.
steps.tip: Cold-water slurry prevents lumps.

steps.title: Boil the water
steps.instruction: Bring remaining 3 cups water to a rolling boil with salt.

steps.title: Add the slurry
steps.instruction: Pour slurry into boiling water, stirring constantly. Cook 5 minutes on medium heat.

steps.title: Add remaining flour
steps.instruction: Gradually add remaining flour, stirring vigorously. Mixture will become very thick.
steps.tip: Use a strong wooden spoon.

steps.title: Shape and serve
steps.instruction: When toh pulls from pot sides, wet a bowl, scoop in, smooth top, turn out as a mound.
steps.tip: Serve immediately — toh hardens as it cools.

collections.name: Quick & Easy
collections.description: Delicious African dishes ready in 30 minutes or less. Perfect for busy weeknight dinners.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Lakh
recipes.description: Mauritanian-style lakh, soft millet couscous served with sweet fermented milk, yogurt, coconut, and warm spice.
recipes.story: Lakh should feel cool, creamy, and lightly tangy against the nutty millet. Let the grains cool before the milk goes on so the sauce stays fresh.
recipes.occasion: Breakfast and dessert
recipes.best_served_with: Mint tea, dates, roasted peanuts, or fresh fruit
recipes.image_alt: Lakh dessert from Mauritania on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

ingredients.name: fermented milk, lait caille, or plain yogurt

steps.title: Steam millet
steps.instruction: Steam the millet couscous over simmering water until the grains are tender and fluffy.

steps.title: Cool and fluff
steps.instruction: Spread the millet in a shallow bowl, sprinkle with salt, and fluff gently so it cools without clumping.

steps.title: Sweeten milk
steps.instruction: Whisk fermented milk or yogurt with sugar, coconut, vanilla, nutmeg, and baobab powder if using.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Spoon the cool millet into bowls and pour the sweet milk sauce over the top just before eating.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Maru We Llham
recipes.description: Mauritanian meat, rice, and vegetable pot scented with mustard, onion, bay, pepper, and broth.
recipes.story: Maru we llham sits between Sahel rice pot and desert hospitality: meat, rice, vegetables, mustard sharpness, and a broth that feeds the whole tray.
recipes.occasion: Best for shared platters, family lunch, and one-pot rice meals.
recipes.best_served_with: Green salad, chili sauce, or Mauritanian mint tea after the meal.
recipes.regional_variations: Chicken, lamb, goat, or beef may be used; vegetables can include cassava, cabbage, carrot, or eggplant.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Brown meat
steps.instruction: Brown meat with onion and garlic in oil or butter.

steps.title: Braise
steps.instruction: Add mustard, bay, vegetables, salt, pepper, and stock. Simmer until meat is nearly tender.

steps.title: Cook rice
steps.instruction: Stir in rice, cover, and cook on low until grains absorb the broth.

steps.title: Rest
steps.instruction: Rest covered, then fluff gently and serve from a shared platter.

recipes.name: Mechoui
recipes.description: Mauritanian-style mechoui, slow-roasted lamb rubbed with butter, cumin, garlic, and paprika until tender enough to pull apart.
recipes.story: Mechoui is celebration lamb. The magic is not a crowded spice drawer; it is patience, generous seasoning, and a final blast of heat for the edges.
recipes.occasion: Weddings and religious celebrations
recipes.best_served_with: Flatbread, couscous, salad, roasted onions, or mint tea
recipes.image_alt: Mechoui recipe from Mauritania on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make rub
steps.instruction: Mix butter, cumin, paprika, garlic, coriander if using, salt, and pepper into a thick paste.

steps.title: Season lamb
steps.instruction: Score the lamb and rub the paste deeply into the cuts and over the surface. Rest at room temperature briefly or chill for a longer marinade.

steps.title: Slow roast
steps.instruction: Roast covered at 150C until the lamb is very tender, basting occasionally with pan juices.

steps.title: Crisp outside
steps.instruction: Uncover, raise the oven to 220C, and roast until the surface browns and crackles at the edges.

steps.title: Rest and serve
steps.instruction: Rest the lamb before carving or tearing. Serve with bread, couscous, salad, and lemon if using.

collections.name: Sunday Specials
collections.description: Elaborate weekend dishes worth the extra effort. These are the recipes that make Sunday lunch special.

recipes.name: Dholl Puri
recipes.description: Mauritian dholl puri, thin yellow-split-pea flatbread folded around butter bean curry, rougaille, chutney, and achard.
recipes.story: The beauty is in the thinness. Grind the peas fine, keep the dough soft, and roll gently so the filling spreads without tearing.
recipes.best_served_with: Butter bean curry, rougaille, coriander chutney, achard, and chili paste
recipes.image_alt: Dholl Puri recipe from Mauritius on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Cook Split Peas
steps.instruction: Boil soaked split peas until very soft. Drain well. Grind to a coarse powder with cumin, turmeric, and salt.
steps.tip: The split peas must be completely dry before grinding — excess moisture makes the dough soggy.

steps.title: Make Dough
steps.instruction: Mix flour, salt, oil, and water into a soft dough. Knead 5 minutes. Rest 30 minutes covered.

steps.title: Stuff
steps.instruction: Divide dough into balls. Flatten each, place a spoonful of dholl filling in center, fold edges over, and seal. Gently roll out into a thin circle.
steps.tip: Roll gently to avoid the filling breaking through — if it does, pinch it closed and try again.

steps.title: Cook
steps.instruction: Cook on a hot dry tawa or griddle until golden spots appear on both sides. Brush lightly with oil.
steps.tip: The puri should puff slightly and have golden-brown spots — do not overcook or it becomes crispy.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Place puri on paper, spread with curry, add coriander chutney and chili paste. Fold and eat with hands.
steps.tip: The authentic Mauritian way: butter bean curry, coriander chutney, and hot chili — all in one fold.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

collections.name: Sunday Specials
collections.description: Elaborate weekend dishes worth the extra effort. These are the recipes that make Sunday lunch special.

collections.name: Street Food
collections.description: Popular street snacks and quick bites found at markets and roadside stalls across Africa.

recipes.name: Gateau Piment
recipes.description: Mauritian gateau piment, crisp chilli fritters made from soaked yellow split peas, herbs, cumin, and green chile.
recipes.story: Do not puree the peas into baby food. A coarse paste gives gateau piment its crisp shell and tender, grainy middle.
recipes.best_served_with: Fresh baguette, butter, coriander chutney, or rougaille
recipes.image_alt: Gateau Piment recipe from Mauritius on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Grind peas
steps.instruction: Drain soaked split peas well and grind to a coarse paste, scraping down the bowl so some texture remains.

steps.title: Season paste
steps.instruction: Mix in onion, chilies, garlic, coriander, spring onions, cumin, curry leaves if using, baking soda, and salt.

steps.title: Shape fritters
steps.instruction: With damp hands, shape small balls or rough patties so they fry evenly.

steps.title: Fry crisp
steps.instruction: Fry in hot oil until deeply golden and crisp outside, turning as needed.

steps.title: Serve hot
steps.instruction: Drain well and serve immediately, plain or tucked into bread with chutney or rougaille.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

collections.name: Street Food
collections.description: Popular street snacks and quick bites found at markets and roadside stalls across Africa.

recipes.name: Rougaille
recipes.description: Mauritian sausage rougaille, a thick tomato, thyme, garlic, ginger, and chile sauce simmered with browned sausages.
recipes.story: Rougaille should taste bright and cooked down, not raw tomato. Let the tomatoes collapse before the sausage goes back in.
recipes.best_served_with: Steamed rice, lentils, dholl puri, cucumber salad, or achard
recipes.image_alt: Rougaille recipe from Mauritius on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

ingredients.name: Mauritian, smoked, pork, chicken, or beef sausages

steps.title: Brown sausage
steps.instruction: Heat oil and brown the sausage slices on both sides. Remove them so they do not overcook.

steps.title: Cook aromatics
steps.instruction: Cook onions in the same pot until soft, then add garlic, ginger, thyme, and curry leaves if using.

steps.title: Reduce tomatoes
steps.instruction: Add tomatoes and whole chile. Cook until the tomatoes collapse into a thick sauce.

steps.title: Simmer sausage
steps.instruction: Return sausage to the pot and simmer gently until the sauce coats every piece.

steps.title: Finish
steps.instruction: Remove the whole chile if you want a milder dish. Stir in coriander or parsley and adjust salt.

recipes.name: Bessara
recipes.description: Moroccan fava bean or split pea soup pureed smooth and finished with olive oil, cumin, paprika, and bread.
recipes.story: Bessara is a Moroccan comfort soup, thick and humble, often eaten for breakfast with olive oil, cumin, paprika, and bread.
recipes.occasion: Best for breakfast, cold mornings, and simple vegetarian meals.
recipes.best_served_with: Khobz, olive oil, cumin, paprika, and olives.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions use dried fava beans, others use split peas, and thickness ranges from soup to dip.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

ingredients.name: dried split peas or peeled fava beans

steps.title: Cook legumes
steps.instruction: Simmer split peas or fava beans with garlic and water until completely soft.

steps.title: Blend smooth
steps.instruction: Blend the soup until velvety, adding hot water if needed.

steps.title: Season
steps.instruction: Return to the pot with cumin, paprika, olive oil, salt, and pepper. Simmer briefly.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve hot with more olive oil, cumin, and bread.

recipes.name: Chebakia
recipes.description: Moroccan sesame, almond, honey, and orange blossom pastries folded into flowers and fried for Ramadan.
recipes.story: Chebakia is Morocco's Ramadan pastry jewel. Toasted sesame, almonds, spices, orange blossom water, and honey become folded flower pastries that are fried, soaked, and sprinkled with sesame.
recipes.occasion: Best for Ramadan iftar, Eid trays, and tea tables.
recipes.best_served_with: Harira, mint tea, dates, or sellou.
recipes.regional_variations: Shapes and spice blends vary, but sesame, honey, orange blossom water, cinnamon, anise, and mastic are common markers.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Make the dough
steps.instruction: Mix flour, ground sesame, almonds, spices, butter, egg, and orange blossom water into a firm dough.

steps.title: Rest and roll
steps.instruction: Rest the dough, then roll thin and cut into rectangles for folding.

steps.title: Shape flowers
steps.instruction: Slit and fold each rectangle into a chebakia flower shape.

steps.title: Fry and honey
steps.instruction: Fry until deep golden, soak in warm honey, and sprinkle with sesame.

recipes.name: Chicken Pastilla
recipes.description: A Moroccan savory-sweet pie of spiced chicken, soft egg sauce, toasted almonds, cinnamon, and crisp pastry.
recipes.story: Chicken Pastilla is Morocco's celebration pie: savory chicken cooked with onion and warm spices, eggs stirred into the reduced sauce, almonds sweetened with cinnamon, all wrapped in thin pastry and dusted lightly at the end.
recipes.occasion: Best for Eid tables, weddings, special dinners, or a showpiece weekend meal.
recipes.best_served_with: A simple salad, mint tea, olives, or a light soup.
recipes.regional_variations: Traditional versions may use pigeon and warqa pastry. Home versions often use chicken and phyllo for access.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Cook the chicken
steps.instruction: Simmer chicken with onion, butter, ginger, cinnamon, turmeric, herbs, salt, pepper, and a little water until tender.

steps.title: Make the egg sauce
steps.instruction: Remove and shred the chicken. Reduce the onion sauce until thick, then stir in beaten eggs over low heat until softly set.
steps.tip: The filling should be moist, not runny.

steps.title: Prepare almonds
steps.instruction: Mix toasted chopped almonds with powdered sugar and cinnamon.

steps.title: Layer and bake
steps.instruction: Brush phyllo sheets with butter in a round pan. Layer chicken, egg sauce, and almonds, fold pastry over, brush again, and bake until golden.
steps.tip: Cool 10 minutes before dusting with a little sugar and cinnamon.

recipes.name: Harira
recipes.description: Moroccan harira, a Ramadan tomato soup with lamb, chickpeas, lentils, celery, herbs, vermicelli, and lemon.
recipes.story: Harira should be hearty but pourable. Let the legumes soften first, then thicken gently so the soup turns silky instead of pasty.
recipes.occasion: Ramadan iftar
recipes.best_served_with: Dates, lemon wedges, chebakia, hard-boiled eggs, or crusty bread
recipes.image_alt: Harira recipe from Morocco on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

ingredients.name: plain flour
ingredients.prep_note: mixed with water into a slurry

steps.title: Brown base
steps.instruction: Heat oil and brown meat if using. Add onion and celery and cook until soft.

steps.title: Spice tomatoes
steps.instruction: Add ginger, turmeric, cinnamon, pepper, tomatoes, cilantro, and parsley. Cook until fragrant.

steps.title: Simmer legumes
steps.instruction: Add chickpeas, lentils, and water or stock. Simmer until the chickpeas and lentils are tender.

steps.title: Add noodles
steps.instruction: Stir in vermicelli and cook until tender.

steps.title: Thicken and brighten
steps.instruction: Whisk in the flour slurry gradually and simmer until silky. Finish with lemon juice and adjust salt.

recipes.name: Kefta Tagine
recipes.description: Moroccan meatball tagine simmered in spiced tomato sauce and finished with eggs.
recipes.story: Kefta tagine is Moroccan comfort in a clay pot: small spiced meatballs simmered in tomato sauce, with eggs cracked in at the end.
recipes.occasion: Best for family dinner, bread-scooping meals, and quick tagine nights.
recipes.best_served_with: Khobz, salad, olives, or roasted vegetables.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks add preserved lemon or olives, but the meatball, tomato, and egg version is the classic home base.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Shape meatballs
steps.instruction: Mix meat with onion, herbs, half the spices, salt, and pepper. Shape small meatballs.

steps.title: Cook sauce
steps.instruction: Simmer tomatoes, garlic, olive oil, remaining spices, salt, and pepper until thick.

steps.title: Simmer kefta
steps.instruction: Add meatballs to the sauce, cover, and cook until done.

steps.title: Set eggs
steps.instruction: Crack eggs into the sauce, cover, and cook until whites set and yolks are still soft.

recipes.name: Lamb Tagine with Prunes and Almonds
recipes.description: Moroccan lamb tagine with ginger, saffron, cinnamon, honeyed prunes, toasted almonds, and sesame.
recipes.story: Lamb with prunes and almonds is one of Morocco's great sweet-savory celebration tagines. Slow-cooked meat, ginger, saffron, cinnamon, and onions meet glossy honeyed prunes and crunchy almonds.
recipes.occasion: Best for weddings, Eid, guest lunches, and special family meals.
recipes.best_served_with: Khobz, couscous, Moroccan salads, or mint tea.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks use beef instead of lamb, add apricots, or garnish with boiled eggs and sesame.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Start the tagine
steps.instruction: Brown lamb with oil, onions, ginger, turmeric, cinnamon, saffron, salt, and pepper.

steps.title: Braise slowly
steps.instruction: Add water, cover, and cook gently until the lamb is very tender.

steps.title: Glaze the prunes
steps.instruction: Simmer prunes with a ladle of sauce, honey, and cinnamon until glossy.

steps.title: Finish and garnish
steps.instruction: Reduce the meat sauce, then top with prunes, almonds, sesame, and a little syrup.

recipes.name: Maakouda
recipes.description: Moroccan potato cakes seasoned with herbs, cumin, turmeric, garlic, and egg, then fried until crisp.
recipes.story: Maakouda is the potato cake that appears in Moroccan homes, markets, and sandwich stalls. Mashed potato is seasoned with herbs and spices, shaped, and fried until the outside turns crisp.
recipes.occasion: Best for street-food sandwiches, iftar spreads, and snack plates.
recipes.best_served_with: Harissa, olives, khobz, salad, or grilled meats.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks dredge in flour, others keep it gluten-free. Garlic, turmeric, cumin, parsley, cilantro, and chili vary by home.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Cook potatoes
steps.instruction: Boil potatoes in salted water until tender, then drain very well.

steps.title: Season and mash
steps.instruction: Mash potatoes with eggs, herbs, garlic, cumin, turmeric, salt, and pepper.

steps.title: Shape cakes
steps.instruction: Shape into small patties and dust lightly with flour if needed.

steps.title: Fry crisp
steps.instruction: Fry in hot oil until both sides are golden and crisp.

recipes.name: Msemen
recipes.description: Moroccan msemen, flaky square griddle flatbread laminated with oil, butter, and fine semolina.
recipes.story: Msemen is all touch. The dough needs enough rest to stretch paper-thin, and enough oil and semolina to make the layers separate.
recipes.occasion: Breakfast or tea time
recipes.best_served_with: Honey, butter, jam, soft cheese, mint tea, or amlou
recipes.image_alt: Msemen recipe from Morocco on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Knead dough
steps.instruction: Mix flour, semolina, salt, sugar if using, and warm water. Knead until smooth, soft, and elastic.

steps.title: Rest balls
steps.instruction: Divide into small balls, coat with oil, cover, and rest so the dough relaxes.

steps.title: Stretch and fold
steps.instruction: Stretch one ball very thin on an oiled surface. Brush with butter-oil, dust with semolina, and fold into a square.

steps.title: Cook griddle
steps.instruction: Flatten each square gently and cook on a hot griddle until golden spots form and the layers puff.

steps.title: Serve warm
steps.instruction: Serve hot with honey, butter, jam, cheese, or mint tea.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

collections.name: Street Food
collections.description: Popular street snacks and quick bites found at markets and roadside stalls across Africa.

recipes.name: Rfissa
recipes.description: Moroccan chicken, lentil, and fenugreek stew served over shredded msemen or trid pastry.
recipes.story: Rfissa is one of Morocco's great communal dishes: shredded msemen or trid under saffron-scented chicken, lentils, fenugreek, onions, and broth.
recipes.occasion: Best for family gatherings, postpartum meals, and special lunches.
recipes.best_served_with: Msemen, trid pastry, or other thin flatbread.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions use ras el hanout, smen, or extra fenugreek, and the bread base may be homemade or store-bought msemen.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Season chicken
steps.instruction: Season chicken with ginger, turmeric, saffron if using, salt, and pepper.

steps.title: Start stew
steps.instruction: Cook onions in oil, add chicken, and brown lightly.

steps.title: Simmer lentils
steps.instruction: Add lentils, soaked fenugreek, and enough water to cover. Simmer until chicken and lentils are tender.

steps.title: Serve over bread
steps.instruction: Pile shredded msemen on a platter and spoon chicken, lentils, and broth over it.

recipes.name: Sardine Kefta Tagine
recipes.description: Moroccan sardine meatballs simmered in tomato, chermoula, olives, lemon, and peppers.
recipes.story: Sardine kefta tagine is coastal Moroccan cooking at its smartest. Sardines are chopped with herbs and spices, shaped into small balls, and simmered in tomato chermoula sauce until tender.
recipes.occasion: Best for seafood lunches, Friday meals, and bread-and-salad tables.
recipes.best_served_with: Khobz, taktouka, olives, and preserved lemon.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions add rice to the kefta, while others keep the fish pure. Potatoes, peppers, olives, and preserved lemon can be added to the sauce.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Mix sardine kefta
steps.instruction: Combine chopped sardines, herbs, garlic, cumin, paprika, lemon, salt, and pepper.

steps.title: Shape balls
steps.instruction: Shape into small balls and chill while the sauce starts.

steps.title: Simmer tomato sauce
steps.instruction: Cook tomatoes, olive oil, pepper, olives, and preserved lemon until saucy.

steps.title: Cook the kefta
steps.instruction: Nestle sardine balls into the sauce and simmer gently until cooked through.

recipes.name: Seffa Medfouna
recipes.description: Moroccan sweet-savory vermicelli or couscous mound hiding spiced chicken and finished with almonds, cinnamon, and powdered sugar.
recipes.story: Seffa medfouna is one of Morocco's elegant sweet-savory dishes: steamed vermicelli or couscous hiding saffron chicken, decorated with almonds, cinnamon, and sugar.
recipes.occasion: Best for special lunches, guests, and celebration tables.
recipes.best_served_with: Mint tea, simple salads, or a light soup.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions use couscous, others use broken vermicelli. The filling may be chicken, lamb, or no meat.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Cook chicken
steps.instruction: Cook chicken with onions, spices, salt, pepper, and water until tender. Shred and reduce the sauce.

steps.title: Steam vermicelli
steps.instruction: Steam vermicelli in stages, fluffing with butter and a little water until tender.

steps.title: Build mound
steps.instruction: Layer vermicelli on a platter, hide chicken in the center, and cover with more vermicelli.

steps.title: Decorate
steps.instruction: Top with almonds, cinnamon, and powdered sugar just before serving.

recipes.name: Taktouka
recipes.description: Moroccan roasted pepper and tomato salad cooked with garlic, cumin, paprika, olive oil, and herbs.
recipes.story: Taktouka is a Moroccan cooked salad of roasted peppers and tomato. It is smoky, soft, and bright enough to sit beside tagines, grilled meats, or bread.
recipes.occasion: Best as a starter, side dish, or bread dip.
recipes.best_served_with: Khobz, grilled fish, kefta, tagines, or eggs.
recipes.regional_variations: Peppers may be roasted over flame or under a grill, and chile can be added for heat.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Roast peppers
steps.instruction: Roast peppers until blistered, then steam briefly, peel, seed, and chop.

steps.title: Cook tomatoes
steps.instruction: Cook tomatoes, garlic, olive oil, cumin, paprika, salt, and pepper until thick.

steps.title: Combine
steps.instruction: Add chopped peppers and cook until the salad is soft and glossy.

steps.title: Finish
steps.instruction: Stir in herbs and serve warm or room temperature.

recipes.name: Zaalouk
recipes.description: Moroccan eggplant and tomato salad cooked with garlic, olive oil, paprika, cumin, cilantro, and lemon.
recipes.story: Zaalouk is a Moroccan cooked salad where eggplant and tomato melt together with olive oil, garlic, cumin, paprika, herbs, and lemon.
recipes.occasion: Best as a starter, side dish, mezze plate, or bread dip.
recipes.best_served_with: Khobz, grilled fish, tagines, or couscous.
recipes.regional_variations: Eggplant may be roasted, boiled, or pan-cooked before being mashed with tomato.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Cook eggplant
steps.instruction: Steam or roast eggplant until very tender.

steps.title: Cook tomato base
steps.instruction: Cook tomatoes, garlic, olive oil, paprika, cumin, salt, and pepper until reduced.

steps.title: Mash together
steps.instruction: Add eggplant and mash into the tomato base. Cook until thick and glossy.

steps.title: Finish bright
steps.instruction: Stir in herbs and lemon juice. Serve warm or room temperature.

recipes.name: Caril de Camarao
recipes.description: Mozambican caril de camarão, prawns simmered briefly in coconut milk, tomato, curry spice, garlic, ginger, and piri-piri.
recipes.story: The sauce can take its time, but the prawns cannot. Build the curry first, then add seafood at the end and stop as soon as it curls.
recipes.best_served_with: White rice, xima, lemon wedges, or simple tomato salad
recipes.image_alt: Caril de Camarao recipe from Mozambique on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Saute Aromatics
steps.instruction: Heat olive oil in a wide pan. Saute onion, garlic, and ginger until soft and fragrant.

steps.title: Build Sauce
steps.instruction: Add curry powder, turmeric, and chili. Stir for 30 seconds. Add chopped tomatoes and cook until they break down into a sauce.
steps.tip: Toast the spices briefly before adding tomatoes to deepen their flavor.

steps.title: Add Coconut Milk
steps.instruction: Pour in coconut milk and stir well. Bring to a gentle simmer and cook for 5 minutes to meld flavors.

steps.title: Cook Prawns
steps.instruction: Add prawns to the sauce in a single layer. Cook for 2-3 minutes per side until pink and curled. Do not overcook.
steps.tip: Prawns cook quickly — remove from heat as soon as they turn pink to keep them tender.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Garnish with fresh cilantro and lime wedges. Serve over steamed rice or with crusty bread to soak up the sauce.
steps.tip: This curry is best served immediately — prawns toughen if left sitting in hot sauce.

recipes.name: Matapa
recipes.description: Mozambican matapa, pounded cassava leaves simmered with ground peanuts, coconut milk, garlic, piri-piri, and shrimp.
recipes.story: Matapa should be creamy and earthy, with the cassava leaves cooked past toughness. The seafood seasons the pot rather than taking it over.
recipes.best_served_with: White rice, xima, grilled prawns, or piri-piri chicken
recipes.image_alt: Matapa recipe from Mozambique on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

ingredients.name: shrimp, dried shrimp, or crab meat

steps.title: Start leaves
steps.instruction: Put pounded cassava leaves, garlic, chile if using, and water in a pot. Simmer until the leaves begin to soften.

steps.title: Add peanut
steps.instruction: Stir in ground peanuts and onion. Cook gently, stirring often so the peanut does not catch.

steps.title: Simmer creamy
steps.instruction: Add coconut milk and continue simmering until the leaves are tender and the sauce is creamy.

steps.title: Cook seafood
steps.instruction: Add shrimp, dried shrimp, or crab and simmer until cooked through and absorbed into the sauce.

steps.title: Finish
steps.instruction: Season with salt and lemon juice if using. Serve hot over rice or xima.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Piri Piri Chicken
recipes.description: Mozambican piri piri chicken, spatchcocked and grilled with African bird's-eye chile, garlic, lemon, vinegar, paprika, and oil.
recipes.story: The sauce is both marinade and basting brush. Let it sit with the chicken long enough to stain the meat, then grill until smoky and glossy.
recipes.best_served_with: Matapa, rice, fries, tomato salad, lime wedges, or extra piri-piri sauce
recipes.image_alt: Piri Piri Chicken recipe from Mozambique on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Blend sauce
steps.instruction: Blend piri-piri chiles, garlic, lemon juice, roasted pepper, paprika, oregano, vinegar, olive oil, and salt into a punchy sauce.

steps.title: Marinate chicken
steps.instruction: Score the spatchcocked chicken, rub with most of the sauce, and chill for at least four hours or overnight. Reserve some sauce for basting.

steps.title: Grill and baste
steps.instruction: Grill over medium-hot charcoal, turning and basting regularly until the skin is burnished and smoky.

steps.title: Check and rest
steps.instruction: Cook until juices run clear and the thickest part reaches 75C. Rest before cutting so the juices settle.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Cut into pieces and squeeze lime over the top. Serve with rice, fries, matapa, or extra piri-piri sauce.

recipes.name: Kapana
recipes.description: Namibian kapana, fast-grilled beef strips tossed with kapana spice and served with tomato, onion, chile, vinegar, and oil salsa.
recipes.story: Kapana lives on heat and speed. Grill the meat hard, slice it while hot, and let people dip into extra spice and salsa at the table.
recipes.occasion: Street food or casual lunch
recipes.best_served_with: Tomato salsa, extra kapana spice, vetkoek, pap, or oshifima
recipes.image_alt: Kapana recipe from Namibia on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

ingredients.name: beef sirloin, rump, or game meat

steps.title: Mix salsa
steps.instruction: Combine tomatoes, onion, chilies, vinegar, oil, and a pinch of salt. Let it sit while the grill heats.

steps.title: Season beef
steps.instruction: Rub beef with oil, kapana spice, and salt. Keep extra spice nearby for dipping.

steps.title: Grill hot
steps.instruction: Grill over very hot coals until charred outside and juicy inside.

steps.title: Slice and serve
steps.instruction: Slice into bite-sized pieces and serve immediately with salsa and extra kapana spice.

collections.name: Quick & Easy
collections.description: Delicious African dishes ready in 30 minutes or less. Perfect for busy weeknight dinners.

collections.name: Street Food
collections.description: Popular street snacks and quick bites found at markets and roadside stalls across Africa.

recipes.name: Oshifima
recipes.description: Namibian oshifima, a smooth stiff porridge made from mahangu or pearl millet flour and served as the base for stews and grilled meat.
recipes.story: Oshifima is simple but physical. Add flour in stages and stir with purpose until the porridge pulls from the pot and holds its shape.
recipes.occasion: Everyday staple
recipes.best_served_with: Kapana, oshiwambo chicken, greens, beef stew, or tomato relish
recipes.image_alt: Oshifima recipe from Namibia on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

ingredients.name: mahangu or pearl millet flour
ingredients.prep_note: maize meal can replace part of it

steps.title: Boil water
steps.instruction: Bring the water to a rolling boil in a heavy-bottomed pot. Add salt if using.

steps.title: Add millet flour gradually
steps.instruction: Reduce heat to medium. Sprinkle in about a third of the millet flour while stirring constantly with a strong wooden spoon or oshini (wooden paddle) to prevent lumps.
steps.tip: A flat wooden paddle gives you more leverage for stirring the thick porridge.

steps.title: Build thickness
steps.instruction: Continue adding the millet flour in batches, stirring vigorously after each addition. The mixture will become increasingly thick and heavy. Keep stirring to work out any lumps.
steps.tip: This requires real arm strength — the thicker it gets, the harder you stir.

steps.title: Cook through
steps.instruction: Once all the flour is incorporated and the mixture pulls away from the sides of the pot, reduce heat to low. Cover and steam for 5-8 minutes.

steps.title: Shape and serve
steps.instruction: Wet a wooden spoon and shape the oshifima into a smooth mound. Turn out onto a plate. Serve alongside stew, greens, or grilled meat.
steps.tip: Dip your hands in water when eating to prevent the oshifima from sticking to your fingers.

collections.name: Quick & Easy
collections.description: Delicious African dishes ready in 30 minutes or less. Perfect for busy weeknight dinners.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Dounguouri Soko
recipes.description: Nigerien white bean and lamb stew cooked with onion, tomato, pepper, garlic, and a slow savory sauce.
recipes.story: Dounguouri Soko is Niger's bean stew comfort, built like a Sahelian cousin to cassoulet: white beans, lamb, tomato, pepper, and time.
recipes.occasion: Best for cool evenings, bean lovers, and hearty family stews.
recipes.best_served_with: Rice, millet, flatbread, or a spoon of mayonnaise as some references note.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions use natron to soften beans; others skip it and simply soak beans overnight.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Cook beans
steps.instruction: Simmer soaked beans in fresh water until just tender.

steps.title: Brown lamb
steps.instruction: Brown lamb in oil, then add onions and peppers and cook until softened.

steps.title: Simmer stew
steps.instruction: Add tomatoes, tomato paste, garlic, cooked beans, and stock. Simmer until lamb and beans are tender.

steps.title: Reduce
steps.instruction: Simmer uncovered to thicken, then adjust salt and pepper.

recipes.name: Kilishi
recipes.description: Nigerien-Hausa kilishi, paper-thin lean beef dried, coated in spicy groundnut labu paste, dried again, and roasted crisp.
recipes.story: Kilishi is preservation cooking. Thin slices, patient drying, and a well-seasoned labu paste make the meat crisp, spicy, and long-lasting.
recipes.occasion: Snack and gift
recipes.best_served_with: Kunu, pap, garri, cucumber salad, or extra chile powder
recipes.image_alt: Kilishi recipe from Niger on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

ingredients.name: very lean beef topside or flank
roasted groundnut paste or kuli-kuli powder

steps.title: Slice meat
steps.instruction: Trim all fat and slice the beef into broad, paper-thin sheets.

steps.title: Dry first
steps.instruction: Lay meat on racks and dry in strong sun or a very low oven until firm but still flexible.

steps.title: Make labu
steps.instruction: Mix groundnut paste with chili, ginger, garlic, onion, cloves if using, seasoning cube if using, salt, and enough water to spread.

steps.title: Coat and dry
steps.instruction: Brush labu paste over each sheet and return to racks until the coating dries and clings to the meat.

steps.title: Roast crisp
steps.instruction: Roast briefly over low coals or in a hot oven until aromatic and lightly crisp, then cool before storing.

collections.name: Sunday Specials
collections.description: Elaborate weekend dishes worth the extra effort. These are the recipes that make Sunday lunch special.

collections.name: Street Food
collections.description: Popular street snacks and quick bites found at markets and roadside stalls across Africa.

recipes.name: Kopto
recipes.description: Nigerien moringa leaf salad-sauce mixed with peanut paste, soumbala, onion, chile, and sometimes gari.
recipes.story: Kopto means leaf in Zarma, and this Nigerien dish lets moringa stay green and direct, dressed with peanut, soumbala, onion, and pepper.
recipes.occasion: Best for light lunches, side plates, and moringa season.
recipes.best_served_with: Dambou, rice, millet, gari, grilled fish, or beans.
recipes.regional_variations: Moringa is classic, but cabbage, spinach, or white bissap leaves may stand in when moringa is not available.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Blanch leaves
steps.instruction: Blanch moringa leaves briefly, then drain and squeeze out excess water.

steps.title: Make dressing
steps.instruction: Mix peanut paste, soumbala, chili, onion, salt, and a splash of water into a creamy dressing.

steps.title: Combine
steps.instruction: Fold leaves into the dressing until evenly coated.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Top with tomato, pepper, or gari if desired and serve cool or room temperature.

recipes.name: Tuwo Masara
recipes.description: Tuwo masara, a smooth firm maize swallow from Niger and the wider Hausa region, shaped for dipping into soups and stews.
recipes.story: Tuwo rewards steady stirring. Start with a slurry, then add flour until the paste is thick enough to mound and pull cleanly from the pot.
recipes.occasion: Daily staple
recipes.best_served_with: Miyan kuka, okra soup, egusi soup, groundnut stew, or vegetable sauce
recipes.image_alt: Tuwo Masara recipe from Niger on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make slurry
steps.instruction: Mix 1 cup maize flour with 1 cup cold water until smooth.

steps.title: Boil water
steps.instruction: Bring remaining 3 cups water to a boil with salt.

steps.title: Cook the tuwo
steps.instruction: Pour slurry into boiling water, stirring vigorously. Cook on medium heat for 5 minutes, stirring constantly.

steps.title: Thicken
steps.instruction: Gradually add remaining maize flour, stirring hard. The mixture will become very thick and pull away from the pot. Cook 10 more minutes.
steps.tip: Consistent stirring prevents lumps.

steps.title: Shape and serve
steps.instruction: Wet a bowl, scoop tuwo in, and shape into a smooth mound. Turn out onto a plate and serve with soup.

collections.name: Quick & Easy
collections.description: Delicious African dishes ready in 30 minutes or less. Perfect for busy weeknight dinners.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Abacha
recipes.description: Igbo cassava salad tossed with palm oil dressing, ugba, fish, crayfish, onion, garden egg, and utazi.
recipes.story: Abacha is a bright Igbo cassava salad, chewy and savory with palm oil dressing, ugba, fish, crayfish, onion, garden egg, and bitter utazi accents.
recipes.occasion: Best for snacks, visitors, market-style meals, and festive side plates.
recipes.best_served_with: Garden eggs, fried fish, kpomo, onions, and chilled drinks.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions are fully mixed, others are plated with toppings. Ugba and utazi bring the unmistakable eastern Nigerian profile.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Soften abacha
steps.instruction: Soak abacha in warm water until flexible, then rinse and drain well.

steps.title: Make palm oil dressing
steps.instruction: Stir palm oil with strained potash water until it thickens and turns bright orange.

steps.title: Season the salad
steps.instruction: Add crayfish, pepper, salt, ugba, and fish to the dressing, then toss in abacha.

steps.title: Finish with toppings
steps.instruction: Top with onion, garden egg, and utazi. Serve warm or room temperature.

recipes.name: Afang Soup
recipes.description: Efik-Ibibio vegetable soup with shredded afang leaves, waterleaf, palm oil, meat, stockfish, crayfish, and periwinkles.
recipes.story: Afang soup is a South-South Nigerian classic, dense with greens and rich with seafood notes. Afang leaves bring chew and depth while waterleaf keeps the pot lush.
recipes.occasion: Best for swallow meals, celebrations, and Sunday cooking.
recipes.best_served_with: Garri, fufu, pounded yam, or semolina.
recipes.regional_variations: Periwinkles, stockfish, smoked fish, beef, and kpomo all vary by household, but afang and waterleaf remain the backbone.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Cook the protein
steps.instruction: Simmer meat and stockfish until tender and the stock is reduced.

steps.title: Add waterleaf
steps.instruction: Add waterleaf and cook until it wilts and releases moisture.

steps.title: Season richly
steps.instruction: Add palm oil, crayfish, pepper, periwinkles if using, and salt. Simmer until glossy.

steps.title: Finish with afang
steps.instruction: Stir in afang leaves and cook until softened but still green.

recipes.name: Akara
recipes.description: Nigerian black-eyed pea fritters blended with onion and pepper, whipped light, then fried until crisp outside and fluffy inside.
recipes.story: Akara is one of Nigeria's great breakfast and street foods: peeled black-eyed peas, onion, pepper, salt, and hot oil. The magic is in peeling well and whipping air into the batter before frying.
recipes.occasion: Best for breakfast, Saturday street-food plates, Ramadan iftar, or a snack tray.
recipes.best_served_with: Pap, custard, agege bread, ogi, hot sauce, or garri.
recipes.regional_variations: Across West Africa, related bean fritters appear as akara, koose, akla, and more. Nigerian versions often stay simple with onion and chile, while richer versions may add shrimp or bell pepper.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Soak and peel
steps.instruction: Soak beans until softened, rub off the skins, and rinse until mostly clean.
steps.tip: Peeling is the work, but it gives akara its light texture.

steps.title: Blend thick
steps.instruction: Blend peeled beans with onion, peppers, and as little water as possible until smooth and thick.

steps.title: Whip the batter
steps.instruction: Beat the batter with salt until slightly lighter and airy.
steps.tip: Air in the batter helps the fritters puff.

steps.title: Fry the akara
steps.instruction: Drop spoonfuls into hot oil and fry until deep golden, turning once. Drain and serve hot.

recipes.name: Amala, Ewedu and Gbegiri
recipes.description: A classic Yoruba plate of yam flour swallow served with jute leaf ewedu, silky bean gbegiri, and stew.
recipes.story: Amala with ewedu and gbegiri is one of the great Yoruba plates: dark yam flour swallow, stretchy jute leaf soup, bean soup, and red stew sharing one bowl.
recipes.occasion: Best for weekend lunch, buka-style plates, parties, and proper swallow cravings.
recipes.best_served_with: Assorted meat stew, ponmo, beef, goat, or fish.
recipes.regional_variations: Some plates use abula, the full combination of ewedu, gbegiri, and stew. Others serve only ewedu and stew.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

ingredients.name: peeled brown beans or black-eyed peas

steps.title: Cook gbegiri
steps.instruction: Cook peeled beans until very soft, then blend smooth with cooking liquid.

steps.title: Finish gbegiri
steps.instruction: Return blended beans to the pot with palm oil, crayfish if using, and salt. Simmer until silky.

steps.title: Cook ewedu
steps.instruction: Boil ewedu leaves briefly with optional potash, then whisk or blend until stretchy. Season lightly.

steps.title: Make amala
steps.instruction: Bring water to a boil, reduce heat, sprinkle in yam flour, and beat until smooth and elastic.

steps.title: Plate abula style
steps.instruction: Serve amala with ewedu, gbegiri, and pepper stew in the same bowl.

recipes.name: Ayamase
recipes.description: Yoruba green pepper stew cooked with bleached palm oil, iru, assorted meats, and boiled eggs, usually served with Ofada rice.
recipes.story: Ayamase is intense, smoky, and unmistakably Yoruba. Green peppers, bleached palm oil, iru, and assorted meats create the stew that makes Ofada rice feel complete.
recipes.occasion: Best for parties, Sunday lunch, and serious rice plates.
recipes.best_served_with: Ofada rice, boiled eggs, fried plantain, or plain white rice.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks use more green bell pepper, some more green scotch bonnet, and the meat mix depends on the table.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Blend and reduce
steps.instruction: Blend green peppers and onion coarsely, then boil down until thick and most water evaporates.

steps.title: Bleach palm oil safely
steps.instruction: Heat palm oil in a covered pot over medium heat until darker and smoky, then turn off heat and let it cool before opening.
steps.tip: Ventilate the kitchen and never open the hot smoky pot toward your face.

steps.title: Fry the stew
steps.instruction: Add iru to the oil, then the reduced pepper blend. Fry until the oil begins to separate.

steps.title: Add meat and eggs
steps.instruction: Add assorted meats, crayfish if using, and salt. Simmer, then add eggs and coat well.

recipes.name: Banga Soup
recipes.description: Nigerian palm nut soup with meat or fish, banga spices, dried fish, crayfish, chile, and fresh herbs.
recipes.story: Banga soup is a southern Nigerian palm nut soup, especially loved in the Niger Delta. The palm fruit extract gives body, while banga spices, dried fish, crayfish, and herbs bring its signature aroma.
recipes.occasion: Best for starch, eba, fufu, rice, and weekend family meals.
recipes.best_served_with: Starch, eba, pounded yam, white rice, or fufu.
recipes.regional_variations: Delta-style banga often uses banga spice blends and starch, while Igbo ofe akwu leans toward rice and a slightly different herb profile.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Cook protein
steps.instruction: Cook meat or fish with salt until tender, keeping the stock.

steps.title: Add palm base
steps.instruction: Stir palm nut concentrate into stock with water and simmer until it loosens and starts to thicken.

steps.title: Season deeply
steps.instruction: Add dried fish, crayfish, banga spice, pepper, and salt. Simmer until the soup is glossy.

steps.title: Finish with herbs
steps.instruction: Add scent leaf or bitterleaf and simmer briefly before serving.

recipes.name: Boli
recipes.description: Nigerian roasted plantain served hot with groundnuts or a spicy pepper sauce.
recipes.story: Boli is the smell of Nigerian streets: plantain roasting over heat until charred, sweet, smoky, and soft. It can be as simple as groundnuts or as lively as pepper sauce.
recipes.occasion: Best for street-food plates, snacks, lunch, or barbecue sides.
recipes.best_served_with: Roasted groundnuts, pepper sauce, grilled fish, or avocado.
recipes.regional_variations: In some southern cities, boli is paired with fish and pepper sauce. In simpler versions, roasted groundnuts do all the work.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Prepare plantains
steps.instruction: Peel plantains and leave them whole or cut each in half crosswise.

steps.title: Roast
steps.instruction: Roast over charcoal, grill, or hot oven, turning often until charred in spots and tender.

steps.title: Make pepper sauce
steps.instruction: Cook tomatoes, peppers, onion, oil, and salt until thick and glossy.

steps.title: Serve hot
steps.instruction: Serve roasted plantain with groundnuts, pepper sauce, or grilled fish.

recipes.name: Dan Wake
recipes.description: Hausa bean flour dumplings boiled until tender, then dressed with oil, yaji, onions, tomato, and boiled egg.
recipes.story: Dan Wake is a northern Nigerian bean dumpling dish, humble and filling. The dumplings are mild, then the plate wakes up with yaji, oil, fresh onion, tomato, and egg.
recipes.occasion: Best for breakfast, lunch, and quick street-food-style meals.
recipes.best_served_with: Yaji, pepper oil, sliced onions, tomatoes, cabbage, cucumber, and boiled eggs.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks add kuka powder to the dough, some use cassava flour as binder, and toppings vary from simple oil and pepper to a full salad plate.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Mix the dough
steps.instruction: Combine bean flour, cassava flour, optional kuka, salt, and enough water to make a soft sticky dough.

steps.title: Boil the dumplings
steps.instruction: Bring water to a rolling boil. Drop small spoonfuls of dough into the pot and cook until firm.

steps.title: Drain
steps.instruction: Lift the dumplings out with a slotted spoon and rinse briefly with hot water to separate them.

steps.title: Dress and serve
steps.instruction: Toss with oil and yaji, then top with onions, tomato, and boiled egg if using.

recipes.name: Edikang Ikong
recipes.description: Efik-Ibibio vegetable soup loaded with ugu, waterleaf, palm oil, meat, stockfish, crayfish, and periwinkles.
recipes.story: Edikang Ikong is a vegetable-rich South-South soup where the greens are the main event. Ugu and waterleaf cook down with seafood, meat, palm oil, and crayfish into a dense, celebratory pot.
recipes.occasion: Best for celebrations, Sunday lunch, and hearty swallow meals.
recipes.best_served_with: Garri, fufu, pounded yam, or semolina.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions use spinach outside Nigeria, but fluted pumpkin leaves and waterleaf are the classic combination.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Cook the protein
steps.instruction: Cook assorted meat and stockfish until tender with a small amount of stock left.

steps.title: Add waterleaf
steps.instruction: Stir in waterleaf and cook until it wilts and releases liquid.

steps.title: Season the soup
steps.instruction: Add palm oil, crayfish, pepper, periwinkles if using, and salt. Simmer until the oil blends in.

steps.title: Finish with ugu
steps.instruction: Add ugu leaves and cook briefly until tender and bright. Serve hot.

recipes.name: Efo Riro
recipes.description: A Yoruba spinach and pepper stew cooked with palm oil, locust beans, crayfish, and assorted meats or fish.
recipes.story: Efo Riro is a rich Yoruba vegetable soup where greens are folded into a pepper base enriched with palm oil, iru, crayfish, and cooked proteins. The magic is to cook the pepper base until it tastes deep, then fold in the greens briefly so they stay lively.
recipes.occasion: Best for family meals, swallow plates, and rice bowls.
recipes.best_served_with: Amala, eba, pounded yam, fufu, boiled rice, or plantain.
recipes.regional_variations: Amaranth, efo shoko, efo tete, or spinach may be used depending on market access. Protein mixes vary widely.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Blend and reduce the peppers
steps.instruction: Blend bell peppers, scotch bonnet, and half the onion into a coarse pepper base. Simmer until some water cooks off.
steps.tip: A reduced base keeps the soup rich instead of watery.

steps.title: Fry the aromatics
steps.instruction: Heat palm oil gently, add sliced onion and iru, then fry until fragrant. Stir in the pepper base and crayfish.

steps.title: Add the proteins
steps.instruction: Add cooked meat and smoked fish. Simmer so the pepper sauce coats the proteins and the oil rises slightly.

steps.title: Fold in the greens
steps.instruction: Add squeezed greens in batches, stirring until just wilted and coated. Taste and season with salt.
steps.tip: Do not cook the greens to mush. Efo Riro should still have body.

recipes.name: Egusi Soup
recipes.description: Nigerian egusi soup with ground melon seeds, palm oil, pepper base, assorted meats, stockfish, crayfish, and leafy greens.
recipes.story: Egusi can be smooth or lumpy. For this pot, spoon in a thick egusi paste and let the lumps set before stirring gently.
recipes.occasion: Everyday meals and family gatherings
recipes.best_served_with: Pounded yam, eba, amala, semovita, fufu, or starch swallow
recipes.image_alt: Egusi Soup recipe from Nigeria on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Cook the meats
steps.instruction: Season beef and tripe with onions, seasoning cubes, and salt. Boil until tender, about 25 minutes. Reserve the stock.
steps.tip: The meat stock is the flavour backbone of the soup.

steps.title: Prepare egusi paste
steps.instruction: Mix ground egusi with a small amount of warm water to form a thick paste.

steps.title: Build the soup base
steps.instruction: Heat palm oil in a pot. Add chopped onions and fry until softened. Add blended peppers and cook for 10 minutes.

steps.title: Add egusi
steps.instruction: Scoop egusi paste in spoonfuls into the pot forming lumps. Add reserved meat stock and stir gently. Cover and simmer for 15 minutes.
steps.tip: Do not stir too much — let the egusi clumps set.

steps.title: Add meats and greens
steps.instruction: Add cooked meats and stockfish. Stir gently and cook for 5 minutes. Add chopped greens and cook 3-5 minutes until wilted.
steps.tip: Add greens last so they keep their colour.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Adjust seasoning and serve hot alongside pounded yam or any preferred swallow.

recipes.name: Fisherman Soup
recipes.description: South-South Nigerian seafood soup with fresh fish, prawns, periwinkles, crayfish, pepper, yam thickener, and aromatic leaves.
recipes.ethnic_group: Rivers, Akwa Ibom, and coastal Nigerian
recipes.story: Fisherman soup is coastal Nigerian comfort, built around fresh seafood and a spicy broth. Fish, prawns, periwinkles, crayfish, and aromatic leaves make it taste like the river and the kitchen met in one pot.
recipes.occasion: Best for seafood lovers, Sunday lunch, and swallow meals by the tableful.
recipes.best_served_with: Fufu, eba, pounded yam, boiled plantain, or rice.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions use yam or cocoyam as thickener, some add okra, and seafood changes with what is fresh.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Make thickener
steps.instruction: Boil yam or cocoyam until soft, then pound into a smooth paste.

steps.title: Season the broth
steps.instruction: Simmer stock with onion, crayfish, pepper, and salt until flavorful.

steps.title: Cook the fish
steps.instruction: Add fish steaks and simmer gently until almost cooked.

steps.title: Finish seafood
steps.instruction: Add prawns, periwinkles if using, thickener, and herbs. Simmer until the soup thickens and seafood is just cooked.

recipes.name: Masa
recipes.description: Northern Nigerian fermented rice cakes cooked in small wells until lightly crisp outside and soft inside.
recipes.story: Masa, also called waina, is a northern Nigerian rice cake with a gentle fermented tang. It is cooked in a special pan or an ebelskiver-style pan and eaten with pepper sauce, yaji, soup, or honey.
recipes.occasion: Best for breakfast, market snacks, Ramadan tables, or a light supper.
recipes.best_served_with: Yaji, suya pepper, miyan taushe, honey, or spicy tomato sauce.
recipes.regional_variations: Some batters include cooked rice for softness, some use yeast, and some rely more on natural fermentation.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Soak the rice
steps.instruction: Soak raw rice overnight, then drain well.

steps.title: Blend the batter
steps.instruction: Blend soaked rice, cooked rice, sugar, salt, yeast, and warm water into a smooth thick batter.

steps.title: Ferment
steps.instruction: Cover and leave the batter in a warm place until bubbly and slightly risen.

steps.title: Cook the masa
steps.instruction: Oil a masa pan, pour batter into each well, and cook until set and golden, turning once.

recipes.name: Miyan Kuka
recipes.description: Northern Nigerian baobab leaf soup cooked with meat stock, dried fish, dawadawa, palm oil, chile, and fine kuka powder.
recipes.story: Miyan Kuka gets its unmistakable body from powdered baobab leaves. The soup is earthy, slightly tangy, and deeply northern Nigerian, especially when dawadawa and dried fish perfume the stock.
recipes.occasion: Best for tuwo meals, northern family lunch, and rainy-day soup bowls.
recipes.best_served_with: Tuwo shinkafa, tuwo masara, or rice.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks use potash, some skip it, and meat can be beef, goat, dried fish, or a mix.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Cook the meat
steps.instruction: Simmer meat with salt and water until tender and the stock tastes rich.

steps.title: Build the soup
steps.instruction: Add dried fish, dawadawa, pepper, crayfish if using, and palm oil. Simmer until the oil settles into the stock.

steps.title: Add kuka
steps.instruction: Lower the heat and sprinkle in sifted kuka powder while stirring constantly.
steps.tip: Do not dump the powder in one spot. It can clump fast.

steps.title: Finish gently
steps.instruction: Simmer briefly until the soup thickens and turns silky. Adjust salt and serve.

recipes.name: Miyan Taushe
recipes.description: Northern Nigerian pumpkin and groundnut soup with meat, leafy greens, tomato-pepper base, dawadawa, and warm spices.
recipes.story: Miyan Taushe is a rich northern Nigerian soup where pumpkin softens into the pot and groundnut paste gives body. It is generous, nutty, lightly sweet, and built for tuwo.
recipes.occasion: Best for weekend northern meals, guests, and cold evenings.
recipes.best_served_with: Tuwo shinkafa, tuwo masara, rice, or couscous.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions include sorrel leaves, spinach, yakuwa, or bitter greens. The groundnut level changes by household.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Cook meat and pumpkin
steps.instruction: Simmer meat with onion, salt, and water until nearly tender. Add pumpkin and cook until soft.

steps.title: Add tomato pepper base
steps.instruction: Stir in blended tomatoes and peppers, dawadawa, and dried pepper. Simmer until the raw tomato taste fades.

steps.title: Enrich with groundnut
steps.instruction: Loosen groundnut paste with warm stock, then stir it into the soup and simmer gently.
steps.tip: Stir often because groundnut can catch at the bottom.

steps.title: Finish with greens
steps.instruction: Mash some pumpkin into the soup, add greens, and cook until just tender. Adjust salt.

recipes.name: Moi Moi
recipes.description: A steamed Nigerian bean pudding made from peeled black-eyed peas blended with peppers, onion, oil, and seasoning, then cooked until set and tender.
recipes.story: Moi Moi is one of Nigeria's classic bean dishes, often served beside jollof rice, fried rice, pap, garri, or bread. A good batter is smooth, lightly peppery, and soft after steaming, with optional egg, fish, corned beef, or crayfish folded in when the table calls for it.
recipes.occasion: Best for Sunday rice plates, parties, breakfast spreads, and make-ahead meal prep.
recipes.best_served_with: Jollof rice, fried rice, pap, custard, garri, or soft bread.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks use banana leaves for aroma, some use ramekins or foil bags. Add-ins vary by household, with boiled egg, fish, corned beef, and crayfish all common.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

ingredients.name: warm water
ingredients.prep_note: plus more as needed for blending

steps.title: Soak and peel the beans
steps.instruction: Soak the beans until the skins loosen, then rub and rinse until most skins are removed. Drain well.
steps.tip: Pre-peeled beans save time, but whole beans give the most control over texture.

steps.title: Blend the batter
steps.instruction: Blend peeled beans with bell pepper, scotch bonnet, onion, oil, nutmeg, crayfish if using, and enough warm water to make a smooth pourable batter.
steps.tip: The batter should pour like thick pancake batter, not sit like paste.

steps.title: Season and fill
steps.instruction: Season with salt, fold in optional egg pieces, and pour into leaves, ramekins, tins, or foil cups. Leave headroom for expansion.

steps.title: Steam until set
steps.instruction: Set the cups in a pot with a little simmering water, cover tightly, and steam until the pudding is firm through the center.
steps.tip: Top up hot water around the cups if the pot dries, but keep water out of the batter.

steps.title: Rest and serve
steps.instruction: Let the Moi Moi rest briefly so it firms up, then serve warm or at room temperature.

recipes.name: Nigerian Pepper Soup
recipes.description: A hot, aromatic Nigerian broth made with goat, chicken, fish, or assorted meat, pepper soup spice, chile, onion, and scent leaves.
recipes.story: Pepper soup is a flexible Nigerian broth served in homes, bars, and gatherings. It should be hot, aromatic, and clear enough to sip, with meat or fish, ehu or pepper soup spice, chile heat, onion, and fresh herbs like scent leaf or uziza.
recipes.occasion: Best for cold evenings, celebrations, recovery meals, and small chops menus.
recipes.best_served_with: White rice, boiled yam, agidi, or served alone as a hot broth.
recipes.regional_variations: Goat meat, chicken, catfish, assorted beef, cow foot, and yam versions are common. Scent leaf is preferred, while uziza or parsley may appear as substitutes.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

ingredients.name: pepper soup spice or ground ehu

steps.title: Start the meat
steps.instruction: Place meat in a pot with quartered onion, salt, and enough water to cover. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a steady simmer.
steps.tip: Goat and cow foot take longer than chicken or fish. Top up water as needed.

steps.title: Season the broth
steps.instruction: Add pepper soup spice or ground ehu, scotch bonnet, thyme if using, and sliced onion. Simmer until the broth smells aromatic.

steps.title: Finish with herbs
steps.instruction: Stir in scent leaves or uziza and simmer briefly. Taste for salt and pepper.

steps.title: Serve hot
steps.instruction: Serve immediately as a broth, or ladle over boiled yam, rice, or agidi.

recipes.name: Ofe Nsala
recipes.description: Igbo white soup made without palm oil, thickened with yam and cooked with catfish, crayfish, uziza, and utazi.
recipes.story: Ofe Nsala is called white soup because it skips palm oil. The flavor is clean and peppery, with yam thickener, catfish, crayfish, uziza, and a little utazi bitterness.
recipes.occasion: Best for swallow meals, special family cooking, and peppery fish soup cravings.
recipes.best_served_with: Pounded yam, semolina, fufu, or boiled yam.
recipes.regional_variations: Catfish is common, but chicken or goat can be used. Some cooks add more uziza for heat and aroma.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Make yam thickener
steps.instruction: Boil yam until soft, then pound or blend into a smooth paste.

steps.title: Season the broth
steps.instruction: Bring water or stock to a simmer with crayfish, uziza, pepper, and salt.

steps.title: Cook the fish
steps.instruction: Add catfish pieces and simmer gently until almost cooked.

steps.title: Thicken and finish
steps.instruction: Add yam paste in small lumps, simmer until dissolved and thickened, then finish with utazi.

recipes.name: Oha Soup
recipes.description: Igbo oha leaf soup cooked with cocoyam thickener, palm oil, meat, dried fish, crayfish, and ogiri.
recipes.story: Oha soup is one of the treasured Igbo soups, with tender oha leaves added late so their fragrance stays fresh. Cocoyam gives the soup body and palm oil gives it warmth.
recipes.occasion: Best for Sunday swallow, celebrations, and family cooking.
recipes.best_served_with: Pounded yam, fufu, garri, or semolina.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks use achi or ofor as thickener when cocoyam is unavailable, but cocoyam is the classic route.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Cook meat and fish
steps.instruction: Simmer meat until tender, then add dried fish and continue until the stock is rich.

steps.title: Prepare cocoyam
steps.instruction: Boil cocoyam until soft, peel, and pound into a smooth paste.

steps.title: Thicken the soup
steps.instruction: Add palm oil, crayfish, pepper, ogiri if using, and cocoyam paste in small lumps. Simmer until thickened.

steps.title: Add oha leaves
steps.instruction: Stir in torn oha leaves and cook briefly until just wilted. Adjust salt.

recipes.name: Okpa
recipes.description: Eastern Nigerian Bambara nut pudding steamed with palm oil, pepper, onion, and salt until firm and tender.
recipes.story: Okpa is a beloved eastern Nigerian breakfast and road-trip food made from Bambara nut flour. The batter is simple, but the steamed result is rich, nutty, and filling.
recipes.occasion: Best for breakfast, travel food, school lunch, and make-ahead snacks.
recipes.best_served_with: Pap, custard, tea, or eaten on its own.
