recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks steam okpa in banana leaves, some in cups or bags. Enugu-style okpa is especially famous.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Mix flour and oil
steps.instruction: Rub palm oil into okpa flour until evenly colored.

steps.title: Make batter
steps.instruction: Add warm water gradually with pepper, onion if using, and salt. Mix into a pourable batter.

steps.title: Fill wraps
steps.instruction: Pour batter into banana leaves, heatproof cups, or food-safe steaming bags, leaving room to expand.

steps.title: Steam until set
steps.instruction: Steam until firm all the way through and no raw bean smell remains.

recipes.name: Puff Puff
recipes.description: Soft Nigerian fried dough balls made from yeasted flour batter, lightly sweetened and fried until golden.
recipes.story: Puff Puff is a beloved Nigerian snack and small-chops staple. The common version is a simple yeasted batter of flour, sugar, salt, warm water, and sometimes nutmeg, fried by the spoonful until round and golden.
recipes.occasion: Best for parties, breakfast snacks, tea time, and street-food platters.
recipes.best_served_with: Tea, zobo, pap, or a spicy pepper dip.
recipes.regional_variations: Similar fried dough snacks appear across West Africa under names such as bofrot, kala, or mikate, with small changes in spice and texture.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Mix the batter
steps.instruction: Combine flour, sugar, yeast, salt, and nutmeg if using. Stir in warm water until the batter is smooth, stretchy, and thicker than pancake batter.

steps.title: Let it rise
steps.instruction: Cover the bowl and leave in a warm place until the batter is bubbly and roughly doubled.

steps.title: Fry in batches
steps.instruction: Heat oil over medium heat. Scoop batter into the oil with wet fingers or a spoon and fry, turning often, until deep golden.
steps.tip: If the outside browns before the center cooks, lower the heat.

steps.title: Drain and serve
steps.instruction: Drain on paper towels and serve warm.

recipes.name: Suya
recipes.description: Nigerian suya, thin beef skewers coated in yaji, the smoky Hausa spice blend of kuli-kuli, chile, ginger, onion, garlic, and salt.
recipes.story: Suya should be sliced thin enough to cook fast, but not so thin that it dries out. The final shower of yaji is part of the dish.
recipes.occasion: Evening snack and street food
recipes.best_served_with: Raw onion, tomato, cabbage, cucumber, extra yaji, or jollof rice
recipes.image_alt: Suya recipe from Nigeria on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

ingredients.name: kuli-kuli powder or roasted peanut powder

steps.title: Make the yaji spice
steps.instruction: Combine groundnut powder, cayenne, ginger, onion powder, paprika, and salt. Mix thoroughly.
steps.tip: Toast the groundnuts in a dry pan first for deeper flavour.

steps.title: Prepare the meat
steps.instruction: Thread thinly sliced beef onto wooden skewers. Brush with oil and coat generously with yaji spice on all sides.
steps.tip: Soak wooden skewers in water for 30 minutes to prevent burning.

steps.title: Marinate
steps.instruction: Let coated skewers rest for at least 30 minutes, or refrigerate up to 2 hours.

steps.title: Grill the suya
steps.instruction: Grill over high charcoal heat, turning every 2-3 minutes, until charred on edges but juicy inside. About 8-10 minutes total.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Sprinkle extra yaji spice over the grilled suya. Serve with sliced onions, tomatoes, and fresh cabbage.

collections.name: Street Food
collections.description: Popular street snacks and quick bites found at markets and roadside stalls across Africa.

recipes.name: Tuwo Shinkafa
recipes.description: A northern Nigerian rice swallow cooked until soft, mashed smooth, and shaped into mounds for soups like miyan kuka or miyan taushe.
recipes.story: Tuwo Shinkafa is a northern Nigerian staple made from soft-cooked rice pounded or stirred into a smooth swallow. It is plain by design, because the soup beside it carries the seasoning.
recipes.occasion: Best for northern soup meals, family lunch, and any table built around a thick draw or leafy soup.
recipes.best_served_with: Miyan kuka, miyan taushe, okra soup, or groundnut soup.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks use short-grain rice, some use broken rice, and some finish with rice flour if the pot needs more structure.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Cook the rice soft
steps.instruction: Add rice and water to a heavy pot. Bring to a boil, then cook until the grains are very soft.

steps.title: Mash the grains
steps.instruction: Lower the heat and mash with a wooden spoon or masher until the rice breaks down.

steps.title: Beat into tuwo
steps.instruction: Keep stirring and folding until the mixture pulls together into a smooth swallow.
steps.tip: Splash in hot water if it stiffens before becoming smooth.

steps.title: Shape and serve
steps.instruction: Scoop into wet bowls or wrap briefly to shape, then serve hot with soup.

recipes.name: Mbisi ya Makala
recipes.description: Congolese charcoal-grilled river fish with bright pili-pili sauce made from chile, onion, garlic, lemon, and oil.
recipes.story: Keep the fish simple and the fire hot. The pili-pili brings the bite, while plantain or chikwangue catches the juices.
recipes.occasion: Riverside dining, everyday meals, gatherings
recipes.best_served_with: Fried plantains, chikwangue, rice, cassava, or cucumber salad
recipes.image_alt: Mbisi ye Kalou recipe from Republic of Congo on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

ingredients.name: tilapia, capitaine, or other firm river fish

steps.title: Prepare the fish
steps.instruction: Rinse the tilapia and pat dry. Make 3-4 diagonal slashes on each side. Rub with oil and season generously with salt inside and out.
steps.tip: Scoring helps the heat penetrate evenly and lets the seasoning flavour the flesh.

steps.title: Make the pili-pili sauce
steps.instruction: Combine the chopped chillies, diced onion, lemon juice, oil, and a pinch of salt in a bowl. Mix well and set aside to let the flavours meld.
steps.tip: Make this at least 15 minutes ahead for the best flavour.

steps.title: Grill the fish
steps.instruction: Heat a charcoal grill or grill pan to high heat. Grill the fish for 8-10 minutes per side until the skin is charred and crispy and the flesh is opaque and flakes easily.
steps.tip: Oil the grill grate well to prevent sticking. Do not move the fish until it releases naturally.

steps.title: Flip and finish
steps.instruction: Carefully flip the fish and grill the other side for another 8 minutes until cooked through.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Transfer the grilled fish to a platter and spoon the pili-pili sauce generously over the top. Serve immediately with boiled or fried plantains.

recipes.name: Saka-Saka au Poisson Fumé
recipes.description: Congolese saka-saka with smoked fish, pounded cassava leaves, palm oil, peanut butter, onion, garlic, and chile.
recipes.story: Saka-saka cannot be rushed. Cassava leaves need a long simmer before they become soft, savory, and ready to carry smoked fish.
recipes.occasion: Daily meals, communal gatherings
recipes.best_served_with: Chikwangue, fufu, rice, plantain, or poulet muambe
recipes.image_alt: Saka-Saka au Poisson Fumé recipe from Republic of Congo on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Cook leaves
steps.instruction: Simmer cassava leaves with water or fish stock until they begin to soften.

steps.title: Make base
steps.instruction: Cook onion, garlic, green pepper or leek if using, and chile in palm oil until fragrant.

steps.title: Combine
steps.instruction: Stir the palm-oil base and peanut butter into the cassava leaves until the sauce looks rich.

steps.title: Add fish
steps.instruction: Fold in smoked fish and simmer gently until the leaves are very tender and the fish seasons the pot.

steps.title: Finish
steps.instruction: Taste before adding salt, then serve hot with chikwangue, fufu, rice, or plantain.

recipes.name: Igisafuliya
recipes.description: Rwandan igisafuliya, a one-pot stew of chicken or beef, green plantains, potatoes, tomatoes, carrots, and green beans.
recipes.story: Igisafuliya means the pot is doing the work. Let the plantain soften into the broth so the stew thickens naturally.
recipes.best_served_with: Rice, ubugali, avocado, fried plantains, or a simple tomato salad
recipes.image_alt: Igisafuria recipe from Rwanda on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Brown Meat
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a large pot. Season beef with salt and sear until browned on all sides.

steps.title: Build Stew
steps.instruction: Add onions and garlic, cook until softened. Add tomatoes and cook until they break down into a sauce.

steps.title: Add Vegetables
steps.instruction: Add plantains, potatoes, carrots, green beans, and water. Drop in the whole scotch bonnet. Bring to a boil.
steps.tip: Keep the scotch bonnet whole for gentle heat — piercing it will make the stew very spicy.

steps.title: Simmer
steps.instruction: Reduce heat, cover, and simmer until all vegetables are tender and the stew has thickened naturally from the plantains breaking down.
steps.tip: The plantains will partially dissolve and thicken the stew — this is desired.

steps.title: Season and Serve
steps.instruction: Remove scotch bonnet. Adjust salt to taste. Serve hot in deep bowls — this is a complete meal in itself.
steps.tip: Igisafuria improves with reheating the next day as flavors deepen overnight.

collections.name: One-Pot Wonders
collections.description: Hearty single-pot dishes that are easy to make and full of flavor. Less cleanup, more taste.

recipes.name: Isombe
recipes.description: Rwandan isombe, pounded cassava leaves simmered with peanut butter, palm oil, eggplant, spinach, onion, garlic, and pepper.
recipes.story: The peanut butter is what turns the greens into isombe. Add it after the leaves soften so it melts into a rich, smooth sauce.
recipes.best_served_with: Ubugali, rice, boiled plantains, beans, or avocado
recipes.image_alt: Isombe recipe from Rwanda on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Start leaves
steps.instruction: Simmer pounded cassava leaves with water or vegetable stock until they begin to soften.

steps.title: Cook vegetables
steps.instruction: In palm oil, cook onion, garlic, eggplant, and bell pepper until soft and fragrant.

steps.title: Combine
steps.instruction: Stir the vegetable base into the cassava leaves. Add the whole chile if using.

steps.title: Add peanut
steps.instruction: Stir in peanut butter until it melts into the sauce. Simmer until the leaves are tender and the stew is thick.

steps.title: Add spinach
steps.instruction: Fold in spinach or amaranth leaves and cook until wilted. Adjust salt and pepper before serving.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Rwandan Brochettes
recipes.description: Rwandan goat brochettes grilled over charcoal with tomato, onion, garlic, chile, and oil marinade.
recipes.story: Brochettes are bar food and celebration food at once. Keep the pieces even, grill over real heat, and serve them straight from the fire.
recipes.best_served_with: Fried potatoes, fried plantains, kachumbari, ugali, or cold beer
recipes.image_alt: Rwandan Brochettes recipe from Rwanda on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Marinate meat
steps.instruction: Mix tomatoes, grated onion, garlic, chile if using, oil, salt, and pepper. Coat the meat and rest.

steps.title: Thread skewers
steps.instruction: Thread meat onto skewers with a few onion chunks, leaving small gaps for even heat.

steps.title: Grill
steps.instruction: Grill over hot charcoal, turning often, until smoky and cooked through.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve hot with fried potatoes or plantains and tomato-onion salad.

collections.name: Street Food
collections.description: Popular street snacks and quick bites found at markets and roadside stalls across Africa.

recipes.name: Angú de Banana
recipes.description: São Toméan angú de banana, green bananas or breadfruit boiled soft, mashed smooth, and finished with palm oil and salt.
recipes.story: Angú should be smooth enough to carry calulu sauce. Pound while hot and loosen only with a little cooking water if needed.
recipes.occasion: Daily staple
recipes.best_served_with: Calulu, grilled fish, fish stew, or palm-oil vegetables
recipes.image_alt: Angú de Banana recipe from São Tomé and Príncipe on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Boil the bananas
steps.instruction: Place peeled green bananas in salted boiling water. Cook until very soft, about 15-20 minutes.
steps.tip: They should be completely soft — undercooked bananas won't mash smoothly.

steps.title: Mash
steps.instruction: Drain the bananas. Mash with a potato masher or wooden pestle until completely smooth with no lumps.

steps.title: Add palm oil
steps.instruction: Stir in palm oil and salt. Mix until the angú is smooth, golden, and uniform.
steps.tip: The palm oil gives it a beautiful golden colour and rich flavour.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Shape into a mound and serve alongside calulu or other stews.

collections.name: Quick & Easy
collections.description: Delicious African dishes ready in 30 minutes or less. Perfect for busy weeknight dinners.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Ceebu Yapp
recipes.description: Senegalese meat and rice cooked with vegetables, onion, mustard, garlic, chile, and rich stock.
recipes.story: Ceebu yapp is Senegal's meat rice cousin to thieboudienne. The rice absorbs a deeply seasoned meat stock, with vegetables and a bright onion-mustard profile.
recipes.occasion: Best for family lunch, large pots, and rice-centered gatherings.
recipes.best_served_with: Cucumber salad, hot pepper sauce, or simple pickled vegetables.
recipes.regional_variations: Beef, lamb, or goat can be used, and vegetables shift by market and household.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Brown meat
steps.instruction: Season and brown meat in oil until well colored.

steps.title: Build stock
steps.instruction: Add onions, garlic, mustard, pepper, vegetables, and stock. Simmer until meat is tender.

steps.title: Cook rice
steps.instruction: Remove vegetables, add rinsed rice to the stock, and cook covered until liquid is absorbed.

steps.title: Steam and serve
steps.instruction: Steam on low heat, then serve rice with meat and vegetables on top.

recipes.name: Chicken Yassa
recipes.description: Senegalese chicken yassa, grilled or browned chicken braised in caramelized onions, lemon, mustard, garlic, chile, and bay leaf.
recipes.story: The onions are the sauce. Give the lemon-mustard marinade time, then cook the onions down until sweet enough to balance the citrus.
recipes.occasion: Celebrations and gatherings
recipes.best_served_with: White rice, fonio, fried plantain, cucumber salad, or extra chile
recipes.image_alt: Chicken Yassa recipe from Senegal on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Marinate the chicken
steps.instruction: Combine chicken with lemon juice, half the onions, mustard, garlic, salt, and pepper. Refrigerate at least 2 hours.
steps.tip: Overnight marination is best.

steps.title: Grill the chicken
steps.instruction: Remove chicken from marinade (reserve it). Grill until golden and slightly charred, about 15 minutes.

steps.title: Caramelise the onions
steps.instruction: Heat oil. Add all onions and cook on medium-low until deeply caramelised, about 25 minutes.
steps.tip: Low and slow is the key.

steps.title: Combine and simmer
steps.instruction: Add grilled chicken, reserved marinade, and whole scotch bonnet. Cover and simmer 20 minutes.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve chicken smothered in onion sauce over fluffy white rice.

recipes.name: Dibi
recipes.description: Senegalese grilled lamb or meat seasoned simply, grilled over high heat, and served with mustard-onion sauce.
recipes.story: Dibi is Senegalese grilled meat culture: smoky lamb or beef, simple seasoning, hot fire, and a sharp mustard-onion sauce that cuts through the richness.
recipes.occasion: Best for grilling, gatherings, and street-food-style dinners.
recipes.best_served_with: Mustard onions, bread, fries, salad, or hot pepper.
recipes.regional_variations: Lamb is common, but beef or goat may be used. Seasoning can stay simple or include garlic, chile, and stock powder.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Season meat
steps.instruction: Rub meat with oil, garlic, pepper, and salt. Rest briefly.

steps.title: Prepare onions
steps.instruction: Toss onions with mustard, lemon juice, salt, and pepper.

steps.title: Grill hot
steps.instruction: Grill meat over high heat, turning until charred outside and cooked through.

steps.title: Rest and serve
steps.instruction: Rest meat, then serve with mustard onions and bread or fries.

recipes.name: Fataya
recipes.description: Senegalese fried pastries filled with spiced fish or meat and served with tomato-onion dipping sauce.
recipes.story: Fataya are Senegalese hand pies, crisp outside and savory inside. Fish versions are especially beloved, often eaten with a sharp tomato-onion sauce.
recipes.occasion: Best for snacks, parties, street-food trays, and Ramadan tables.
recipes.best_served_with: Tomato-onion sauce, hot pepper, or tea.
recipes.regional_variations: Fillings may be fish, beef, chicken, or vegetables. Shape and spice vary by cook.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Make dough
steps.instruction: Mix flour, oil, salt, and enough water to make a firm dough. Rest covered.

steps.title: Cook filling
steps.instruction: Cook onion, garlic, tomato paste, fish, salt, and pepper until dry.

steps.title: Fill pastries
steps.instruction: Roll dough thin, cut circles, fill, fold, and seal edges.

steps.title: Fry
steps.instruction: Fry in hot oil until golden and crisp, then drain.

recipes.name: Mafe
recipes.description: Senegalese mafé, a peanut and tomato stew with beef, sweet potatoes, carrots, onion, garlic, ginger, and slow-cooked sauce.
recipes.story: Mafé should taste roasted and creamy, not like raw peanut butter. Fry the tomato paste, loosen the peanut paste with warm stock, then simmer patiently.
recipes.occasion: Everyday family meals
recipes.best_served_with: White rice, fonio, couscous, or steamed millet
recipes.image_alt: Mafe recipe from Senegal on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

ingredients.name: natural peanut butter or groundnut paste

steps.title: Brown the meat
steps.instruction: Heat oil. Season beef and brown on all sides. Remove and set aside.

steps.title: Build the base
steps.instruction: Fry onions until soft. Add tomato paste and cook 3 minutes. Add garlic and ginger.

steps.title: Add peanut butter
steps.instruction: Dissolve peanut butter in 3 cups warm water. Pour into pot with browned meat. Simmer.
steps.tip: Dissolve fully to avoid lumps.

steps.title: Add vegetables
steps.instruction: Add sweet potatoes, carrots. Simmer until tender and sauce is thick, 35-40 minutes.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Ladle the mafe over fluffy white rice and serve hot, with enough sauce to coat the rice without drowning the vegetables.

recipes.name: Ndambe
recipes.description: Senegalese black-eyed pea stew simmered with tomato, onion, chile, and spices, often served in bread.
recipes.story: Ndambe is Senegalese black-eyed pea stew, rich with tomato and onion and often tucked into bread as a filling street-food breakfast or snack.
recipes.occasion: Best for breakfast sandwiches, lunch bowls, and budget-friendly meal prep.
recipes.best_served_with: Baguette, rice, couscous, or salad.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions include meat, but black-eyed peas in tomato sauce are the common heart of the dish.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Cook beans
steps.instruction: Cook soaked black-eyed peas in water until tender.

steps.title: Make sauce
steps.instruction: Cook onions, tomatoes, tomato paste, pepper, paprika, salt, and pepper in oil until thick.

steps.title: Simmer together
steps.instruction: Add cooked beans and a little bean liquid. Simmer until creamy and thick.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve in bread or over rice, with extra pepper if desired.

recipes.name: Soupe Kandia
recipes.description: Senegalese okra and seafood stew with palm oil, fish, shrimp, dried seafood, vegetables, and rice.
recipes.story: Soupe kandia is Senegal's rich okra and seafood stew, glossy with palm oil and full of fish, shrimp, dried seafood, vegetables, and rice on the side.
recipes.occasion: Best for seafood lunches, family meals, and okra lovers.
recipes.best_served_with: White rice and hot pepper condiment.
recipes.regional_variations: Seafood varies by coast and market, and some versions add meat alongside fish.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Cook base
steps.instruction: Cook onions, garlic, tomatoes, palm oil, salt, and pepper until softened.

steps.title: Build okra stew
steps.instruction: Add half the okra and stock. Simmer until the stew thickens.

steps.title: Add fish
steps.instruction: Add fish and dried shrimp powder if using. Simmer gently.

steps.title: Finish seafood
steps.instruction: Add shrimp and remaining okra. Cook just until shrimp turn pink.

recipes.name: Thiakry
recipes.description: Senegalese millet couscous dessert folded into sweet yogurt or milk with vanilla, nutmeg, and fruit.
recipes.story: Thiakry is Senegal's cool millet dessert, a soft mix of steamed millet couscous and sweetened yogurt or milk scented with vanilla and nutmeg.
recipes.occasion: Best for dessert, Ramadan tables, parties, and chilled snacks.
recipes.best_served_with: Fresh fruit, raisins, coconut, or a light dinner.
recipes.regional_variations: Millet couscous is traditional, but some modern versions use couscous, cream, evaporated milk, or fruit cocktail.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Steam millet
steps.instruction: Steam or hydrate millet couscous with water until tender.

steps.title: Cool
steps.instruction: Fluff and cool the millet completely.

steps.title: Mix cream
steps.instruction: Whisk yogurt, milk, sugar, vanilla, and nutmeg until smooth.

steps.title: Fold and chill
steps.instruction: Fold in millet and optional fruit. Chill before serving.

recipes.name: Grilled Fish Creole
recipes.description: Seychellois grilled fish with garlic, ginger, lime, chile, and a chunky Creole tomato sauce.
recipes.story: Use the freshest fish you can find and do not bury it. The Creole sauce should brighten the fish, not hide it.
recipes.best_served_with: Rice, lentils, fried plantain, breadfruit, or cucumber salad
recipes.image_alt: Grilled Fish Creole recipe from Seychelles on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

ingredients.name: snapper, grouper, or firm fish fillets

steps.title: Season Fish
steps.instruction: Rub fish fillets with olive oil, lemon juice, and salt. Let rest 15 minutes.

steps.title: Make Creole Sauce
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a pan. Saute onion, garlic, and ginger until fragrant. Add tomatoes, scotch bonnet, and thyme. Cook until tomatoes break down into a chunky sauce.
steps.tip: The sauce should be chunky, not smooth — the texture is part of the Creole style.

steps.title: Grill Fish
steps.instruction: Grill fish fillets over high heat or in a hot pan for 3-4 minutes per side until skin is crispy and flesh flakes easily.
steps.tip: Do not move the fish too early — let the skin crisp and release naturally from the grill.

steps.title: Finish Sauce
steps.instruction: Stir parsley into the Creole sauce. Season with salt to taste.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Place grilled fish on plates and spoon Creole sauce generously over the top. Serve with rice and fried plantains.
steps.tip: In Seychelles, this dish is enjoyed with your feet in the sand and the ocean in view.

recipes.name: Ladob
recipes.description: Seychellois sweet ladob, ripe plantains and sweet potatoes simmered in coconut milk with vanilla, nutmeg, sugar, and salt.
recipes.story: Ladob should be creamy but still shaped. Simmer gently so the coconut milk thickens while the plantain and sweet potato stay intact.
recipes.best_served_with: Tea, grilled fish meal finish, coconut flakes, or fresh fruit
recipes.image_alt: Ladob dessert from Seychelles on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Combine
steps.instruction: Place coconut milk, water, sugar, vanilla bean and seeds, nutmeg, and salt in a pot. Bring to a gentle simmer.

steps.title: Add Vegetables
steps.instruction: Add sweet potato chunks first as they take longer to cook. Simmer until slightly softened.

steps.title: Add Plantains
steps.instruction: Add plantain halves. Continue simmering gently until both are tender but still holding their shape.
steps.tip: Do not stir too vigorously — you want the plantains and sweet potatoes to stay intact.

steps.title: Thicken
steps.instruction: If sauce is too thin, gently remove fruit and reduce the coconut sauce until creamy. Return fruit.
steps.tip: The sauce should be thick enough to coat the back of a spoon.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve warm in bowls with plenty of the vanilla coconut sauce spooned over. Remove vanilla pod before serving.
steps.tip: Ladob is equally delicious served cold the next day — the flavors deepen overnight.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Shark Chutney
recipes.description: Seychellois shark chutney, or satini reken, made with boiled flaked shark, bilimbi, lime, onion, ginger, garlic, turmeric, and chile.
recipes.story: The chutney should be tangy and textured, not watery. Flake the fish fine, then cook it just enough for the bilimbi and spice to grip.
recipes.best_served_with: Rice, lentils, grilled fish, breadfruit, or Creole curry
recipes.image_alt: Shark Chutney recipe from Seychelles on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

ingredients.name: shark fillet or firm white fish

steps.title: Boil Shark
steps.instruction: Place shark fillet in a pot of salted water. Boil until cooked through and firm. Drain, remove any skin, and flake the meat finely.
steps.tip: Boiling removes any strong flavor from the shark — drain well.

steps.title: Cook Bilimbi
steps.instruction: In a pan, heat oil and saute onion, garlic, and ginger until soft. Add bilimbi and cook until soft and pulpy.

steps.title: Combine
steps.instruction: Add flaked shark meat, turmeric, scotch bonnet, and lime juice to the pan. Mash and stir until well combined and mixture has a rough paste-like consistency.
steps.tip: The chutney should be chunky, not smooth — the texture is important.

steps.title: Season
steps.instruction: Season with salt. Cook 2 more minutes, stirring constantly, to meld flavors.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Let cool to room temperature. Serve as a condiment alongside rice, lentils, or grilled fish.
steps.tip: Shark chutney keeps well refrigerated for 3-4 days and the flavor improves over time.

recipes.name: Banana Akara
recipes.description: Sierra Leonean banana akara, sweet rice-flour fritters made with ripe banana, sugar, nutmeg, and hot oil.
recipes.story: This is Salone akara, not the bean fritter from elsewhere. The banana should be ripe enough to perfume the batter before it hits the oil.
recipes.occasion: Breakfast and snacks
recipes.best_served_with: Ginger beer, tea, onion gravy, bread, or a dusting of sugar
recipes.image_alt: Akara recipe from Sierra Leone on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Mash bananas
steps.instruction: Mash ripe bananas with sugar and nutmeg until mostly smooth.

steps.title: Make batter
steps.instruction: Stir in rice flour and baking powder if using. Add warm water only if needed to make a thick spoonable batter.

steps.title: Rest batter
steps.instruction: Let the batter rest so the rice flour hydrates and the mixture firms slightly.

steps.title: Fry akara
steps.instruction: Drop spoonfuls into hot oil and fry until golden on both sides.

steps.title: Drain and serve
steps.instruction: Drain well and serve hot with tea, ginger beer, bread, or onion gravy.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

collections.name: Street Food
collections.description: Popular street snacks and quick bites found at markets and roadside stalls across Africa.

recipes.name: Binch Akara
recipes.description: Sierra Leonean black-eyed bean fritters, whipped light and fried until crisp, often sold with onion gravy or bread.
recipes.ethnic_group: Sierra Leonean Krio and West African
recipes.story: Binch akara is Sierra Leone's bean fritter language: peeled black-eyed peas, onion, pepper, air beaten into the batter, and a hot-oil finish.
recipes.occasion: Best for breakfast, snacks, street-food boards, and party trays.
recipes.best_served_with: Onion gravy, bread rolls, pepper sauce, or pap.
recipes.regional_variations: Akara names travel across West Africa; Sierra Leonean vendors often serve binch akara as a street snack with onion gravy.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Peel beans
steps.instruction: Soak beans, rub off skins, and rinse until mostly clean.

steps.title: Blend batter
steps.instruction: Blend beans with onion, pepper, and minimal water into a thick paste.

steps.title: Whip and season
steps.instruction: Whip batter with salt and cayenne until lighter and slightly airy.

steps.title: Fry
steps.instruction: Drop spoonfuls into hot oil and fry until golden and crisp.

recipes.name: Groundnut Stew
recipes.description: Sierra Leonean groundnut stew with chicken, peanut butter, tomato, onion, garlic, ginger, chile, and rich stock.
recipes.story: The peanut needs heat and patience. Simmer until the sauce turns glossy and thick enough to coat rice.
recipes.occasion: Everyday meals
recipes.best_served_with: White rice, plantain, fufu, cassava, or gari
recipes.image_alt: Groundnut Stew recipe from Sierra Leone on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

ingredients.name: smooth peanut butter or groundnut paste
sweet potato, carrot, cabbage, or okra

steps.title: Cook the chicken
steps.instruction: Season chicken and boil until tender, about 25 minutes. Reserve stock.

steps.title: Fry the base
steps.instruction: Heat oil, fry onions until golden. Add blended tomatoes and cook 10 minutes until reduced.

steps.title: Add peanut butter
steps.instruction: Dissolve peanut butter in 2 cups chicken stock. Pour into the tomato base and stir well.

steps.title: Simmer the stew
steps.instruction: Add chicken pieces and scotch bonnets. Simmer 20 minutes until thick and creamy.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Serve over white rice or with fufu.

recipes.name: Bariis Iskukaris
recipes.description: Somali bariis iskukaris, spiced basmati rice cooked with goat or lamb broth, xawaash, caramelized onion, raisins, and warm spices.
recipes.story: The rice should taste like the meat broth and the spice pot together. Cook the meat first, then let the rice finish in that broth.
recipes.best_served_with: Banana, salad, basbaas, roasted meat, or yogurt
recipes.image_alt: Bariis Iskukaris recipe from Somalia on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Cook Meat
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a pot. Brown goat meat on all sides. Add one diced onion, garlic, and half the xawaash. Add water to cover and simmer until tender. Reserve broth.
steps.tip: Cook the meat until very tender — the broth becomes the flavorful liquid for the rice.

steps.title: Caramelize Onions
steps.instruction: In a separate pot, heat oil and caramelize the remaining onion until golden brown.

steps.title: Toast Spices
steps.instruction: Add remaining xawaash, cumin, cardamom, and cinnamon to the onions. Stir for 1 minute until fragrant.

steps.title: Cook Rice
steps.instruction: Add drained rice and raisins. Pour in 4 cups of the reserved meat broth. Bring to boil, reduce to lowest heat, cover tightly, and cook until rice is fluffy.
steps.tip: Use the meat broth instead of water — this is what makes Somali rice so flavorful.

steps.title: Combine
steps.instruction: Fluff rice and gently fold in the cooked meat. Let rest covered for 5 minutes.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Mound on a large platter, garnish with cilantro. Serve with banana and salad on the side.
steps.tip: Somalis traditionally eat bariis with a banana — the sweetness complements the spices.

collections.name: Sunday Specials
collections.description: Elaborate weekend dishes worth the extra effort. These are the recipes that make Sunday lunch special.

collections.name: One-Pot Wonders
collections.description: Hearty single-pot dishes that are easy to make and full of flavor. Less cleanup, more taste.

recipes.name: Canjeero
recipes.description: Somali canjeero, a lightly fermented one-sided pancake with a soft, spongy surface and gentle tang.
recipes.story: The bubbles are the sign. Give the batter time to ferment, then cook it thin and only on one side.
recipes.best_served_with: Sugar, honey, sesame oil, stew, suqaar, tea, or banana
recipes.image_alt: Canjeero recipe from Somalia on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

ingredients.name: fine cornmeal, sorghum flour, or whole wheat flour

steps.title: Mix batter
steps.instruction: Whisk flours, sugar, yeast or starter, salt, and warm water into a thin smooth batter.

steps.title: Ferment
steps.instruction: Cover loosely and let the batter ferment until bubbly and lightly sour, several hours or overnight.

steps.title: Adjust texture
steps.instruction: Stir gently and add water if needed. The batter should pour more thinly than pancake batter.

steps.title: Cook one side
steps.instruction: Pour a thin layer into a lightly oiled pan. Cook without flipping until holes form and the surface sets.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Fold and serve warm with sweet toppings or with stew and suqaar.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Somali Sambusa
recipes.description: Crisp Somali fried pastries filled with spiced beef, onion, garlic, cilantro, chile, and xawaash.
recipes.story: Sambusa is a Ramadan favorite and party snack across Somali homes. The triangle is crisp, the filling is dry enough to keep the pastry flaky, and xawaash gives the beef its warm Somali signature.
recipes.occasion: Best for Ramadan iftar, tea time, parties, and snack trays.
recipes.best_served_with: Green chutney, basbaas, lemon wedges, or Somali tea.
recipes.regional_variations: Fillings can be beef, lamb, chicken, tuna, or vegetables. Homemade wrappers are common, but spring roll wrappers are a practical shortcut.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Cook the filling
steps.instruction: Cook beef with onion, garlic, chile, xawaash, cumin, salt, and pepper until browned and dry. Stir in cilantro and cool.
steps.tip: A wet filling makes the wrappers split.

steps.title: Fold triangles
steps.instruction: Place filling on each wrapper strip and fold into tight triangles. Seal the edge with flour paste.

steps.title: Fry in batches
steps.instruction: Fry sambusas in hot oil until golden and crisp on both sides.

steps.title: Drain and serve
steps.instruction: Drain on a rack or paper towel and serve hot with basbaas or lemon.

recipes.name: Suqaar
recipes.description: Somali suqaar, small-cut meat sauteed with onion, garlic, peppers, tomato, chile, and xawaash.
recipes.story: Suqaar is quick food, so cut the meat small and keep the pan hot. The vegetables should soften into a light sauce around the meat.
recipes.best_served_with: Canjeero, rice, pasta, sabaayad, banana, or basbaas
recipes.image_alt: Suqaar recipe from Somalia on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Sear Meat
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a pan over high heat. Add diced beef and sear until browned, stirring occasionally.
steps.tip: Keep the heat high so the meat sears rather than steams — work in batches if needed.

steps.title: Add Aromatics
steps.instruction: Add onion, garlic, and jalapeno. Cook until softened.

steps.title: Spice
steps.instruction: Add cumin, coriander, and turmeric. Stir to coat the meat evenly.

steps.title: Add Vegetables
steps.instruction: Add peppers and tomatoes. Cook until peppers are tender and tomatoes have broken down into a sauce.
steps.tip: The tomatoes should cook down to a thick coating, not a watery sauce.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Season with salt. Serve suqaar with canjeero for breakfast, or with rice or pasta for lunch and dinner.
steps.tip: Somalis love suqaar with spaghetti — a legacy of Italian colonial influence.

recipes.name: Chakalaka
recipes.description: South African chakalaka, a spicy vegetable relish with carrots, peppers, tomato, baked beans, curry powder, onion, garlic, and chile.
recipes.story: Chakalaka should be bright, spicy, and spoonable. Cook the vegetables until tender but not tired, then let the relish settle before serving.
recipes.occasion: Braai
recipes.best_served_with: Pap, braai meat, boerewors, bread, rice, or samp
recipes.image_alt: Chakalaka recipe from South Africa on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Sauté aromatics
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a large pot over medium heat. Add the onions and fry until translucent, about 4 minutes. Add garlic and curry powder, stirring for 1 minute until fragrant.

steps.title: Add vegetables
steps.instruction: Add the grated carrots and diced peppers. Cook for 5 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the vegetables begin to soften.

steps.title: Add tomatoes and beans
steps.instruction: Stir in the chopped tomatoes and chilli peppers. Simmer for 10 minutes until the sauce thickens and the vegetables are tender.
steps.tip: Adjust the chilli to your heat preference — some like it fiery, others mild.

steps.title: Add beans and finish
steps.instruction: Add the baked beans, stir gently, and simmer for another 10 minutes. Season with salt and pepper to taste.

steps.title: Rest and serve
steps.instruction: Remove from heat and let sit for 5 minutes before serving. Chakalaka can be served warm or at room temperature alongside pap and braai meat.
steps.tip: Chakalaka tastes even better the next day as the flavors develop overnight.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Malva Pudding
recipes.description: A warm South African baked pudding flavored with apricot jam, then soaked with a buttery cream sauce.
recipes.story: Malva pudding is a South African favorite with a soft sponge, apricot jam, vinegar and bicarbonate lift, and a hot cream sauce poured over the pudding as it comes from the oven.
recipes.occasion: Best for Sunday lunch, winter dessert, braai desserts, and holiday tables.
recipes.best_served_with: Custard, vanilla ice cream, or whipped cream.
recipes.regional_variations: Some recipes use evaporated milk, cream, or milk in the sauce. The apricot jam and hot soak are the important shared features.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Make the sponge batter
steps.instruction: Beat egg and sugar until pale. Mix in apricot jam, melted butter, and vinegar. Fold in flour, bicarbonate of soda, salt, and milk.

steps.title: Bake
steps.instruction: Pour into a greased baking dish and bake at 180 C until risen and golden.

steps.title: Make the sauce
steps.instruction: Warm cream, sugar, butter, and hot water until the sugar dissolves and the sauce is smooth.

steps.title: Soak the pudding
steps.instruction: Pierce the hot pudding with a skewer or fork and pour the warm sauce over it gradually so it absorbs.
steps.tip: Serve warm, not cold, for the classic soft texture.

recipes.name: Milk Tart
recipes.description: South African milk tart with a sweet pastry shell, creamy milk custard, and cinnamon on top.
recipes.story: Melktert is the South African milk custard tart that tastes like a quiet afternoon: sweet crust, soft milk filling, and cinnamon on top. It is lighter and milkier than many egg custard pies.
recipes.occasion: Best for tea time, Sunday dessert, birthdays, and make-ahead entertaining.
recipes.best_served_with: Rooibos tea, coffee, or fresh fruit.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions bake the custard in the shell, while others cook the custard first and set it in a blind-baked crust.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Infuse the milk
steps.instruction: Heat milk with the cinnamon stick until steaming. Turn off heat and let it infuse briefly, then remove the stick.

steps.title: Mix the custard
steps.instruction: Whisk sugar, eggs, cornstarch, flour, and salt until smooth. Slowly whisk in hot milk.

steps.title: Cook until thick
steps.instruction: Return custard to the pot and cook over medium-low heat, whisking constantly, until thick and glossy. Stir in butter and vanilla.
steps.tip: Whisk the corners of the pot so no starch clumps hide there.

steps.title: Fill and chill
steps.instruction: Pour custard into the pastry shell, dust with cinnamon, and chill until set before slicing.

recipes.name: Asida
recipes.description: South Sudanese asida, a smooth dome of sorghum, millet, or maize flour served with stew in the center.
recipes.story: Asida is about texture. Stir hard until it turns smooth and heavy, then shape it while hot so the stew has a place to pool.
recipes.best_served_with: Bamia, mulah, meat stew, okra sauce, or vegetable stew
recipes.image_alt: Asida recipe from South Sudan on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Boil water
steps.instruction: Bring water to a boil with salt and oil if using.

steps.title: Stir flour
steps.instruction: Lower heat and add flour gradually while stirring hard to prevent lumps.

steps.title: Cook thick
steps.instruction: Keep stirring until the asida is smooth, thick, and pulls from the pot.

steps.title: Shape
steps.instruction: Wet a bowl or spoon, shape into a dome, and make a well for stew.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Spoon bamia, mulah, or another stew into the well and around the base.

collections.name: Quick & Easy
collections.description: Delicious African dishes ready in 30 minutes or less. Perfect for busy weeknight dinners.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Bamia
recipes.description: South Sudanese bamia, lamb and okra stew with tomato, onion, garlic, coriander, and a bright lemon finish.
recipes.story: Give the meat its time before the okra joins the pot. That keeps the lamb tender and the okra from disappearing.
recipes.best_served_with: Asida, kisra, rice, or flatbread
recipes.image_alt: Bamia recipe from South Sudan on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

ingredients.name: okra
ingredients.prep_note: trimmed, left whole if small or sliced if large

steps.title: Brown meat
steps.instruction: Season and brown the meat in oil until the edges color.

steps.title: Build tomato base
steps.instruction: Add onion, garlic, tomatoes, tomato paste, coriander, and cumin if using. Cook until the tomatoes soften.

steps.title: Simmer meat
steps.instruction: Add water or stock and simmer until the meat is nearly tender.

steps.title: Add okra
steps.instruction: Add okra and cook until tender and the stew thickens.

steps.title: Finish
steps.instruction: Stir in lemon or lime juice and adjust salt before serving.

recipes.name: Kisra with Mulah
recipes.description: South Sudanese kisra with mulah, fermented sorghum flatbread served with a rich okra and meat stew.
recipes.story: Kisra needs fermentation for its tang and texture. Cook it thin, then use it like edible cloth for scooping the mulah.
recipes.best_served_with: Bamia, meat mulah, okra sauce, lentil stew, or vegetable stew
recipes.image_alt: Kisra with Mulah recipe from South Sudan on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Ferment kisra
steps.instruction: Mix sorghum flour and warm water into a thin batter. Cover and ferment until lightly sour and bubbly.

steps.title: Cook mulah base
steps.instruction: Brown meat in oil, then add onions, garlic, tomatoes, and water or stock. Simmer until the meat is tender.

steps.title: Finish mulah
steps.instruction: Add okra and peanut butter if using. Simmer until the stew thickens.

steps.title: Cook kisra
steps.instruction: Salt the fermented batter, thin if needed, and pour a very thin layer on a hot pan. Cook one side until set.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Fold or roll kisra and serve with mulah for scooping.

recipes.name: Agashe
recipes.description: Sudanese skewered beef rubbed with peanut, chili, ginger, garlic, and warm spices, then grilled over high heat.
recipes.story: Agashe is Sudan's peanut-spiced grilled meat, kin to the wider Sahelian suya family but with its own street-side Sudanese character. Thin beef is rubbed with peanut powder, chili, ginger, and garlic before a fast grill.
recipes.occasion: Best for street-food nights, cookouts, and party skewers.
recipes.best_served_with: Onion, tomato, lime, flatbread, salad, and chili powder.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks use lamb or chicken, but beef skewers with peanut spice are the common street-food image.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Make the rub
steps.instruction: Mix peanut powder, paprika, cayenne, ginger, garlic, cumin, and salt.

steps.title: Coat beef
steps.instruction: Thread beef onto skewers and coat with oil and peanut spice.

steps.title: Grill hot
steps.instruction: Grill over high heat, turning once or twice, until browned and just cooked.

steps.title: Rest and serve
steps.instruction: Rest briefly and serve with onion, tomato, lime, and extra spice.

recipes.name: Basbousa
recipes.description: Sudanese-style basbousa, a syrup-soaked semolina cake with yoghurt, butter, almonds, coconut, and rose water.
recipes.story: The syrup has to meet the cake at the right moment. Cold syrup over hot basbousa gives a tender, sweet crumb without making it soggy.
recipes.occasion: Eid, weddings, celebrations
recipes.best_served_with: Tea, coffee, fresh fruit, or extra syrup
recipes.image_alt: Basbousa dessert from Sudan on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make the syrup
steps.instruction: Combine 1.5 cups sugar and 1 cup water in a saucepan. Bring to a boil, add lemon juice, and simmer for 8 minutes until slightly thickened. Stir in rose water and set aside to cool completely.
steps.tip: The syrup MUST be cold when poured on the hot cake — this is the secret to proper absorption.

steps.title: Mix the batter
steps.instruction: Combine the semolina, 1 cup sugar, yoghurt, melted butter, baking powder, and coconut. Mix well and let rest for 30 minutes so the semolina absorbs the moisture.
steps.tip: Resting is essential — it softens the semolina for a smoother cake.

steps.title: Bake
steps.instruction: Preheat oven to 180°C (350°F). Spread the batter evenly in a greased 23×33cm baking dish. Score into diamond shapes and press an almond into each piece. Bake for 25-30 minutes until golden.
steps.tip: Score before baking so the syrup penetrates deeply into each piece.

steps.title: Soak with syrup
steps.instruction: Remove the hot cake from the oven and immediately pour the cold syrup evenly over the entire surface. Let it sit for at least 30 minutes to absorb.
steps.tip: You will hear the satisfying sizzle when the cold syrup hits the hot cake.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Re-cut along the scored lines and serve at room temperature. Basbousa keeps well covered at room temperature for 2-3 days.
steps.tip: In Sudan, basbousa is always served with strong coffee or sweet tea with milk.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

collections.name: Sunday Specials
collections.description: Elaborate weekend dishes worth the extra effort. These are the recipes that make Sunday lunch special.

recipes.name: Gurasa
recipes.description: Sudanese soft wheat flatbread cooked on a griddle and served with stews, mullah, or spiced meat.
recipes.story: Gurasa is a soft Sudanese griddle bread, thicker and more tender than many flatbreads. It is built to catch stews, sauces, and meat juices, especially mullah and shaiyah.
recipes.occasion: Best for lunch platters, stew meals, and communal tables.
recipes.best_served_with: Mullah ahmar, mullah rob, shaiyah, lentil stew, or peanut sauce.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions are plain flour and water, while others add a little yeast or oil for softness.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Mix batter
steps.instruction: Whisk flour, yeast, salt, oil, and warm water into a thick smooth batter.

steps.title: Rest
steps.instruction: Cover and let the batter stand until slightly bubbly and relaxed.

steps.title: Cook on griddle
steps.instruction: Pour onto an oiled griddle and spread into thick rounds, cooking until set and lightly browned.

steps.title: Keep soft
steps.instruction: Stack under a towel so the breads steam and stay tender.

recipes.name: Kisra with Mullah Tagalia
recipes.description: Sudanese kisra with mullah tagalia, fermented sorghum crepes served with thick onion, tomato, meat, coriander, and red pepper sauce.
recipes.story: Good kisra brings the tang, and tagalia brings the depth. Cook the onions long enough for the sauce to turn dark and glossy.
recipes.occasion: Daily family lunch or dinner
recipes.best_served_with: Salad, lime, extra chile, or asida
recipes.image_alt: Kisra with Mullah Tagalia recipe from Sudan on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Prepare kisra batter
steps.instruction: Mix sorghum flour with warm water and a pinch of yeast to form a thin, pourable batter (thinner than crepe batter). Cover and let ferment at room temperature for 12-24 hours until slightly sour.
steps.tip: Traditional fermentation uses no yeast — just time. The natural bacteria create the sour tang.

steps.title: Cook the kisra
steps.instruction: Heat a large flat pan or griddle (doka). Pour a thin layer of batter and quickly spread it in a circular motion using a palette or the back of a spoon. Cook until set and edges lift, about 1 minute. Do not flip — kisra is cooked on one side only.
steps.tip: Sudanese women are expert at spreading kisra paper-thin with a special gourd tool called a mafrash.

steps.title: Build the mullah
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a deep pot. Fry the onions over medium heat, stirring often, until deeply caramelised and dark brown, about 20 minutes. Add the dried meat, tomatoes, coriander, and 2 cups of water. Simmer for 20 minutes.
steps.tip: The dark onions are the soul of tagalia — don't rush this step.

steps.title: Add peanut butter
steps.instruction: Stir in the peanut butter until fully dissolved. Simmer for another 10 minutes until the stew is thick and rich. Season with salt.
steps.tip: The peanut butter thickens the stew and adds a nutty depth.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Lay kisra on a large platter or individual plates. Ladle the mullah over the top. Eat by tearing pieces of kisra and scooping the stew.
steps.tip: In Sudanese tradition, everyone eats from one large shared platter.

recipes.name: Mullah Ahmar Tagalia
recipes.description: Sudanese red meat stew thickened with dried okra powder and seasoned with onion, tomato, garlic, and spices.
recipes.story: Mullah ahmar tagalia is a Sudanese red stew built around meat, tomato, onion, and powdered dried okra. The okra powder thickens the sauce into the texture that makes kisra or gurasa essential.
recipes.occasion: Best for family lunch, Eid leftovers, and stew-and-bread meals.
recipes.best_served_with: Kisra, gurasa, rice, or asida.
recipes.regional_variations: Dried okra powder may be called waika, and the stew can be red with tomato or pale and yogurt-based in other mullah styles.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Brown meat
steps.instruction: Brown meat with oil and onions until the onions soften and the meat starts to color.

steps.title: Simmer red base
steps.instruction: Add garlic, tomatoes, tomato paste, spices, salt, and water, then simmer until meat is tender.

steps.title: Thicken with okra
steps.instruction: Whisk dried okra powder with cool broth and stir it into the pot.

steps.title: Finish slowly
steps.instruction: Simmer gently until the stew thickens and tastes rounded.

recipes.name: Shaiyah
recipes.description: Sudanese fried lamb or beef cooked with onion, garlic, tomato, cumin, coriander, and lemon.
recipes.story: Shaiyah is Sudanese pan-fried meat, often made with lamb, cooked until browned and savory with onion, garlic, tomato, cumin, coriander, and lemon. It is direct, generous, and made for bread.
recipes.occasion: Best for Eid meat, quick lunch platters, and shared family trays.
recipes.best_served_with: Gurasa, kisra, salad, chili, or rice.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions are dry-fried with very little tomato, while others add tomato and a splash of water to make a light sauce.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Brown meat
steps.instruction: Fry meat in oil over medium-high heat until browned on several sides.

steps.title: Cook aromatics
steps.instruction: Add onions and cook until golden, then stir in garlic, tomatoes, spices, and salt.

steps.title: Tenderize
steps.instruction: Add a small splash of water, cover, and cook until the meat is tender.

steps.title: Fry dry
steps.instruction: Uncover and cook until the liquid reduces and the meat fries in its oil. Finish with lemon.

recipes.name: Chipsi Mayai
recipes.description: Tanzanian chipsi mayai, a street-food omelette built around crisp fries, eggs, onion, tomato, and chile.
recipes.story: The chips have to stay proud inside the egg. Fry them first, then set the omelette around them instead of drowning them.
recipes.best_served_with: Kachumbari, ketchup, pili pili sauce, or extra tomato salad
recipes.image_alt: Chipsi Mayai recipe from Tanzania on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Fry Chips
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a deep pan to 170C. Fry potato chips in batches until golden and crispy. Drain on paper towels.
steps.tip: Double frying makes crispier chips — fry once at lower heat, rest, then fry again at higher heat.

steps.title: Prepare Egg Mix
steps.instruction: Beat eggs with salt, pepper, diced onion, tomato, and chili.

steps.title: Combine
steps.instruction: Place fried chips in a hot oiled skillet. Pour beaten egg mixture over the chips, making sure eggs fill all the gaps.
steps.tip: Press chips down so they are fully coated in egg.

steps.title: Cook Omelette
steps.instruction: Cook on medium heat until the bottom is golden and set. Carefully flip using a plate and cook the other side until golden.
steps.tip: Use a plate larger than the pan to flip — slide omelette onto plate, then invert back into pan.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Slide onto a plate and cut into wedges. Serve hot with ketchup and pili pili sauce on the side.
steps.tip: Best eaten immediately while chips are still crispy inside the omelette.

collections.name: Quick & Easy
collections.description: Delicious African dishes ready in 30 minutes or less. Perfect for busy weeknight dinners.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

collections.name: Street Food
collections.description: Popular street snacks and quick bites found at markets and roadside stalls across Africa.

recipes.name: Tanzanian Pilau
recipes.description: Tanzanian pilau, Zanzibar-influenced spiced rice with caramelized onions, meat, potatoes, garlic, ginger, and whole spices.
recipes.story: Pilau lives in the onion and spice oil. Let the onions color deeply before rice and stock go in.
recipes.best_served_with: Kachumbari, banana, pili pili, coconut beans, or grilled meat
recipes.image_alt: Tanzanian Pilau recipe from Tanzania on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Toast Spices
steps.instruction: Dry-toast cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, cumin, and peppercorns in a hot pan until fragrant. Set aside.
steps.tip: Toasting whole spices releases their essential oils for deeper flavor.

steps.title: Caramelize Onions
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a heavy pot. Add onions and cook until deeply browned and caramelized.

steps.title: Brown Meat
steps.instruction: Add beef and garlic to onions. Sear until browned. Add toasted spices and potatoes. Stir to coat.

steps.title: Cook Pilau
steps.instruction: Add drained rice and hot stock. Bring to a boil, then reduce to lowest heat. Cover tightly and cook until rice is tender and liquid absorbed.
steps.tip: Do not lift the lid during cooking — the steam does the work.

steps.title: Rest and Serve
steps.instruction: Remove from heat. Let rest covered for 10 minutes, then fluff with a fork. Serve garnished with fresh lime wedges.

collections.name: Party Favorites
collections.description: Festive dishes that bring people together. These crowd-pleasers are served at celebrations across Africa.

collections.name: One-Pot Wonders
collections.description: Hearty single-pot dishes that are easy to make and full of flavor. Less cleanup, more taste.

recipes.name: Ugali na Nyama
recipes.description: Tanzanian ugali na nyama, firm maize ugali served with slow-simmered beef, tomato, onion, garlic, and pepper stew.
recipes.story: Start the stew first. Ugali takes minutes, but nyama needs time to soften and make sauce worth scooping.
recipes.best_served_with: Kachumbari, sukuma wiki, pili pili, or avocado
recipes.image_alt: Ugali na Nyama recipe from Tanzania on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Brown Beef
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a heavy pot. Season beef with salt and pepper, then sear until browned on all sides.

steps.title: Cook Aromatics
steps.instruction: Add onions and garlic, cook until softened. Add bell peppers, tomatoes, and curry powder. Stir well.
steps.tip: Crush the tomatoes with your spoon to release their juices.

steps.title: Simmer Stew
steps.instruction: Pour in beef stock. Bring to a boil, then reduce heat. Cover and simmer until beef is fork-tender.
steps.tip: Low heat and patience make the beef meltingly tender.

steps.title: Make Ugali
steps.instruction: Bring 3 cups water to a boil. Gradually add maize flour, stirring vigorously until firm and pulling from sides of pot.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Turn ugali onto a wet plate in a dome shape. Ladle the beef stew alongside. Serve hot.
steps.tip: Tanzanians say the stew should be thick enough to cling to the ugali, not soupy.

recipes.name: Ablo
recipes.description: Togolese steamed rice-and-corn cakes, lightly sweet, soft, and served with stew, grilled fish, or pepper sauce.
recipes.story: Ablo is soft, white, and gently sweet, the steamed cake that makes pepper sauce, fish, or tomato stew feel complete.
recipes.occasion: Best for parties, snacks, breakfast, and stew sides.
recipes.best_served_with: Tomato stew, grilled fish, chicken, pepper sauce, or beans.
recipes.regional_variations: Togo, Benin, Ghana, and Ivory Coast all have related ablo or abolo styles using rice flour, corn flour, starch, yeast, and sugar.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Mix batter
steps.instruction: Whisk flours, starch, sugar, yeast, salt, and warm water into a smooth pourable batter.

steps.title: Ferment
steps.instruction: Cover and let the batter rise until bubbly and slightly expanded.

steps.title: Steam
steps.instruction: Pour into greased molds and steam until set and springy.

steps.title: Rest and serve
steps.instruction: Cool briefly before unmolding and serving with stew or pepper sauce.

recipes.name: Akoumé
recipes.description: Togolese akoumé, a firm corn-flour dough shaped into a smooth mound and served with rich sauces.
recipes.story: The trick is smoothness. Start with a slurry, then add flour gradually and stir until the dough holds its shape.
recipes.occasion: Daily staple
recipes.best_served_with: Gboma dessi, tomato sauce, okra sauce, grilled fish, or chicken stew
recipes.image_alt: Akoumé recipe from Togo on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make slurry
steps.instruction: Mix 1 cup corn flour with 1 cup cold water to a smooth paste.

steps.title: Cook
steps.instruction: Bring 3 cups water to boil with salt. Pour in slurry, stirring constantly. Cook 5 minutes.

steps.title: Thicken
steps.instruction: Add remaining flour gradually, stirring vigorously until very thick and smooth. Cook 10 more minutes.

steps.title: Shape and serve
steps.instruction: Wet a bowl, scoop akoumé in, smooth top, turn out as a mound. Serve with stew.

collections.name: Quick & Easy
collections.description: Delicious African dishes ready in 30 minutes or less. Perfect for busy weeknight dinners.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Djenkoume
recipes.description: Togolese tomato cornmeal dough cooked with onion, garlic, ginger, palm oil, and broth, often served with grilled chicken.
recipes.story: Djenkoume is tomato-seasoned cornmeal with backbone: toasted maize, onion, garlic, ginger, palm oil, and the patience to stir until it turns glossy.
recipes.occasion: Best for grilled chicken, fried fish, and Togolese party plates.
recipes.best_served_with: Togolese grilled chicken, fried fish, tomato sauce, or chili.
recipes.regional_variations: Related to Beninese amiwo, some versions use chicken broth, dried shrimp, coconut milk, or a separate tomato sauce.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Toast cornmeal
steps.instruction: Toast cornmeal in a dry pot, stirring, until it smells nutty but does not burn.

steps.title: Cook tomato base
steps.instruction: In another pot, cook onion, garlic, ginger, tomatoes, tomato paste, and palm oil into a red sauce.

steps.title: Stir dough
steps.instruction: Add broth, then stream in cornmeal while stirring constantly until thick and smooth.

steps.title: Shape
steps.instruction: Cook until the dough pulls from the pot, then shape and serve hot.

recipes.name: Fufu with Gboma Dessi
recipes.description: Togolese fufu with gboma dessi, pounded yam served with spinach tomato stew, palm oil, garlic, ginger, chile, and seafood or meat.
recipes.story: Gboma dessi needs the greens to cook into the tomato base. Let the sauce thicken until it clings to the fufu.
recipes.occasion: Everyday meals
recipes.best_served_with: Akoumé, ablo, rice, or extra chile sauce
recipes.image_alt: Fufu with Gboma Dessi recipe from Togo on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

ingredients.name: beef, smoked fish, crab, or shrimp
red palm oil or vegetable oil

steps.title: Make the fufu
steps.instruction: Boil yam pieces until very soft, about 20 minutes. Pound in a mortar or mash until completely smooth with no lumps.
steps.tip: Alternatively, use instant pounded yam flour.

steps.title: Prepare the stew base
steps.instruction: Heat palm oil. Fry onions until soft. Add blended tomatoes and cook until reduced and oil separates, about 15 minutes.

steps.title: Add fish and greens
steps.instruction: Add smoked fish and stir. Add the chopped greens and cook for 10 minutes until wilted and integrated into the sauce.

steps.title: Season and simmer
steps.instruction: Season with salt and pepper. Simmer 5 more minutes until thick. The stew should coat a spoon.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Shape fufu into smooth balls and serve in a bowl alongside the gboma dessi.

recipes.name: Brik a l'Oeuf
recipes.description: Tunisian brik à l'oeuf, a crisp malsouka pastry filled with egg, tuna, capers, parsley, onion, lemon, and harissa.
recipes.story: Brik waits for no one. Assemble, fry, drain briefly, and eat while the shell still shatters.
recipes.occasion: Ramadan iftar, appetiser
recipes.best_served_with: Lemon wedges, harissa, salad mechouia, or olives
recipes.image_alt: Brik a l'Oeuf recipe from Tunisia on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Make the filling base
steps.instruction: Mix together the flaked tuna, sauteed onion, capers, parsley, salt, and pepper in a bowl.

steps.title: Assemble the brik
steps.instruction: Lay a sheet of malsouka pastry on a flat surface. Spoon a quarter of the tuna mixture onto one half. Make a small well in the centre and crack a whole egg into it. Season the egg with salt and pepper.
steps.tip: Work quickly — the pastry dries out fast.

steps.title: Fold and seal
steps.instruction: Carefully fold the pastry over to form a half-moon shape, pressing the edges to seal. Be gentle so the egg yolk stays intact.
steps.tip: A dab of egg white along the edges helps seal the pastry.

steps.title: Fry until golden
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a deep skillet to 180C (355F). Carefully slide the brik into the hot oil and fry for about 1-2 minutes per side until deep golden and crispy.
steps.tip: The egg should remain runny inside — do not overcook.

steps.title: Drain and serve
steps.instruction: Remove with a slotted spoon and drain briefly on paper towels. Serve immediately with lemon wedges and harissa.
steps.tip: Eat immediately — brik waits for no one.

collections.name: Quick & Easy
collections.description: Delicious African dishes ready in 30 minutes or less. Perfect for busy weeknight dinners.

collections.name: Street Food
collections.description: Popular street snacks and quick bites found at markets and roadside stalls across Africa.

recipes.name: Makroudh
recipes.description: Tunisian semolina pastries filled with date paste, fried or baked, then soaked in honey syrup.
recipes.story: Makroudh is a North African date-filled semolina pastry strongly associated with Kairouan in Tunisia. The diamond-shaped pieces are fried or baked, then dipped in fragrant honey syrup.
recipes.occasion: Best for Eid, weddings, tea trays, and gift boxes.
recipes.best_served_with: Mint tea, coffee, or fresh fruit.
recipes.regional_variations: Tunisia is famous for date-filled makroudh, while Algerian and Moroccan versions may vary the filling, shape, or syrup fragrance.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Hydrate semolina
steps.instruction: Rub semolina with oil or butter and rest so the grains absorb the fat.

steps.title: Make dough and filling
steps.instruction: Add warm water and orange blossom water to form dough, then season date paste with cinnamon and oil.

steps.title: Shape diamonds
steps.instruction: Enclose date paste in ropes of dough, flatten, mark, and cut into diamonds.

steps.title: Fry and honey
steps.instruction: Fry until golden, then dip warm pastries in honey scented with orange blossom water.

recipes.name: Ojja with Merguez
recipes.description: Tunisian ojja with merguez, eggs poached in a fiery tomato, pepper, harissa, garlic, caraway, and coriander sauce.
recipes.story: Ojja is bolder than a gentle egg breakfast. Let the harissa bloom in oil before the eggs go in.
recipes.occasion: Brunch, quick lunch
recipes.best_served_with: Crusty bread, olives, salad, or extra harissa
recipes.image_alt: Ojja with Merguez recipe from Tunisia on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Cook the merguez
steps.instruction: Heat olive oil in a deep skillet over medium heat. Add the merguez slices and cook until browned and slightly crispy, about 5 minutes. Remove and set aside.

steps.title: Build the sauce
steps.instruction: In the same skillet, cook the green pepper and garlic for 2 minutes. Add the harissa, caraway, and a pinch of salt. Stir for 30 seconds until fragrant.

steps.title: Simmer tomatoes
steps.instruction: Add the chopped tomatoes and simmer for 10 minutes until the sauce thickens and reduces. Return the merguez to the skillet.
steps.tip: The sauce should be thick, not watery.

steps.title: Add the eggs
steps.instruction: Make 4 wells in the sauce and crack an egg into each. Cover the skillet and cook for 5-6 minutes until the egg whites are set but the yolks are still runny.
steps.tip: For set yolks, cook a minute or two longer with the lid on.

steps.title: Serve in the skillet
steps.instruction: Remove from heat and serve straight from the skillet with plenty of crusty bread for dipping.
steps.tip: Bring the skillet to the table for a rustic, communal meal.

recipes.name: Slata Mechouia
recipes.description: Tunisian grilled pepper, tomato, garlic, and chili salad finished with olive oil, tuna, eggs, olives, and capers.
recipes.story: Slata mechouia is Tunisia's smoky grilled salad, made by charring peppers, tomatoes, garlic, and chilies, then chopping them into a bright, olive-oil-rich spread.
recipes.occasion: Best for mezze, grilled fish, couscous days, and summer lunches.
recipes.best_served_with: Bread, tuna, hard-boiled eggs, olives, capers, or grilled meats.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions are very hot with extra chilies, while others are milder and topped heavily with tuna and eggs.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Char vegetables
steps.instruction: Grill peppers, tomatoes, chilies, and garlic until blistered and smoky.

steps.title: Steam and peel
steps.instruction: Cover vegetables in a bowl, then peel and seed them once cool enough to handle.

steps.title: Chop and season
steps.instruction: Chop vegetables by hand and dress with olive oil, lemon, caraway, salt, and pepper.

steps.title: Garnish
steps.instruction: Top with tuna, eggs, olives, and capers before serving.

recipes.name: Tunisian Fish Couscous
recipes.description: Tunisian couscous steamed over spicy tomato fish broth with vegetables, chickpeas, and harissa.
recipes.story: Tunisian fish couscous, often called couscous bil hout, brings the coast to the couscousier. Spicy tomato broth, vegetables, chickpeas, and firm fish season the grains as they steam.
recipes.occasion: Best for Friday couscous, coastal lunches, and family gatherings.
recipes.best_served_with: Extra harissa broth, lemon, olives, and salad.
recipes.regional_variations: Fish choice changes by coast and season. Some versions include pumpkin, potatoes, carrots, turnips, or peppers.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Start broth
steps.instruction: Cook onion, tomato paste, harissa, spices, chickpeas, and water into a red broth.

steps.title: Steam couscous
steps.instruction: Rub couscous with oil and steam over the broth, then fluff and steam again.

steps.title: Cook vegetables
steps.instruction: Add carrots and potatoes to the broth, then zucchini near the end.

steps.title: Poach fish
steps.instruction: Add fish steaks and simmer gently until cooked, then serve broth and fish over couscous.

recipes.name: Tunisian Fricassee
recipes.description: Tunisian fried sandwich rolls filled with tuna, potato, egg, olives, harissa, and preserved lemon.
recipes.story: Tunisian fricassee is a little fried roll with a big personality. The warm bread is slit and filled with harissa, tuna, potato, egg, olives, and preserved lemon for a street-food bite that is rich, sharp, and spicy.
recipes.occasion: Best for parties, beach food, street-food spreads, and Ramadan evenings.
recipes.best_served_with: Mint tea, slata mechouia, pickles, or extra harissa.
recipes.regional_variations: Fillings vary by household, but tuna, potato, egg, olives, harissa, and preserved lemon are the common core.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Make dough
steps.instruction: Mix flour, yeast, sugar, salt, and warm water into a soft dough.

steps.title: Rise and shape
steps.instruction: Let rise until puffy, then shape into small oval rolls.

steps.title: Fry rolls
steps.instruction: Fry until golden and cooked through, then drain.

steps.title: Fill
steps.instruction: Slit rolls and fill with harissa, tuna, potato, egg, olives, and preserved lemon.

recipes.name: Tunisian Mloukhia
recipes.description: Tunisian slow-cooked jute leaf stew with beef, olive oil, garlic, bay, coriander, caraway, and chili.
recipes.story: Tunisian mloukhia is a patient, glossy green-black stew made from powdered jute leaf, olive oil, garlic, spices, and beef. It cooks slowly until the oil rises and the texture turns silky.
recipes.occasion: Best for weekend cooking, family gatherings, and make-ahead meals.
recipes.best_served_with: Crusty bread, lemon, pickled peppers, or salad mechouia.
recipes.regional_variations: Some homes use beef, others lamb or tripe. The stew can be very spicy or warmly aromatic depending on the spice paste.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

steps.title: Bloom the powder
steps.instruction: Whisk mloukhia powder into warm olive oil until smooth and dark.

steps.title: Add water slowly
steps.instruction: Whisk in boiling water gradually until the stew is smooth.

steps.title: Simmer with meat
steps.instruction: Add beef, garlic, bay, spices, harissa, and salt, then simmer very gently.

steps.title: Finish glossy
steps.instruction: Cook until the meat is tender and oil rises to the surface. Serve with lemon.

recipes.name: Binyebwa
recipes.description: Ugandan groundnut sauce made from peanut paste simmered with onion, tomato, and gentle seasoning until creamy.
recipes.story: Binyebwa is Uganda's beloved groundnut sauce, simple but deeply comforting. Peanut paste cooks slowly with onion, tomato, and water until the oil rises and the sauce becomes smooth enough for matoke, sweet potatoes, rice, or greens.
recipes.occasion: Best for everyday meals, vegetarian plates, and matoke lunches.
recipes.best_served_with: Matoke, sweet potatoes, cassava, rice, greens, or millet bread.
recipes.regional_variations: Some households add smoked fish, meat, mushrooms, or greens. This base version stays vegetarian so it can sit beside many Ugandan plates.
recipes.author: AfroKitchen research desk

ingredients.name: unsweetened peanut paste or ground roasted peanuts

steps.title: Loosen the paste
steps.instruction: Whisk peanut paste with warm water until smooth and pourable.

steps.title: Cook the aromatics
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a pot and cook onion until soft. Add tomato and simmer until the raw taste fades.

steps.title: Simmer slowly
steps.instruction: Add the peanut mixture, pepper, and salt. Simmer on low heat, stirring often, until thick and creamy.
steps.tip: When a little peanut oil rises to the top, the sauce is ready.

steps.title: Adjust and serve
steps.instruction: Thin with hot water if needed, add optional mushrooms or greens until tender, and serve warm.

recipes.name: Luwombo
recipes.description: Ugandan luwombo, chicken, beef, fish, mushrooms, or groundnut sauce wrapped in banana leaves and steamed until tender.
recipes.story: The banana leaf is the pot. Seal it well and let the steam build a sauce inside the parcel.
recipes.best_served_with: Matoke, rice, posho, sweet potatoes, or greens
recipes.image_alt: Luwombo recipe from Uganda on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Prepare Banana Leaves
steps.instruction: Pass banana leaves briefly over an open flame to soften them and prevent tearing. Wipe clean with a damp cloth.
steps.tip: The leaves should become pliable and darker green — do not char them.

steps.title: Mix Filling
steps.instruction: In a large bowl, combine chicken, ground peanuts, onions, tomatoes, bell peppers, garlic, ginger, mushrooms, salt, pepper, and water. Mix thoroughly.
steps.tip: Mixing the ground peanuts into the raw sauce ensures they cook into a rich, thick gravy.

steps.title: Wrap Parcels
steps.instruction: Place a portion of the mixture onto each banana leaf. Fold the leaf into a secure parcel, tying with banana fiber or kitchen string.
steps.tip: Ensure parcels are tightly sealed so steam cannot escape during cooking.

steps.title: Steam
steps.instruction: Place parcels in a large steamer or pot with a raised rack and water in the bottom. Cover tightly and steam over medium heat until chicken is very tender.

steps.title: Check Doneness
steps.instruction: Carefully open one parcel to check that chicken is cooked through and sauce is thick. If needed, reseal and steam longer.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Place unopened parcels on plates — let guests unwrap their own. Serve with matooke (steamed green bananas) or posho.
steps.tip: The aroma when opening the parcel at the table is part of the experience.

collections.name: Sunday Specials
collections.description: Elaborate weekend dishes worth the extra effort. These are the recipes that make Sunday lunch special.

recipes.name: Matoke
recipes.description: Ugandan matoke, steamed green cooking bananas mashed into tomato, onion, garlic, and pepper sauce.
recipes.story: The bananas should steam until fully soft before mashing. That is how matoke becomes smooth instead of chunky.
recipes.best_served_with: Groundnut sauce, beans, beef stew, chicken luwombo, greens, or avocado
recipes.image_alt: Matoke recipe from Uganda on AfroKitchen
recipes.author: AfroKitchen

steps.title: Prepare Bananas
steps.instruction: Peel green bananas and place in a pot lined with banana leaves. The sap can stain, so oil your hands before peeling.
steps.tip: Rub cooking oil on your hands before peeling to prevent the sticky sap from staining.

steps.title: Make Sauce
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a pan. Saute onions until soft, add garlic, tomatoes, bell peppers, and curry powder. Cook until tomatoes break down into a sauce.

steps.title: Combine and Steam
steps.instruction: Pour the sauce over the bananas. Add water. Wrap banana leaves over the top to seal. Cover pot with a tight lid and steam over low heat.
steps.tip: The banana leaves create a steam chamber that gives matooke its distinctive flavor.

steps.title: Mash
steps.instruction: Once bananas are completely soft, mash them in the pot using a wooden spoon, mixing with the sauce until smooth and golden.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Mound the matooke on a platter, still in its banana leaf. Serve with groundnut sauce, beans, or meat stew.
steps.tip: Matooke should be smooth and lump-free — keep mashing until it reaches a uniform consistency.

collections.name: Vegetarian Africa
collections.description: Plant-based dishes from across the continent. African cuisine has a rich tradition of meatless cooking.

recipes.name: Rolex
recipes.description: Ugandan rolex, a hot chapati rolled around an egg omelette with onion, tomato, cabbage, and pepper.
recipes.story: The roll has to happen while both chapati and egg are hot, so the layers bend together instead of cracking.
recipes.best_served_with: Tea, kachumbari, avocado, hot sauce, or extra cabbage
